subject: Chemistry
question_html: Define Azeotropes. What type of azeotrope is formed by negative deviation from Raoult's law? Give an example.
question_text: Define Azeotropes. What type of azeotrope is formed by negative deviation from Raoult's law? Give an example.
explanation: Azeotropes are binary mixtures having the same composition in the liquid and vapor phase and boil at constant temperatures. Azeotropes showing negative deviation from Raoult's law form maximum boiling azeotropes at a specific composition, e.g. Azeotrope formed from nitric acid and water. Azeotropes showing positive deviation from Raoult's law form minimum boiling azeotropes at a specific composition. e.g. Ethanol-water mixture.
solution: Azeotropes are binary mixtures having the same composition in the liquid and vapor phase and boil at constant temperatures. Azeotropes showing negative deviation from Raoult's law form maximum boiling azeotropes at a specific composition, e.g. Azeotrope formed from nitric acid and water. Azeotropes showing positive deviation from Raoult's law form minimum boiling azeotropes at a specific composition. e.g. Ethanol-water mixture.
solution_html:
Azeotropes are binary mixtures having the same composition in the liquid and vapor phase and boil at constant temperatures.
Azeotropes showing negative deviation from Raoult's law form maximum boiling azeotropes at a specific composition, e.g. Azeotrope formed from nitric acid and water.
Azeotropes showing positive deviation from Raoult's law form minimum boiling azeotropes at a specific composition. e.g. Ethanol-water mixture.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A solution of glucose (molar mass = 180 g mol-1) in water has a boiling point of 100.20oC. Calculate the freezing point of the same solution. Molal constants for water Kf and Kb are 1.86 K kg mol-1and 0.512 K kg {tex} {mol}^{-1} {/tex} respectively.
question_text: A solution of glucose (molar mass = 180 g mol -1 ) in water has a boiling point of 100.20 o C. Calculate the freezing point of the same solution. Molal constants for water K f and K b are 1.86 K kg mol -1 and 0.512 K kg {tex} {mol}^{-1} {/tex} respectively.
explanation: Given: T b of glucose solution = 100.20 o C {tex}\Delta{/tex} Tb = K b m m = {tex}\frac{0.20 }{ 0.512}{/tex} m = 0.390 mol/kg {tex}\Delta T_f=K_f \cdot m{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}{/tex} = 1.86 K kg/mol {tex}\times{/tex} 0.390 mol/ kg {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}{/tex} = 0.725 K Freezing point of solution = 273.15 - 0.725 = 272.425 K
solution: Given: T b of glucose solution = 100.20 o C {tex}\Delta{/tex} Tb = K b m m = {tex}\frac{0.20 }{ 0.512}{/tex} m = 0.390 mol/kg {tex}\Delta T_f=K_f \cdot m{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}{/tex} = 1.86 K kg/mol {tex}\times{/tex} 0.390 mol/ kg {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}{/tex} = 0.725 K Freezing point of solution = 273.15 - 0.725 = 272.425 K
solution_html:
Given: Tb of glucose solution = 100.20oC
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = Kb m
m = {tex}\frac{0.20 }{ 0.512}{/tex}
m = 0.390 mol/kg
{tex}\Delta T_f=K_f \cdot m{/tex}
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}{/tex} = 1.86 K kg/mol {tex}\times{/tex} 0.390 mol/ kg
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}{/tex} = 0.725 K
Freezing point of solution = 273.15 - 0.725
= 272.425 K

question_html:
0.3 g of acetic acid (M = 60 g mol-1) dissolved in 30 g of benzene shows a depression in freezing point equal to 0.45o C. Calculate the percentage association of acid if it forms a dimer in the solution.
(Given: Kf for benzene = 5.12 K kg mol-1)
question_text: 0.3 g of acetic acid (M = 60 g mol -1 ) dissolved in 30 g of benzene shows a depression in freezing point equal to 0.45 o C. Calculate the percentage association of acid if it forms a dimer in the solution. (Given: K f for benzene = 5.12 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{/tex} 0.45 = i {tex}\times{/tex} 5.12 K kg mol -1 {tex}\times \frac{0 \cdot 3 \mathrm{~g}}{60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}} \times \frac{1000}{30 \mathrm{~kg}}{/tex} i = 0.527 {tex}\alpha=\frac{1-\mathrm{i}}{1-\frac{1}{n}}{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\frac{1-0.527}{1-\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} Percentage association = 0.946 or 94.6%
solution: {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{/tex} 0.45 = i {tex}\times{/tex} 5.12 K kg mol -1 {tex}\times \frac{0 \cdot 3 \mathrm{~g}}{60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}} \times \frac{1000}{30 \mathrm{~kg}}{/tex} i = 0.527 {tex}\alpha=\frac{1-\mathrm{i}}{1-\frac{1}{n}}{/tex} {tex}\alpha=\frac{1-0.527}{1-\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} Percentage association = 0.946 or 94.6%
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{/tex}
0.45 = i {tex}\times{/tex} 5.12 K kg mol-1 {tex}\times \frac{0 \cdot 3 \mathrm{~g}}{60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}} \times \frac{1000}{30 \mathrm{~kg}}{/tex}
i = 0.527
{tex}\alpha=\frac{1-\mathrm{i}}{1-\frac{1}{n}}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha=\frac{1-0.527}{1-\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}

Percentage association = 0.946 or 94.6%

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Henry's law constant for {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in water is {tex}1.67 \times 10^{8} {~Pa}{/tex} at 298 K. Calculate the number of moles of {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in 500 ml of soda water when packed under {tex}2.53 \times 10^{5} {~Pa}{/tex} at the same temperature.
question_text: Henry's law constant for {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in water is {tex}1.67 \times 10^{8} {~Pa}{/tex} at 298 K. Calculate the number of moles of {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in 500 ml of soda water when packed under {tex}2.53 \times 10^{5} {~Pa}{/tex} at the same temperature.
solution_html:
{tex} {p}_{{CO}_ 2}={K}_{{H}} {X}_{{CO} _2} {/tex}
{tex} {~K}_{{H}}=1.67 \times 10^8 {~Pa} {/tex}
{tex} {p}_{{CO}_ 2}=2.53 \times 10^5 {~Pa} {/tex}
{tex} {X}_{{CO} _2} ={p}_{{CO} _2} / {K}_{{H}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{2.53 \times 10^5}{1.67 \times 10^8} {/tex}
{tex} =1.51 \times 10^{-3} {/tex}
{tex} \chi_{{CO}_ 2} =\frac{n_{{CO} _2}}{n_{{H} _2O}} {/tex}
{tex} {n}_{{H}_ 2 {O}} =500 / 18=27.78 {moles} {/tex}
{tex} {n}_{{CO}_ 2} =27.78 \times 1.51 \times 10^{-3} {moles} {/tex}
{tex} =42.0 \times 10^{-3} {~mol}=0.042 \ {moles} {/tex}

question_html: A solution containing 15 g urea (molar mass {tex}=60 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}) per litre of solution in water has the same osmotic pressure (isotonic) as a solution of glucose (molar mass {tex}=180 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}) in water. question_text: A solution containing 15 g urea (molar mass {tex}=60 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} ) per litre of solution in water has the same osmotic pressure (isotonic) as a solution of glucose (molar mass {tex}=180 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} ) in water. Calculate the mass of glucose present in one litre of its solution.
explanation: {tex} \Pi={CRT}=\frac{w_B {RT}}{M_B V} {/tex} {tex} \pi_{\text {glucose }}=\pi_ \text { urea } {/tex} {tex} {C}_{{G}}={C}_{{U}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{W_G}{M_G}=\frac{W_U}{M_U} {/tex} {tex} \frac{w G}{180}=\frac{15}{60} {/tex} {tex} {w}_{{G}} =\frac{15 \times 180}{60} {/tex} {tex} =45 {~g} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \Pi={CRT}=\frac{w_B {RT}}{M_B V} {/tex} {tex} \pi_{\text {glucose }}=\pi_ \text { urea } {/tex} {tex} {C}_{{G}}={C}_{{U}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{W_G}{M_G}=\frac{W_U}{M_U} {/tex} {tex} \frac{w G}{180}=\frac{15}{60} {/tex} {tex} {w}_{{G}} =\frac{15 \times 180}{60} {/tex} {tex} =45 {~g} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \Pi={CRT}=\frac{w_B {RT}}{M_B V} {/tex}
{tex} \pi_{\text {glucose }}=\pi_ \text { urea } {/tex}
{tex} {C}_{{G}}={C}_{{U}} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{W_G}{M_G}=\frac{W_U}{M_U} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{w G}{180}=\frac{15}{60} {/tex}
{tex} {w}_{{G}} =\frac{15 \times 180}{60} {/tex}
{tex} =45 {~g} {/tex}

question_text: How does sprinkling of salt help in clearing the snow covered roads in hilly areas? What happens when red blood cells are kept in {tex}0 \cdot 5 \%{/tex} (mass/vol) NaCl solution? Justify your answer. Write an application of reverse osmosis.
explanation: Salt lowers the freezing point of water and prevents formation of ice and hence its easy to clean. Red blood cells swell up As the solution is hypotonic, water will flow into the cell/As the solution is hypotonic, endosmosis occurs. Desalination of sea water

  2. Red blood cells swell up

question_text: Liquids 'X' and 'Y' form an ideal solution. The vapour pressure of pure 'X' and pure 'Y' are 120 mm Hg and 160 mm Hg respectively. Calculate the vapour pressure of the solution containing equal moles of 'X' and 'Y'.
explanation: Given {tex}{n}_{{x}}={n}_{{y}}{/tex} {tex}\chi_{{x}} =\chi_{{y}}=0.5{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{X}}{ }^0 x_{x+} \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{Y}}{ }^0 x_Y / p_{\text {total }}=x_1 p_1{ }^0+x_2 p_2{ }^0{/tex} {tex}=120 \times 0.5+160 \times 0.5{/tex} {tex}=60+80{/tex} {tex}=140 {~mm} {Hg}{/tex}
solution: Given {tex}{n}_{{x}}={n}_{{y}}{/tex} {tex}\chi_{{x}} =\chi_{{y}}=0.5{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{X}}{ }^0 x_{x+} \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{Y}}{ }^0 x_Y / p_{\text {total }}=x_1 p_1{ }^0+x_2 p_2{ }^0{/tex} {tex}=120 \times 0.5+160 \times 0.5{/tex} {tex}=60+80{/tex} {tex}=140 {~mm} {Hg}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given {tex}{n}_{{x}}={n}_{{y}}{/tex}
{tex}\chi_{{x}} =\chi_{{y}}=0.5{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{X}}{ }^0 x_{x+} \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{Y}}{ }^0 x_Y / p_{\text {total }}=x_1 p_1{ }^0+x_2 p_2{ }^0{/tex}
{tex}=120 \times 0.5+160 \times 0.5{/tex}
{tex}=60+80{/tex}
{tex}=140 {~mm} {Hg}{/tex}

question_html: Calculate the elevation of boiling point of a solution when 3 g of {tex}{CaCl}_{2}{/tex} (Molar mass {tex}=111 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}) was dissolved in 260 g of water, assuming that {tex}{CaCl}_{2}{/tex} undergoes complete dissociation. ({tex}{K}_{{b}}{/tex} for water {tex}=0.52 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex})
question_text: Calculate the elevation of boiling point of a solution when 3 g of {tex}{CaCl}_{2}{/tex} (Molar mass {tex}=111 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} ) was dissolved in 260 g of water, assuming that {tex}{CaCl}_{2}{/tex} undergoes complete dissociation. ( {tex}{K}_{{b}}{/tex} for water {tex}=0.52 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} )
explanation: {tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}={i} {~K}_{{b}} {~m}{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_{{b}}={i} \frac{K_{{b}} \times 1000 \times w_2}{M_2 \times w_{{i}}}{/tex} {tex}{i}=3{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{3 \times 0.52 \times 3 \times 1000}{111 \times 260}{/tex} {tex}=0.162 {~K}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}={i} {~K}_{{b}} {~m}{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_{{b}}={i} \frac{K_{{b}} \times 1000 \times w_2}{M_2 \times w_{{i}}}{/tex} {tex}{i}=3{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{3 \times 0.52 \times 3 \times 1000}{111 \times 260}{/tex} {tex}=0.162 {~K}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}={i} {~K}_{{b}} {~m}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta T_{{b}}={i} \frac{K_{{b}} \times 1000 \times w_2}{M_2 \times w_{{i}}}{/tex}
{tex}{i}=3{/tex}
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{3 \times 0.52 \times 3 \times 1000}{111 \times 260}{/tex}
{tex}=0.162 {~K}{/tex}

An aqueous solution of NaOH was made and its molar mass from the measurement of osmotic pressure at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} was found to be {tex}28 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}. Calculate the percentage dissociation of NaOH in this solution.
[Atomic mass: {tex}{Na}=23.0 {u}, {O}=16.0 {u}, {H}=1.0 {u}{/tex}]
question_text: An aqueous solution of NaOH was made and its molar mass from the measurement of osmotic pressure at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex} was found to be {tex}28 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} . Calculate the percentage dissociation of NaOH in this solution. [Atomic mass: {tex}{Na}=23.0 {u}, {O}=16.0 {u}, {H}=1.0 {u}{/tex} ]
explanation: {tex} {i} =\text { Normal molar mass/Abnormal molar mass } {/tex} {tex} {i} =40 / 28 {/tex} {tex} =1.428 {/tex} {tex} \alpha ={i}-1 / {n}-1 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1.428-1}{1} {/tex} {tex} =0.428 \times 100 {/tex} {tex} =42.8 \% {/tex}
solution: {tex} {i} =\text { Normal molar mass/Abnormal molar mass } {/tex} {tex} {i} =40 / 28 {/tex} {tex} =1.428 {/tex} {tex} \alpha ={i}-1 / {n}-1 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1.428-1}{1} {/tex} {tex} =0.428 \times 100 {/tex} {tex} =42.8 \% {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} {i} =\text { Normal molar mass/Abnormal molar mass } {/tex}
{tex} {i} =40 / 28 {/tex}
{tex} =1.428 {/tex}
{tex} \alpha ={i}-1 / {n}-1 {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1.428-1}{1} {/tex}
{tex} =0.428 \times 100 {/tex}
{tex} =42.8 \% {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The spontaneous flow of the solvent through a semipermeable membrane from a pure solvent to a solution or from a dilute solution to a concentrated solution is called osmosis. The phenomenon of osmosis can be demonstrated by taking two eggs of the same size. In an egg, the membrane below the shell and around the egg material is semipermeable. The outer hard shell can be removed by putting the egg in dilute hydrochloric acid. After removing the hard shell, one egg is placed in distilled water and the other in a saturated salt solution. After some time, the egg placed in distilled water swells-up while the egg placed in salt solution shrinks. The external pressure applied to stop the osmosis is termed as osmotic pressure (a colligative property). Reverse osmosis takes place when the applied external pressure becomes larger than the osmotic pressure.

  1. Define reverse osmosis. Name one SPM which can be used in the process of reverse osmosis. (2)
    1. What do you expect to happen when red blood corpuscles (RBC's) are placed in {tex}0.5 \% {NaCl}{/tex} solution? (1)

      OR

    2. Which one of the following will have higher osmotic pressure in1 M KCl or 1 M urea solution. Justify your answer. (1)
  3. Why osmotic pressure is a colligative property? (1)
question_text: The spontaneous flow of the solvent through a semipermeable membrane from a pure solvent to a solution or from a dilute solution to a concentrated solution is called osmosis. The phenomenon of osmosis can be demonstrated by taking two eggs of the same size. In an egg, the membrane below the shell and around the egg material is semipermeable. The outer hard shell can be removed by putting the egg in dilute hydrochloric acid. After removing the hard shell, one egg is placed in distilled water and the other in a saturated salt solution. After some time, the egg placed in distilled water swells-up while the egg placed in salt solution shrinks. The external pressure applied to stop the osmosis is termed as osmotic pressure (a colligative property). Reverse osmosis takes place when the applied external pressure becomes larger than the osmotic pressure. Define reverse osmosis. Name one SPM which can be used in the process of reverse osmosis. (2) What do you expect to happen when red blood corpuscles (RBC's) are placed in {tex}0.5 \% {NaCl}{/tex} solution? (1) OR Which one of the following will have higher osmotic pressure in1 M KCl or 1 M urea solution. Justify your answer. (1) Why osmotic pressure is a colligative property? (1)
explanation: When external pressure is larger than the osmotic pressure, then the movement of solvent is from solution to solvent side through semi permeable membrane. / The direction of osmosis can be reversed if a pressure larger than the osmotic pressure is applied to the solution side. Cellulose acetate / Or any other suitable example. RBC swells up / Cells swell and may even burst due to endo-osmosis. OR 1 M KCl, {tex}{i}=2 / {KCl}{/tex} dissociates into ions, whereas urea does not dissociate. It depends upon the number of solute particles in the solution.
solution: When external pressure is larger than the osmotic pressure, then the movement of solvent is from solution to solvent side through semi permeable membrane. / The direction of osmosis can be reversed if a pressure larger than the osmotic pressure is applied to the solution side. Cellulose acetate / Or any other suitable example. RBC swells up / Cells swell and may even burst due to endo-osmosis. OR 1 M KCl, {tex}{i}=2 / {KCl}{/tex} dissociates into ions, whereas urea does not dissociate. It depends upon the number of solute particles in the solution.
solution_html:
• When external pressure is larger than the osmotic pressure, then the movement of solvent is from solution to solvent side through semi permeable membrane. / The direction of osmosis can be reversed if a pressure larger than the osmotic pressure is applied to the solution side.
    • Cellulose acetate / Or any other suitable example.
    1. RBC swells up / Cells swell and may even burst due to endo-osmosis.

      OR

    2. 1 M KCl,
      {tex}{i}=2 / {KCl}{/tex} dissociates into ions, whereas urea does not dissociate.
  3. It depends upon the number of solute particles in the solution.

question_html:
According to the generally accepted definition of the ideal solution there are equal interaction forces acting between molecules belonging to the same or different species. (This is equivalent to the statement that the activity of the components equals the concentration.) Strictly speaking, this condition is fulfilled only in exceptional cases for mixtures (optical isomers, isotopic mixtures of an element, hydrocarbon mixtures). It is still usual to talk about ideal solutions as limiting cases in reality since very dilute solutions behave ideally with respect to the solvent. This view is further supported by the fact that Raoult's law empirically found for describing the behaviour of the solvent in dilute solutions can be deduced thermodynamically via the assumption of ideal behaviour of the solvent. Answer the following questions:

  1. Give one example of miscible liquid pair which shows negative deviation from Raoult's law. What is the reason for such deviation? (2)
    1. State Raoult's law for a solution containing volatile components. (1)

      OR

    2. Raoult's law is a special case of Henry's law. Comment. (1)
  3. Write two characteristics of an ideal solution. (1)

solution: At a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution/ the partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase (p) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas ( {tex}x{/tex} ) in the solution Because the solubility of oxygen increases with decrease in temperature/ Because of low solubility of {tex}{O}_{2}{/tex} in warm water.

• At a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional
    to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution/ the partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase (p) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas ({tex}x{/tex}) in the solution

explanation: {tex} \Delta {T}_{{f}}={iK}_{{f}} {m} {/tex} {tex} \Delta T_f=\frac{{i} \times K_f \times w_2 \times 1000}{M_2 \times w_1} {/tex} {tex} 0.45=\frac{i \times 5.12 \times 0.3 \times 1000}{60 \times 30} {/tex} {tex} i=0.527 {/tex} {tex} \alpha=\frac{i-1}{1 / {n}-1} {/tex} {tex} \alpha=\frac{0.527-1}{1 / 2-1} ({n}=2) {/tex} {tex} \alpha=0.946 \text { or } 94.6 \% {/tex}
solution: {tex} \Delta {T}_{{f}}={iK}_{{f}} {m} {/tex} {tex} \Delta T_f=\frac{{i} \times K_f \times w_2 \times 1000}{M_2 \times w_1} {/tex} {tex} 0.45=\frac{i \times 5.12 \times 0.3 \times 1000}{60 \times 30} {/tex} {tex} i=0.527 {/tex} {tex} \alpha=\frac{i-1}{1 / {n}-1} {/tex} {tex} \alpha=\frac{0.527-1}{1 / 2-1} ({n}=2) {/tex} {tex} \alpha=0.946 \text { or } 94.6 \% {/tex}

{tex} \Delta {T}_{{f}}={iK}_{{f}} {m} {/tex}
{tex} \Delta T_f=\frac{{i} \times K_f \times w_2 \times 1000}{M_2 \times w_1} {/tex}
{tex} 0.45=\frac{i \times 5.12 \times 0.3 \times 1000}{60 \times 30} {/tex}
{tex} i=0.527 {/tex}
{tex} \alpha=\frac{i-1}{1 / {n}-1} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. At the same temperature, CO2 gas is more soluble in water than O2 gas. Which one of them will have higher value of KH and why? (1)
  2. How does the size of blood cells change when placed in an aqueous solution containing more than 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride? (1)
  3. 1 molal aqueous solution of an electrolyte A2B3 is 60% ionized. Calculate the boiling point of the solution. (3) 
    (Given: Kb for H2O = 0.52 K kg mol-1)
question_text: At the same temperature, CO 2 gas is more soluble in water than O 2 gas. Which one of them will have higher value of K H and why? (1) How does the size of blood cells change when placed in an aqueous solution containing more than 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride? (1) 1 molal aqueous solution of an electrolyte A 2 B 3 is 60% ionized. Calculate the boiling point of the solution. (3) (Given: K b for H 2 O = 0.52 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: As {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}} \propto \frac{1}{\text { Solubility }}{/tex} of Gas {tex}\therefore{/tex} O 2 gas has higher K H ; because higher the K H value, lower the solubility of gas in liquid. Blood cells shrink. {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = iK b m T b - T b 0 = i {tex}\times{/tex} 0.52 K Kg mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 mol Kg -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac{i-1}{\mathrm{n}-1}{/tex} n = 5 0.6 = {tex}\frac{i-1}{5-1}{/tex} i = 3.4 T b - 373 K = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.52 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 T b = 1.768 + 373 K T b = 374.768K (If boiling point of water is 373.15K then T b = 374.918K)
solution: As {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}} \propto \frac{1}{\text { Solubility }}{/tex} of Gas {tex}\therefore{/tex} O 2 gas has higher K H ; because higher the K H value, lower the solubility of gas in liquid. Blood cells shrink. {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = iK b m T b - T b 0 = i {tex}\times{/tex} 0.52 K Kg mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 mol Kg -1 {tex}\alpha=\frac{i-1}{\mathrm{n}-1}{/tex} n = 5 0.6 = {tex}\frac{i-1}{5-1}{/tex} i = 3.4 T b - 373 K = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.52 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 T b = 1.768 + 373 K T b = 374.768K (If boiling point of water is 373.15K then T b = 374.918K)
solution_html:
1. As {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}} \propto \frac{1}{\text { Solubility }}{/tex} of Gas
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} O2 gas has higher KH; because higher the KH value, lower the solubility of gas in liquid.
  2. Blood cells shrink.
  3. {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = iKb m
    Tb - Tb0 = i {tex}\times{/tex} 0.52 K Kg mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 mol Kg-1
    {tex}\alpha=\frac{i-1}{\mathrm{n}-1}{/tex}
    n = 5
    0.6 = {tex}\frac{i-1}{5-1}{/tex}
    i = 3.4
    Tb - 373 K = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.52 {tex}\times{/tex} 1
    Tb = 1.768 + 373 K
    Tb = 374.768K (If boiling point of water is 373.15K then Tb = 374.918K)

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. The vapour pressures of A and B at 25oC are 75 mm Hg and 25 mm Hg, respectively. If A and B are mixed such that the mole fraction of A in the mixture is 0.4, then calculate the mole fraction of B in vapour phase. (2)
  2. Define colligative property. Which colligative property is preferred for the molar mass determination of macromolecules? (2)
  3. Why are equimolar solutions of sodium chloride and glucose not isotonic? (1)
question_text: The vapour pressures of A and B at 25 o C are 75 mm Hg and 25 mm Hg, respectively. If A and B are mixed such that the mole fraction of A in the mixture is 0.4, then calculate the mole fraction of B in vapour phase. (2) Define colligative property. Which colligative property is preferred for the molar mass determination of macromolecules? (2) Why are equimolar solutions of sodium chloride and glucose not isotonic? (1)
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}{ }^0 x_A+\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{B}}{ }^0 x_B{/tex} P T = 75 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 + 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.6 P T = 30 + 15 = 45 mm Hg In Vapour phase p B = {tex}y_{B \times} P_T{/tex} y B = {tex}p_B / P_T=\frac{p_B^o x_B}{P_T}{/tex} y B = {tex}\frac{15}{45}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 0.33 mm Hg The property which depends upon the number of solute particles but not on the nature of solute; Osmotic pressure. Because sodium chloride undergoes dissociation (i=2) in water while glucose does not./ {tex}\pi{/tex} = i C R T; For NaCl, i = 2 and for glucose i = 1.
solution: {tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}{ }^0 x_A+\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{B}}{ }^0 x_B{/tex} P T = 75 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 + 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.6 P T = 30 + 15 = 45 mm Hg In Vapour phase p B = {tex}y_{B \times} P_T{/tex} y B = {tex}p_B / P_T=\frac{p_B^o x_B}{P_T}{/tex} y B = {tex}\frac{15}{45}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 0.33 mm Hg The property which depends upon the number of solute particles but not on the nature of solute; Osmotic pressure. Because sodium chloride undergoes dissociation (i=2) in water while glucose does not./ {tex}\pi{/tex} = i C R T; For NaCl, i = 2 and for glucose i = 1.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}{ }^0 x_A+\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{B}}{ }^0 x_B{/tex}
    PT = 75 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 + 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.6
    PT = 30 + 15 = 45 mm Hg
    In Vapour phase
    pB = {tex}y_{B \times} P_T{/tex}
    yB = {tex}p_B / P_T=\frac{p_B^o x_B}{P_T}{/tex}
    yB = {tex}\frac{15}{45}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 0.33 mm Hg
  2. The property which depends upon the number of solute particles but not on the nature of solute; Osmotic pressure.
  3. Because sodium chloride undergoes dissociation (i=2) in water while glucose does not./ {tex}\pi{/tex} = i C R T; For NaCl, i = 2 and for glucose i = 1.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A 6% solution of glucose (molar mass = 180 g mol-1) is isotonic with 2.5% solution of an unknown organic substance. Calculate the molecular weight of the unknown organic substance.
question_text: A 6% solution of glucose (molar mass = 180 g mol -1 ) is isotonic with 2.5% solution of an unknown organic substance. Calculate the molecular weight of the unknown organic substance.
explanation: {tex}\pi_{\text {Glucose }}=\pi_{\text {Unknown }}{/tex} C G = C U {tex}\frac{W_G}{M_G}=\frac{W_U}{M_U}{/tex} {tex}\frac{6}{180}=\frac{2 \cdot 5}{M_U}{/tex} M U = {tex}\frac{2.5 \times 180}{6}{/tex} g mol -1 = 75 g mol -1
solution: {tex}\pi_{\text {Glucose }}=\pi_{\text {Unknown }}{/tex} C G = C U {tex}\frac{W_G}{M_G}=\frac{W_U}{M_U}{/tex} {tex}\frac{6}{180}=\frac{2 \cdot 5}{M_U}{/tex} M U = {tex}\frac{2.5 \times 180}{6}{/tex} g mol -1 = 75 g mol -1
solution_html:
{tex}\pi_{\text {Glucose }}=\pi_{\text {Unknown }}{/tex}
CG = CU
{tex}\frac{W_G}{M_G}=\frac{W_U}{M_U}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{6}{180}=\frac{2 \cdot 5}{M_U}{/tex}
MU = {tex}\frac{2.5 \times 180}{6}{/tex}g mol-1
= 75 g mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The vapour pressure of a solvent at 283 K is 100 mm Hg. Calculate the vapour pressure of a dilute solution containing 1 mole of a strong electrolyte AB in 50 moles of the solvent at 283 K (assuming complete dissociation of solute AB).
question_text: The vapour pressure of a solvent at 283 K is 100 mm Hg. Calculate the vapour pressure of a dilute solution containing 1 mole of a strong electrolyte AB in 50 moles of the solvent at 283 K (assuming complete dissociation of solute AB).
explanation: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{p}^{\circ}-p_s}{\mathrm{p}^{\circ}}=\mathrm{i} \times \chi{/tex} AB {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} A + + B - i = 2 {tex}\frac{100-P_S}{100}=2 \times \frac{n_{A B}}{n_{\text {solvent }}}{/tex} (For dilute solution) {tex}\frac{100-P_s}{100}=2 \frac{1}{50}{/tex} 100 - P s = 4 P s = 96 mm Hg
solution: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{p}^{\circ}-p_s}{\mathrm{p}^{\circ}}=\mathrm{i} \times \chi{/tex} AB {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} A + + B - i = 2 {tex}\frac{100-P_S}{100}=2 \times \frac{n_{A B}}{n_{\text {solvent }}}{/tex} (For dilute solution) {tex}\frac{100-P_s}{100}=2 \frac{1}{50}{/tex} 100 - P s = 4 P s = 96 mm Hg
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{p}^{\circ}-p_s}{\mathrm{p}^{\circ}}=\mathrm{i} \times \chi{/tex}
AB {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} A+ + B-
i = 2
{tex}\frac{100-P_S}{100}=2 \times \frac{n_{A B}}{n_{\text {solvent }}}{/tex}(For dilute solution)
{tex}\frac{100-P_s}{100}=2 \frac{1}{50}{/tex}
100 - Ps = 4
Ps = 96 mm Hg

question_html: A solution is prepared by dissolving 5 g of a non-volatile solute in 200 g of water. It has a vapour pressure of 31.84 mm Hg at 300 K. Calculate the molar mass of the solute. (Vapour pressure of pure water at 300 K = 32 mm Hg)
question_text: A solution is prepared by dissolving 5 g of a non-volatile solute in 200 g of water. It has a vapour pressure of 31.84 mm Hg at 300 K. Calculate the molar mass of the solute. (Vapour pressure of pure water at 300 K = 32 mm Hg)
explanation: {tex} \frac{p_1^0-p_1}{p_1^0}=\frac{{w}_2 \times M_1}{M_2 \times {w}_1} {/tex} {tex} \frac{(32-31 \cdot 84)}{32}=\frac{5 {~g}}{{M}_2} \times \frac{18}{200 {~g}}{/tex} M 2 = 90 g/ mol (Deduct half mark for no or incorrect unit)
solution: {tex} \frac{p_1^0-p_1}{p_1^0}=\frac{{w}_2 \times M_1}{M_2 \times {w}_1} {/tex} {tex} \frac{(32-31 \cdot 84)}{32}=\frac{5 {~g}}{{M}_2} \times \frac{18}{200 {~g}}{/tex} M 2 = 90 g/ mol (Deduct half mark for no or incorrect unit)
solution_html:
{tex} \frac{p_1^0-p_1}{p_1^0}=\frac{{w}_2 \times M_1}{M_2 \times {w}_1} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{(32-31 \cdot 84)}{32}=\frac{5 {~g}}{{M}_2} \times \frac{18}{200 {~g}}{/tex}
M2 = 90 g/ mol
(Deduct half mark for no or incorrect unit)

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Calculate the molar mass of a compound when 6.3 g of it is dissolved in 27 g of chloroform to form a solution that has a boiling point of {tex}68.04^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. The boiling point of pure chloroform is {tex}61.04^{\circ} {C}{/tex} and {tex}{K}_{{b}}{/tex} for chloroform is {tex}3.63^{\circ} {C} \ {kg} \ {mol}^{-1}{/tex}.
question_text: Calculate the molar mass of a compound when 6.3 g of it is dissolved in 27 g of chloroform to form a solution that has a boiling point of {tex}68.04^{\circ} {C}{/tex} . The boiling point of pure chloroform is {tex}61.04^{\circ} {C}{/tex} and {tex}{K}_{{b}}{/tex} for chloroform is {tex}3.63^{\circ} {C} \ {kg} \ {mol}^{-1}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\Delta T_{b}=K_{b} m{/tex} {tex}M_{B}=\frac{K_{b} \times w_{B} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{A}}{/tex} {tex}M_{B}=\frac{3.63 \times 6.3 \times 1000}{7 \times 27}{/tex} {tex}=121 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\Delta T_{b}=K_{b} m{/tex} {tex}M_{B}=\frac{K_{b} \times w_{B} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{A}}{/tex} {tex}M_{B}=\frac{3.63 \times 6.3 \times 1000}{7 \times 27}{/tex} {tex}=121 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta T_{b}=K_{b} m{/tex}
{tex}M_{B}=\frac{K_{b} \times w_{B} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{A}}{/tex}
{tex}M_{B}=\frac{3.63 \times 6.3 \times 1000}{7 \times 27}{/tex}
{tex}=121 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Define reverse osmosis.
  2. Why are aquatic species more comfortable in cold water in comparison to warm water?
  3. A solution containing 2 g of glucose ({tex}{M}=180 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}) in 100 g of water is prepared at 303 K. If the vapour pressure of pure water at 303 K is 32.8 mm Hg, what would be the vapour pressure of the solution?
question_text: Define reverse osmosis. Why are aquatic species more comfortable in cold water in comparison to warm water? A solution containing 2 g of glucose ( {tex}{M}=180 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} ) in 100 g of water is prepared at 303 K. If the vapour pressure of pure water at 303 K is 32.8 mm Hg, what would be the vapour pressure of the solution?
explanation: If a pressure larger than the osmotic pressure is applied to the solution side, resulting in the movement of solvent particles from solution to solvent. Solubility of gases in water decreases with rise in temperature. More oxygen will be available in the cold water. {tex}\frac{p_{1}^{0}-p_{1}}{p_{1}^{0}}=\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_{1}^{0}-p_{1}}{p_{1}^{0}}=\frac{{w}_{2} \times M_{1}}{M_{2} \times {w}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{32.8-p_{1}}{32.8}=\frac{2 \times 18}{180 \times 100}{/tex} {tex}32.8-p_{1}=0.0656{/tex} {tex}p_{1}=32.734 {~mm} {Hg}{/tex}
solution: If a pressure larger than the osmotic pressure is applied to the solution side, resulting in the movement of solvent particles from solution to solvent. Solubility of gases in water decreases with rise in temperature. More oxygen will be available in the cold water. {tex}\frac{p_{1}^{0}-p_{1}}{p_{1}^{0}}=\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_{1}^{0}-p_{1}}{p_{1}^{0}}=\frac{{w}_{2} \times M_{1}}{M_{2} \times {w}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{32.8-p_{1}}{32.8}=\frac{2 \times 18}{180 \times 100}{/tex} {tex}32.8-p_{1}=0.0656{/tex} {tex}p_{1}=32.734 {~mm} {Hg}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. If a pressure larger than the osmotic pressure is applied to the solution side, resulting in the movement of solvent particles from solution to solvent.
  2. Solubility of gases in water decreases with rise in temperature. More oxygen will be available in the cold water.
  3. {tex}\frac{p_{1}^{0}-p_{1}}{p_{1}^{0}}=\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{p_{1}^{0}-p_{1}}{p_{1}^{0}}=\frac{{w}_{2} \times M_{1}}{M_{2} \times {w}_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{32.8-p_{1}}{32.8}=\frac{2 \times 18}{180 \times 100}{/tex}
    {tex}32.8-p_{1}=0.0656{/tex}
    {tex}p_{1}=32.734 {~mm} {Hg}{/tex}

question_html: 18 g of a non-volatile solute is dissolved in 200 g of {tex}{H}_{2} {O}{/tex} freezes at 272.07 K. Calculate the molecular mass of solute {tex}\left({K}_{{f}}\right.{/tex} for water {tex}\left.=1.86 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1}\right){/tex}
question_text: 18 g of a non-volatile solute is dissolved in 200 g of {tex}{H}_{2} {O}{/tex} freezes at 272.07 K. Calculate the molecular mass of solute {tex}\left({K}_{{f}}\right.{/tex} for water {tex}\left.=1.86 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1}\right){/tex}
explanation: {tex}\Delta T_{f}=K_{f} m{/tex} {tex}M_{B}=\frac{K_{f} \times w_{B} \times 1000}{w_{A} \times \Delta T_{f}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_{f}=T_{f}^{o}-T_{f}{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_{f}=273 \cdot 15-272 \cdot 07=1 \cdot 08 {~K}{/tex} {tex}M_{B}=\frac{1 \cdot 86 \times 18 \times 1000}{200 \times 1 \cdot 08}{/tex} {tex}=155 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\Delta T_{f}=K_{f} m{/tex} {tex}M_{B}=\frac{K_{f} \times w_{B} \times 1000}{w_{A} \times \Delta T_{f}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_{f}=T_{f}^{o}-T_{f}{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_{f}=273 \cdot 15-272 \cdot 07=1 \cdot 08 {~K}{/tex} {tex}M_{B}=\frac{1 \cdot 86 \times 18 \times 1000}{200 \times 1 \cdot 08}{/tex} {tex}=155 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta T_{f}=K_{f} m{/tex}
{tex}M_{B}=\frac{K_{f} \times w_{B} \times 1000}{w_{A} \times \Delta T_{f}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta T_{f}=T_{f}^{o}-T_{f}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta T_{f}=273 \cdot 15-272 \cdot 07=1 \cdot 08 {~K}{/tex}
{tex}M_{B}=\frac{1 \cdot 86 \times 18 \times 1000}{200 \times 1 \cdot 08}{/tex}
{tex}=155 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}

question_html: Calculate elevation of the boiling point of solution when 2 g of {tex}{MgSO}_{4}{/tex} (molar mass {tex}=120 {~g} / {mol}{/tex} ) was dissolved in 100 g of water, assuming {tex}{MgSO}_{4}{/tex} undergoes complete dissociation. {tex}\left[{K}_{{b}}\right.{/tex} for water {tex}\left.=0.52 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
question_text: Calculate elevation of the boiling point of solution when 2 g of {tex}{MgSO}_{4}{/tex} (molar mass {tex}=120 {~g} / {mol}{/tex} ) was dissolved in 100 g of water, assuming {tex}{MgSO}_{4}{/tex} undergoes complete dissociation. {tex}\left[{K}_{{b}}\right.{/tex} for water {tex}\left.=0.52 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}={i} {K}_{{b}} {m}{/tex}
{tex}{i}=2{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}=2 \times 0.52 \times \frac{2}{120} \times \frac{1000}{100}{/tex}
{tex}=0.17 {~K}{/tex}
{tex}{T}_{{b}}=373.15+0.17 / 373+0.17{/tex}
{tex}=373.32 {~K} / 373.17 {~K}{/tex}

question_text: Predict whether Van't Hoff factor will be less or greater than one, when Ethanoic acid is dissolved in benzene. Define ideal solution. Calculate the mass of {tex}{CaCl}_{2}{/tex} (molar mass {tex}=111 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} ) to be dissolved in 500 g of water to lower its freezing point by 2 K, assuming that {tex}{CaCl}_{2}{/tex} undergoes complete dissociation. {tex}\left({K}_{{f}}\right.{/tex} for water {tex}\left.=1.86 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1}\right){/tex}

  3. {tex} {i}=3{/tex}

    {tex} \Delta T_f=\frac{i \times K_f \times w_B \times 1000}{M_B \times w_A} {/tex}

    {tex} =19.89 {~g} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
The cause for deviation from Raoult's law in the colligative properties of non-ideal solutions lie in the nature of interactions at the molecular level. These properties show deviations from Raoult's law due to difference in interactions between solute - solvent, solute - solute and solvent - solvent. Some liquids on mixing, form azeotropes which are binary mixtures having the same composition in liquid and vapour phase and boil at a constant temperature. In such cases, it is not possible to separate the components by fractional distillation. There are two types of azeotropes called minimum boiling azeotrope and maximum boiling azeotrope.
Based on the above passage, answer the following questions:

  1. Pure ethanol cannot be prepared by fractional distillation of ethanol - water mixture. Comment. (1)
  2. Why does a mixture of chloroform and acetone show deviation from ideal behaviour? (1)
    1. The vapour pressure of pure benzene at a certain temperature is 1.25 atm. When 1.2 g of non-volatile, non-electrolyte solute is added to 60 g of benzene ({tex}M=78 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}), the vapour pressure of the solution becomes 1.237 atm. Calculate the molar mass of the non-volatile solute. (2)

      OR

    2. The boiling point of benzene is 353.23 K. When 1.80 g of a non-volatile solute was dissolved in 90 g of benzene, the boiling point is raised to 354.11 K. Calculate the molar mass of the solute. {tex}{K}_{{b}}{/tex} for benzene is {tex}2.53 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}. (2)
question_text: The cause for deviation from Raoult's law in the colligative properties of non-ideal solutions lie in the nature of interactions at the molecular level. These properties show deviations from Raoult's law due to difference in interactions between solute - solvent, solute - solute and solvent - solvent. Some liquids on mixing, form azeotropes which are binary mixtures having the same composition in liquid and vapour phase and boil at a constant temperature. In such cases, it is not possible to separate the components by fractional distillation. There are two types of azeotropes called minimum boiling azeotrope and maximum boiling azeotrope. Based on the above passage, answer the following questions: Pure ethanol cannot be prepared by fractional distillation of ethanol - water mixture. Comment. (1) Why does a mixture of chloroform and acetone show deviation from ideal behaviour? (1) The vapour pressure of pure benzene at a certain temperature is 1.25 atm. When 1.2 g of non-volatile, non-electrolyte solute is added to 60 g of benzene ( {tex}M=78 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} ), the vapour pressure of the solution becomes 1.237 atm. Calculate the molar mass of the non-volatile solute. (2) OR The boiling point of benzene is 353.23 K. When 1.80 g of a non-volatile solute was dissolved in 90 g of benzene, the boiling point is raised to 354.11 K. Calculate the molar mass of the solute. {tex}{K}_{{b}}{/tex} for benzene is {tex}2.53 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} . (2)
explanation: Ethanol-water forms azeotropic mixture. Due to the formation of hydrogen bond between chloroform and acetone. {tex}\frac{P^{0}-P}{P^{0}}=x_{2}=\frac{w_{2}}{M_{2}} {\times} \frac{M_{1}}{w_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.25-1.237}{1.25}=\frac{1.2}{M_{2}} {\times} \frac{78}{60}{/tex} {tex}{M}_{2}=\frac{1.2}{M_{2}} \times \frac{78}{60} \times \frac{1.25}{0.013}{/tex} {tex}{M}_{2}=150 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} OR The elevation ( {tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}{/tex} ) in the boiling point {tex}=354.11 {~K}-353.23 {~K}=0.88 {~K}{/tex} {tex}M_2=\frac{2.53 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1} \times 1.8 {~g} \times 1000 {~g} {~kg}^{-1}}{0.88 {~K} \times 90 {~g}}{/tex} {tex}{M}_{2}=57.5 {~g} {~mol}^{-1} \approx 58 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: Ethanol-water forms azeotropic mixture. Due to the formation of hydrogen bond between chloroform and acetone. {tex}\frac{P^{0}-P}{P^{0}}=x_{2}=\frac{w_{2}}{M_{2}} {\times} \frac{M_{1}}{w_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.25-1.237}{1.25}=\frac{1.2}{M_{2}} {\times} \frac{78}{60}{/tex} {tex}{M}_{2}=\frac{1.2}{M_{2}} \times \frac{78}{60} \times \frac{1.25}{0.013}{/tex} {tex}{M}_{2}=150 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} OR The elevation ( {tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}{/tex} ) in the boiling point {tex}=354.11 {~K}-353.23 {~K}=0.88 {~K}{/tex} {tex}M_2=\frac{2.53 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1} \times 1.8 {~g} \times 1000 {~g} {~kg}^{-1}}{0.88 {~K} \times 90 {~g}}{/tex} {tex}{M}_{2}=57.5 {~g} {~mol}^{-1} \approx 58 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Ethanol-water forms azeotropic mixture.
  2. Due to the formation of hydrogen bond between chloroform and acetone.
    1. {tex}\frac{P^{0}-P}{P^{0}}=x_{2}=\frac{w_{2}}{M_{2}} {\times} \frac{M_{1}}{w_{1}}{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{1.25-1.237}{1.25}=\frac{1.2}{M_{2}} {\times} \frac{78}{60}{/tex}
      {tex}{M}_{2}=\frac{1.2}{M_{2}} \times \frac{78}{60} \times \frac{1.25}{0.013}{/tex}
      {tex}{M}_{2}=150 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}

      OR

    2. The elevation ({tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}{/tex}) in the boiling point {tex}=354.11 {~K}-353.23 {~K}=0.88 {~K}{/tex}
      {tex}M_2=\frac{2.53 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1} \times 1.8 {~g} \times 1000 {~g} {~kg}^{-1}}{0.88 {~K} \times 90 {~g}}{/tex}
      {tex}{M}_{2}=57.5 {~g} {~mol}^{-1} \approx 58 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Boiling point of water at 750 mm Hg pressure is {tex}99.68^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. How much sucrose (Molar mass {tex}=342 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}) is to be added to 500 g of water such that it boils at {tex}100^{\circ} {C} ?{/tex} (Kb for water {tex}=0.52 {~K} {~kg} \left.{mol}^{-1}\right){/tex}.
  2. State Henry's law and write its any one application.
question_text: Boiling point of water at 750 mm Hg pressure is {tex}99.68^{\circ} {C}{/tex} . How much sucrose (Molar mass {tex}=342 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} ) is to be added to 500 g of water such that it boils at {tex}100^{\circ} {C} ?{/tex} (K b for water {tex}=0.52 {~K} {~kg} \left.{mol}^{-1}\right){/tex} . State Henry's law and write its any one application.
explanation: {tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}=100-99.68^{\circ} {C}=0.32^{\circ} {C}{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_{b}=K_{b} \times W_{B} / M_{B} \times W_{A}({~kg}){/tex} {tex}0.32=0.52 \times W_{B} \times 1000 / 342 \times 500{/tex} {tex}W_{B}=0.32 \times 342 / 0.52 \times 2=105.23 {~g}{/tex} At a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directlyproportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution. / The partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase ( {tex}\boldsymbol{p}{/tex} ) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas ( {tex}\boldsymbol{x}{/tex} ) in the solution. Application: To increase the solubility of {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in soft drinks and soda water, the bottle is sealed under high pressure.
solution: {tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}=100-99.68^{\circ} {C}=0.32^{\circ} {C}{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_{b}=K_{b} \times W_{B} / M_{B} \times W_{A}({~kg}){/tex} {tex}0.32=0.52 \times W_{B} \times 1000 / 342 \times 500{/tex} {tex}W_{B}=0.32 \times 342 / 0.52 \times 2=105.23 {~g}{/tex} At a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directlyproportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution. / The partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase ( {tex}\boldsymbol{p}{/tex} ) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas ( {tex}\boldsymbol{x}{/tex} ) in the solution. Application: To increase the solubility of {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in soft drinks and soda water, the bottle is sealed under high pressure.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\Delta {T}_{{b}}=100-99.68^{\circ} {C}=0.32^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta T_{b}=K_{b} \times W_{B} / M_{B} \times W_{A}({~kg}){/tex}
    {tex}0.32=0.52 \times W_{B} \times 1000 / 342 \times 500{/tex}
    {tex}W_{B}=0.32 \times 342 / 0.52 \times 2=105.23 {~g}{/tex}
  2. At a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directlyproportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution. / The partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase ({tex}\boldsymbol{p}{/tex}) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas ({tex}\boldsymbol{x}{/tex}) in the solution.
    Application: To increase the solubility of {tex}{CO}_{2}{/tex} in soft drinks and soda water, the bottle is sealed under high pressure.

question_html:
1. Ishan's automobile radiator is filled with 1.0 kg of water. How many grams of ethylene glycol (Molar mass {tex}=62 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}) must Ishan add to get the freezing point of the solution lowered to {tex}2.8{ }^{\circ} {C} . {K}_{{f}}{/tex} for water is {tex}1.86 {~K} {~kg} \cdot {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}.
  2. What type of deviation from Raoult's law is shown by ethanol and acetone mixture? Give reason.
question_text: Ishan's automobile radiator is filled with 1.0 kg of water. How many grams of ethylene glycol (Molar mass {tex}=62 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} ) must Ishan add to get the freezing point of the solution lowered to {tex}2.8{ }^{\circ} {C} . {K}_{{f}}{/tex} for water is {tex}1.86 {~K} {~kg} \cdot {~mol}^{-1}{/tex} . What type of deviation from Raoult's law is shown by ethanol and acetone mixture? Give reason.
explanation: {tex}\Delta {T}_{{f}}=2.8^{\circ} {C}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T}_{{f}}={K}_{{f}} \times {w}_{{B}} / {M}_{{B}} \times {W}_{{A}}({kg}){/tex} {tex}2.8=1.86 \times {W}_{{B}} / 62 \times 1{/tex} {tex}{W}_{{B}}=(2.8 \times 62) / 1.86{/tex} {tex}{w}_{{B}}=93.33 {~g}{/tex} Positive deviation Interaction between ethanol-ethanol is stronger than ethanol-acteone.
solution: {tex}\Delta {T}_{{f}}=2.8^{\circ} {C}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T}_{{f}}={K}_{{f}} \times {w}_{{B}} / {M}_{{B}} \times {W}_{{A}}({kg}){/tex} {tex}2.8=1.86 \times {W}_{{B}} / 62 \times 1{/tex} {tex}{W}_{{B}}=(2.8 \times 62) / 1.86{/tex} {tex}{w}_{{B}}=93.33 {~g}{/tex} Positive deviation Interaction between ethanol-ethanol is stronger than ethanol-acteone.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\Delta {T}_{{f}}=2.8^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {T}_{{f}}={K}_{{f}} \times {w}_{{B}} / {M}_{{B}} \times {W}_{{A}}({kg}){/tex}
    {tex}2.8=1.86 \times {W}_{{B}} / 62 \times 1{/tex}
    {tex}{W}_{{B}}=(2.8 \times 62) / 1.86{/tex}
    {tex}{w}_{{B}}=93.33 {~g}{/tex}
  2. Positive deviation
    Interaction between ethanol-ethanol is stronger than ethanol-acteone.

question_text: A solution containing 60 g of a non-volatile solute in 250 g of water freezes at 270.67 K. Calculate the molar mass of the solute. ( {tex}{K}_{{f}}{/tex} of water {tex}=1.86 {~K} {kg} {mol}^{-1}{/tex} ).
explanation: {tex}\Delta {T}_{{f}}={K}_{{f}} {~m}{/tex} {tex}{M}_{{B}}=\frac{{K}_{f} \times {W}_{{B}} \times 1000}{{~W}_{{A}} \times \Delta {T}_{f}}{/tex} {tex}=273 \cdot 15-270 \cdot 67=2 \cdot 48 {~K}{/tex} {tex}{M}_{{B}}=\frac{1 \cdot 86 \times 60 \times 1000}{250 \times 2 \cdot 48}{/tex} {tex}=180 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\Delta {T}_{{f}}={K}_{{f}} {~m}{/tex} {tex}{M}_{{B}}=\frac{{K}_{f} \times {W}_{{B}} \times 1000}{{~W}_{{A}} \times \Delta {T}_{f}}{/tex} {tex}=273 \cdot 15-270 \cdot 67=2 \cdot 48 {~K}{/tex} {tex}{M}_{{B}}=\frac{1 \cdot 86 \times 60 \times 1000}{250 \times 2 \cdot 48}{/tex} {tex}=180 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta {T}_{{f}}={K}_{{f}} {~m}{/tex}
{tex}{M}_{{B}}=\frac{{K}_{f} \times {W}_{{B}} \times 1000}{{~W}_{{A}} \times \Delta {T}_{f}}{/tex}
{tex}=273 \cdot 15-270 \cdot 67=2 \cdot 48 {~K}{/tex}
{tex}{M}_{{B}}=\frac{1 \cdot 86 \times 60 \times 1000}{250 \times 2 \cdot 48}{/tex}
{tex}=180 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}

question_text: A solution is prepared by dissolving 10 g of non-volatile solute in 200 g of water. It has a vapor pressure of 31.84 mm Hg at 308 K. Calculate the molar mass of the solute. (Vapour pressure of pure water at 308 K = 32 mm Hg)
explanation: Given, W 2 = 10 g, W 1 = 200 g p = 31.84 mm of Hg p∘ = 32 mm of Hg; M 1 = 18 g mol −1 {tex}\frac{p^{0}-p}{p^{0}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{W_{2} \times M_{1}}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{32-31.84}{32}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10 \times 18}{M_{2} \times 200}{/tex} M 2 = 180 g mol -1
solution: Given, W 2 = 10 g, W 1 = 200 g p = 31.84 mm of Hg p∘ = 32 mm of Hg; M 1 = 18 g mol −1 {tex}\frac{p^{0}-p}{p^{0}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{W_{2} \times M_{1}}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{32-31.84}{32}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10 \times 18}{M_{2} \times 200}{/tex} M 2 = 180 g mol -1
solution_html:
Given,
W2 = 10 g, W1 = 200 g
p = 31.84 mm of Hg
p∘ = 32 mm of Hg; M1 = 18 g mol−1
{tex}\frac{p^{0}-p}{p^{0}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{W_{2} \times M_{1}}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{32-31.84}{32}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10 \times 18}{M_{2} \times 200}{/tex}
M2 = 180 g mol-1

question_text: Differentiate between Ideal solution and Non-ideal solution. 30 g of urea is dissolved in 846 g of water. Calculate the vapour pressure of water for this solution if vapour pressure of pure water at 298 K is 23.8 mm Hg.
explanation: Ideal Solution Non-ideal solution The solution obeys Raoult’s law at all concentrations. The solution does not obey Raoult’s law. {tex}\Delta V{/tex} mixing = 0 and {tex}\Delta H_{mixing}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Delta \mathrm{V}_{\text {mixing }} \neq 0{/tex} and {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {mixing }} \neq 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^0-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}^o}^{\mathrm{o}}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}}}{\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}}+\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}}}{/tex} {tex}1-\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{23 \cdot 8}=\frac{\frac{30}{60}}{\frac{846}{18}}{/tex} or {tex}1-\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{23 \cdot 8}=\frac{\frac{30}{60}}{\frac{846}{18}+\frac{30}{60}}{/tex} P A = {tex}\frac{46.5}{47} \times{/tex} 23.8 = 23.5 mm Hg or vapour pressure of the solution P A = {tex}\frac{47}{47.5} \times {/tex} 23.8 = 23.5 mm Hg
solution: Ideal Solution Non-ideal solution The solution obeys Raoult’s law at all concentrations. The solution does not obey Raoult’s law. {tex}\Delta V{/tex} mixing = 0 and {tex}\Delta H_{mixing}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Delta \mathrm{V}_{\text {mixing }} \neq 0{/tex} and {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {mixing }} \neq 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^0-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}^o}^{\mathrm{o}}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}}}{\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}}+\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}}}{/tex} {tex}1-\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{23 \cdot 8}=\frac{\frac{30}{60}}{\frac{846}{18}}{/tex} or {tex}1-\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{23 \cdot 8}=\frac{\frac{30}{60}}{\frac{846}{18}+\frac{30}{60}}{/tex} P A = {tex}\frac{46.5}{47} \times{/tex} 23.8 = 23.5 mm Hg or vapour pressure of the solution P A = {tex}\frac{47}{47.5} \times {/tex} 23.8 = 23.5 mm Hg
solution_html:
1. Ideal Solution Non-ideal solution
    The solution obeys Raoult’s law at all concentrations. The solution does not obey Raoult’s law.
    {tex}\Delta V{/tex} mixing = 0 and {tex}\Delta H_{mixing}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Delta \mathrm{V}_{\text {mixing }} \neq 0{/tex} and {tex}\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {mixing }} \neq 0{/tex}
  2.  {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^0-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}^o}^{\mathrm{o}}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}}}{\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}}+\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}}}{/tex}
    {tex}1-\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{23 \cdot 8}=\frac{\frac{30}{60}}{\frac{846}{18}}{/tex} or {tex}1-\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}}{23 \cdot 8}=\frac{\frac{30}{60}}{\frac{846}{18}+\frac{30}{60}}{/tex}
    PA = {tex}\frac{46.5}{47} \times{/tex} 23.8 = 23.5 mm Hg or vapour pressure of the solution PA = {tex}\frac{47}{47.5} \times {/tex} 23.8 = 23.5 mm Hg

question_text: Give reason: Measurement of osmotic pressure method is preferred for the determination of molar masses of macromolecules such as proteins and polymers. Aquatic animals are more comfortable in cold water than in warm water. Elevation of boiling point of 1 M KCl solution is nearly double than that of 1 M sugar solution.
explanation: Osmotic pressure is measured at room temperature/molarity of the solution is used instead of molality/as compared to other colligative properties its magnitude is large even for a very dilute solution. Aquatic animal are more comfortable in cold water than in warm water due to solubility of oxygen is higher at a lower temperature. KCl being strong electrolyte dissociates into two moles of ions but sugar will not dissociate/for KCl, i = 2 and for sugar, i = 1.
solution: Osmotic pressure is measured at room temperature/molarity of the solution is used instead of molality/as compared to other colligative properties its magnitude is large even for a very dilute solution. Aquatic animal are more comfortable in cold water than in warm water due to solubility of oxygen is higher at a lower temperature. KCl being strong electrolyte dissociates into two moles of ions but sugar will not dissociate/for KCl, i = 2 and for sugar, i = 1.
solution_html:
1. Osmotic pressure is measured at room temperature/molarity of the solution is used instead of molality/as compared to other colligative properties its magnitude is large even for a very dilute solution.
  2. Aquatic animal are more comfortable in cold water than in warm water due to solubility of oxygen is higher at a lower temperature.
  3. KCl being strong electrolyte dissociates into two moles of ions but sugar will not dissociate/for KCl, i = 2 and for sugar, i = 1.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Why is boiling point of 1M NaCl solution more than that of 1M glucose solution?
  2. A non-volatile solute X (molar mass = 50 g mol-1) when dissolved in 78g of benzene reduced its vapour pressure to 90%. Calculate the mass of X dissolved in the solution.
  3. Calculate the boiling point elevation for a solution prepared by adding 10g of MgCl2 to 200g of water assuming MgCl2 is completely dissociated.
    (Kb for Water = 0.512 K kg mol-1, Molar mass MgCl2 = 95g mol-1)
question_text: Why is boiling point of 1M NaCl solution more than that of 1M glucose solution? A non-volatile solute X (molar mass = 50 g mol -1 ) when dissolved in 78g of benzene reduced its vapour pressure to 90%. Calculate the mass of X dissolved in the solution. Calculate the boiling point elevation for a solution prepared by adding 10g of MgCl 2 to 200g of water assuming MgCl 2 is completely dissociated. (K b for Water = 0.512 K kg mol -1 , Molar mass MgCl 2 = 95g mol -1 )
explanation: Boiling point of 1M NaCl solution is more than that of 1M glucose solution because the van't Hoff factor of 1 M NaCl (2) solution is bigger than that of 1 M glucose (1) solution, boiling point is directly proportional to molality and van't Hoff factor. As a result, the boiling point of 1 M NaCl is higher than that of 1 M glucose solution. Relative lowering of vapour pressure: {tex}\frac{P^0-P_s}{P_s}=\frac{n_B}{n_A}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}^0-0.9 \mathrm{P}^0}{0.9 \mathrm{P}^0}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{50} \times \frac{78}{78}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} W B = {tex}\frac{0.1}{0.9} \times{/tex} 50 {tex}=\frac{50}{9}{/tex} Mass of x dissolve = 5.5 g We know that {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = iK b m MgCl 2 {tex}\to{/tex} Mg 2+ + 2 Cl - i = 3 Here, m = {tex}\frac{W_B \times 1000}{M_B \times W_A}{/tex} = 0.526 {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = iK b m = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.526 = 0.808 K
solution: Boiling point of 1M NaCl solution is more than that of 1M glucose solution because the van't Hoff factor of 1 M NaCl (2) solution is bigger than that of 1 M glucose (1) solution, boiling point is directly proportional to molality and van't Hoff factor. As a result, the boiling point of 1 M NaCl is higher than that of 1 M glucose solution. Relative lowering of vapour pressure: {tex}\frac{P^0-P_s}{P_s}=\frac{n_B}{n_A}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}^0-0.9 \mathrm{P}^0}{0.9 \mathrm{P}^0}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{50} \times \frac{78}{78}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} W B = {tex}\frac{0.1}{0.9} \times{/tex} 50 {tex}=\frac{50}{9}{/tex} Mass of x dissolve = 5.5 g We know that {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = iK b m MgCl 2 {tex}\to{/tex} Mg 2+ + 2 Cl - i = 3 Here, m = {tex}\frac{W_B \times 1000}{M_B \times W_A}{/tex} = 0.526 {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = iK b m = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.526 = 0.808 K
solution_html:
1. Boiling point of 1M NaCl solution is more than that of 1M glucose solution because the van't Hoff factor of 1 M NaCl (2) solution is bigger than that of 1 M glucose (1) solution, boiling point is directly proportional to molality and van't Hoff factor. As a result, the boiling point of 1 M NaCl is higher than that of 1 M glucose solution.
  2. Relative lowering of vapour pressure:
    {tex}\frac{P^0-P_s}{P_s}=\frac{n_B}{n_A}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}^0-0.9 \mathrm{P}^0}{0.9 \mathrm{P}^0}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{50} \times \frac{78}{78}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} WB = {tex}\frac{0.1}{0.9} \times{/tex} 50
    {tex}=\frac{50}{9}{/tex}
    Mass of x dissolve = 5.5 g
  3. We know that
    {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = iKbm
    MgCl2 {tex}\to{/tex} Mg2+ + 2 Cl-
    i = 3
    Here, m = {tex}\frac{W_B \times 1000}{M_B \times W_A}{/tex} = 0.526
    {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = iKbm
    = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.526
    = 0.808 K

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What is Henry's law? Give one application of it.
question_text: What is Henry's law? Give one application of it.
explanation: Henry’s law states that the partial vapour pressure of a gas is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the gas in the solution / p = K H x where p = partial pressure of gas, x = mole fraction in solution, and k H is Henry’s constant. To increase the solubility of CO 2 in soft drinks and soda water, the bottle is sealed under high pressure.
solution: Henry’s law states that the partial vapour pressure of a gas is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the gas in the solution / p = K H x where p = partial pressure of gas, x = mole fraction in solution, and k H is Henry’s constant. To increase the solubility of CO 2 in soft drinks and soda water, the bottle is sealed under high pressure.
solution_html:
Henry’s law states that the partial vapour pressure of a gas is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the gas in the solution / p = KHx where p = partial pressure of gas, x = mole fraction in solution, and kH is Henry’s constant.
To increase the solubility of CO2 in soft drinks and soda water, the bottle is sealed under high pressure.

question_text: The vapour pressure of pure liquid X and pure liquid Y at 25 o C are 120 mm Hg and 160 mm Hg respectively. If equal moles of X and Y are mixed to form an ideal solution, calculate the vapour pressure of the solution.
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{p}_{\text {Total }}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{X}}^{\circ} \chi_{\mathrm{X}}+\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{Y}}^{\circ} \chi_{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} {tex}\chi_{\mathrm{X}}=\chi_{\mathrm{B}}=0.5{/tex} P Total = (120 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5) + (160 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5) = 140 mm Hg
solution: {tex}\mathrm{p}_{\text {Total }}=\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{X}}^{\circ} \chi_{\mathrm{X}}+\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{Y}}^{\circ} \chi_{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} {tex}\chi_{\mathrm{X}}=\chi_{\mathrm{B}}=0.5{/tex} P Total = (120 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5) + (160 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5) = 140 mm Hg

{tex}\chi_{\mathrm{X}}=\chi_{\mathrm{B}}=0.5{/tex}
PTotal = (120 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5) + (160 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5)
= 140 mm Hg

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Why is the value of Van’t Hoff factor for ethanoic acid in benzene close to 0.5?
  2. Determine the osmotic pressure of a solution prepared by dissolving 2.32 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2g of K2SO4 in 2L of solution at 25oC, assuming that K2SO4 is completely dissociated.
    (R = 0.082 L atm K-1 mol-1, Molar mass K2SO4 = 174g mol-1)
  3. When 25.6g of Sulphur was dissolved in 1000g of benzene, the freezing point lowered by 0.512 K.  Calculate the formula of Sulphur (Sx).
    (Kf for benzene = 5.12 K kg mol-1, Atomic mass of Sulphur = 32g mol-1)
question_text: Why is the value of Van’t Hoff factor for ethanoic acid in benzene close to 0.5? Determine the osmotic pressure of a solution prepared by dissolving 2.32 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g of K 2 SO 4 in 2L of solution at 25 o C, assuming that K 2 SO 4 is completely dissociated. (R = 0.082 L atm K -1 mol -1 , Molar mass K 2 SO 4 = 174g mol -1 ) When 25.6g of Sulphur was dissolved in 1000g of benzene, the freezing point lowered by 0.512 K. Calculate the formula of Sulphur (S x ). (K f for benzene = 5.12 K kg mol -1 , Atomic mass of Sulphur = 32g mol -1 )
explanation: In benzene, two molecules of ethanoic acid associate to form a dimer. 2 CH 3 COOH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} (CH 3 COOH 2 ) Thus, the van't Hoff factor, {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{\text { Number of solutes after dissociation or associations }}{\text { Number of solutes before dissociation or associations }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Van't Hoff factor = 0.5 Amount of K 2 SO 4 dissolved = 2.32 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g Volume of solution = 2 L Temperature = 25 + 273 = 298 K Molar mass of K 2 SO 4 = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 39) + 32 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 174 g/mol Since K 2 SO 4 dissociates completely as {tex}\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}{/tex} Total ions produced after dissociation = 3 So, i = 3, {tex}\pi{/tex} = iCRT C = {tex}\frac{4}{V}{/tex} {tex}\pi=\frac{3 \times 2.32 \times 10^{-2}}{174 \times 2} \times{/tex} 0.082 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 {tex}=\frac{85.14}{174 \times 100}{/tex} = 0.0048 Osmotic pressure of the solution = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 atm K f for benzene = 5.12 K kg mol -1 W B = mass of solute = 25.6 g W A = 1000 g {tex}\Delta T_f{/tex} = 5.12 K kg mol -1 Atomic mass of sulphur = 32 g mol -1 {tex}M_B=\frac{K_f \times W_B \times 1000}{\Delta T_f \times W_A}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5.12 \times 25.6 \times 1000}{0.512 \times 1000}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{131.072}{0.152}{/tex} = 256 Molecular mass of S x = x {tex}\times{/tex} 32 = 256 x = {tex}\frac{256}{32}{/tex} = 8 The formula of sulphur = S 8
solution: In benzene, two molecules of ethanoic acid associate to form a dimer. 2 CH 3 COOH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} (CH 3 COOH 2 ) Thus, the van't Hoff factor, {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{\text { Number of solutes after dissociation or associations }}{\text { Number of solutes before dissociation or associations }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Van't Hoff factor = 0.5 Amount of K 2 SO 4 dissolved = 2.32 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g Volume of solution = 2 L Temperature = 25 + 273 = 298 K Molar mass of K 2 SO 4 = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 39) + 32 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 174 g/mol Since K 2 SO 4 dissociates completely as {tex}\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}{/tex} Total ions produced after dissociation = 3 So, i = 3, {tex}\pi{/tex} = iCRT C = {tex}\frac{4}{V}{/tex} {tex}\pi=\frac{3 \times 2.32 \times 10^{-2}}{174 \times 2} \times{/tex} 0.082 {tex}\times{/tex} 298 {tex}=\frac{85.14}{174 \times 100}{/tex} = 0.0048 Osmotic pressure of the solution = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 atm K f for benzene = 5.12 K kg mol -1 W B = mass of solute = 25.6 g W A = 1000 g {tex}\Delta T_f{/tex} = 5.12 K kg mol -1 Atomic mass of sulphur = 32 g mol -1 {tex}M_B=\frac{K_f \times W_B \times 1000}{\Delta T_f \times W_A}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5.12 \times 25.6 \times 1000}{0.512 \times 1000}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{131.072}{0.152}{/tex} = 256 Molecular mass of S x = x {tex}\times{/tex} 32 = 256 x = {tex}\frac{256}{32}{/tex} = 8 The formula of sulphur = S 8
solution_html:
1. In benzene, two molecules of ethanoic acid associate to form a dimer.
    2 CH3COOH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} (CH3COOH2)
    Thus, the van't Hoff factor,
    {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{\text { Number of solutes after dissociation or associations }}{\text { Number of solutes before dissociation or associations }}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    Van't Hoff factor = 0.5
  2. Amount of K2SO4 dissolved = 2.32 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 g
    Volume of solution = 2 L
    Temperature = 25 + 273 = 298 K
    Molar mass of K2SO4 = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 39) + 32 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 174 g/mol
    Since K2SO4 dissociates completely as
    {tex}\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}{/tex}
    Total ions produced after dissociation = 3
    So, i = 3, {tex}\pi{/tex} = iCRT
    C = {tex}\frac{4}{V}{/tex}
    {tex}\pi=\frac{3 \times 2.32 \times 10^{-2}}{174 \times 2} \times{/tex} 0.082 {tex}\times{/tex} 298
    {tex}=\frac{85.14}{174 \times 100}{/tex}
    = 0.0048
    Osmotic pressure of the solution = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 atm
  3. Kf for benzene = 5.12 K kg mol-1
    WB = mass of solute = 25.6 g
    WA = 1000 g
    {tex}\Delta T_f{/tex} = 5.12 K kg mol-1
    Atomic mass of sulphur = 32 g mol-1
    {tex}M_B=\frac{K_f \times W_B \times 1000}{\Delta T_f \times W_A}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{5.12 \times 25.6 \times 1000}{0.512 \times 1000}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{131.072}{0.152}{/tex}
    = 256
    Molecular mass of
    Sx = x {tex}\times{/tex} 32 = 256
    x = {tex}\frac{256}{32}{/tex} = 8
    The formula of sulphur = S8

question_text: When 19.5 g of F - CH 2 – COOH (Molar mass = 78 g mol -1 ), is dissolved in 500 g of water, the depression in freezing point is observed to be 1 o C. Calculate the degree of dissociation of F - CH 2 - COOH. [Given: K f for water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 ]
explanation: {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{iK}_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{/tex} {tex}1=\mathrm{i} \times 1.86 \times \frac{19.5}{78} \times \frac{1000}{500}{/tex} i = 1.075 {tex}\alpha=\frac{\mathrm{i}-1}{\mathrm{n}-1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1-075-1}{2-1}{/tex} Degree of dissociation= 0.075
solution: {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{iK}_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{/tex} {tex}1=\mathrm{i} \times 1.86 \times \frac{19.5}{78} \times \frac{1000}{500}{/tex} i = 1.075 {tex}\alpha=\frac{\mathrm{i}-1}{\mathrm{n}-1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1-075-1}{2-1}{/tex} Degree of dissociation= 0.075
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
{tex}=\mathrm{iK}_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{/tex}
{tex}1=\mathrm{i} \times 1.86 \times \frac{19.5}{78} \times \frac{1000}{500}{/tex}
i = 1.075
{tex}\alpha=\frac{\mathrm{i}-1}{\mathrm{n}-1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1-075-1}{2-1}{/tex}

Degree of dissociation= 0.075

question_html:
If benzoic acid (M = 122 g mol-1) is associated into a dimer when dissolved in benzene and the osmotic pressure of a solution of 6.1 g of benzoic acid in 100 mL benzene is 6.5 atm at 27oC, then what is the percentage association of benzoic acid?
(Given : R = 0.0821 L atm K-1 mol-1)
question_text: If benzoic acid (M = 122 g mol -1 ) is associated into a dimer when dissolved in benzene and the osmotic pressure of a solution of 6.1 g of benzoic acid in 100 mL benzene is 6.5 atm at 27 o C, then what is the percentage association of benzoic acid? (Given : R = 0.0821 L atm K -1 mol -1 )
explanation: {tex}\pi=\mathrm{i} \frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{RT}{/tex} 6.5 = {tex}\mathrm{i} \times \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~V}} \times{/tex} 0.0821 6.5 = {tex}\text { i } \times \frac{6 \cdot 1}{122} \times \frac{1000}{100 L} \times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 K {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{6 \cdot 5 \times 122}{6 \cdot 1 \times 0 \cdot 0821 \times 300 \times 10}{/tex} = 0.528 {tex}\alpha=\frac{1-\mathrm{i}}{1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}}=\frac{1-0.528}{1-\frac{1}{2}}={/tex} percentage association= 0.944 or 94.4%
solution: {tex}\pi=\mathrm{i} \frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{RT}{/tex} 6.5 = {tex}\mathrm{i} \times \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~V}} \times{/tex} 0.0821 6.5 = {tex}\text { i } \times \frac{6 \cdot 1}{122} \times \frac{1000}{100 L} \times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 K {tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{6 \cdot 5 \times 122}{6 \cdot 1 \times 0 \cdot 0821 \times 300 \times 10}{/tex} = 0.528 {tex}\alpha=\frac{1-\mathrm{i}}{1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}}=\frac{1-0.528}{1-\frac{1}{2}}={/tex} percentage association= 0.944 or 94.4%
solution_html:
{tex}\pi=\mathrm{i} \frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{RT}{/tex}
6.5 = {tex}\mathrm{i} \times \frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~V}} \times{/tex} 0.0821
6.5 = {tex}\text { i } \times \frac{6 \cdot 1}{122} \times \frac{1000}{100 L} \times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 K
{tex}\mathrm{i}=\frac{6 \cdot 5 \times 122}{6 \cdot 1 \times 0 \cdot 0821 \times 300 \times 10}{/tex} = 0.528
{tex}\alpha=\frac{1-\mathrm{i}}{1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}}=\frac{1-0.528}{1-\frac{1}{2}}={/tex} percentage association= 0.944 or 94.4%

question_text: Find the van't Hoff factor for aqueous KCl, assuming complete dissociation. A solution of an organic compound is prepared by dissolving 68.4 g in 1000 g of water. Calculate the molar mass of the compound when elevation in boiling point is 0.104 K and K b for water is 0.52 K kg mol -1 .

solution: i = 2(K + and Cl - ) M b = {tex}\frac{K_b X w_2 X1000}{\Delta T_b Xw_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.52 \times 68.4 \times 1000}{0.104 \times 1000}{/tex} = 342 g mol -1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Raoult's law for volatile liquids states that the partial vapour pressure of each component in the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction, whereas for a non-volatile solute, it states that the vapour pressure of a solution of a non-volatile solute is equal to the vapour pressure of the pure solvent at that temperature multiplied by its mole fraction. Two liquids A and B are mixed with each other to form a solution, the vapour phase consists of both components of the solution. Once the components in the solution have reached equilibrium, the total vapour pressure of the solution can be determined by combining Raoult's law with Dalton's law of partial pressures. If a non-volatile solute B is dissolved into a solvent A to form a solution, the vapour pressure of the solution will be lower than that of the pure solvent. The solutions which obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration are ideal solutions, whereas the solutions for which vapour pressure is either higher or lower than that predicted by Raoult's law are called non-ideal solutions. Non-ideal solutions are identified by determining the strength of the intermolecular forces between the different molecules in that particular solution. They can either show positive or negative deviation from Raoult's law depending on whether the A - B interactions in solution are stronger or weaker than A - A and B - B interactions.

  1. 20 mL of a liquid A was mixed with 20 mL of liquid B. The volume of resulting solution was found to be less than 40 mL. What do you conclude from the above data? (1)
  2. Which of the following show positive deviation from Raoult's law? Carbon disulphide and Acetone; Phenol and Aniline; Ethanol and Acetone. (1)
  3. The vapour pressure of a solution of glucose in water is 750 mm Hg at 100oC. Calculate the mole fraction of solute. (2)
    (Vapour pressure of water at 373 K = 760 mm Hg)
    OR
    The boiling point of solution increases when 1 mol of NaCl is added to 1 litre of water while addition of 1 mol of methanol to one litre of water decreases its boiling point. Explain the above observations. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Raoult's law for volatile liquids states that the partial vapour pressure of each component in the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction, whereas for a non-volatile solute, it states that the vapour pressure of a solution of a non-volatile solute is equal to the vapour pressure of the pure solvent at that temperature multiplied by its mole fraction. Two liquids A and B are mixed with each other to form a solution, the vapour phase consists of both components of the solution. Once the components in the solution have reached equilibrium, the total vapour pressure of the solution can be determined by combining Raoult's law with Dalton's law of partial pressures. If a non-volatile solute B is dissolved into a solvent A to form a solution, the vapour pressure of the solution will be lower than that of the pure solvent. The solutions which obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration are ideal solutions, whereas the solutions for which vapour pressure is either higher or lower than that predicted by Raoult's law are called non-ideal solutions. Non-ideal solutions are identified by determining the strength of the intermolecular forces between the different molecules in that particular solution. They can either show positive or negative deviation from Raoult's law depending on whether the A - B interactions in solution are stronger or weaker than A - A and B - B interactions. 20 mL of a liquid A was mixed with 20 mL of liquid B. The volume of resulting solution was found to be less than 40 mL. What do you conclude from the above data? (1) Which of the following show positive deviation from Raoult's law? Carbon disulphide and Acetone; Phenol and Aniline; Ethanol and Acetone. (1) The vapour pressure of a solution of glucose in water is 750 mm Hg at 100 o C. Calculate the mole fraction of solute. (2) (Vapour pressure of water at 373 K = 760 mm Hg) OR The boiling point of solution increases when 1 mol of NaCl is added to 1 litre of water while addition of 1 mol of methanol to one litre of water decreases its boiling point. Explain the above observations. (2)
explanation: Solution shows a negative deviation from Raoult’s law / A-A and B-B interactions are weaker than A-B interactions. Carbon disulphide and acetone, Ethanol and acetone. According to Raoult’s law: p 1 = {tex}p_1^0 x_1{/tex} or x 1 = {tex}\frac{p_1}{p_1^0}{/tex} x 1 = {tex}\frac{750}{760}{/tex} = 0.987 x 2 = 1 - x 1 = 1 - 0.987 = 0.013 OR NaCl is a non-volatile solute, when it is added to water the vapour pressure decreases and hence boiling point increases. Methanol is a volatile solute and its addition to water increases the total vapour pressure of the solution and hence boiling point decreases.
solution: Solution shows a negative deviation from Raoult’s law / A-A and B-B interactions are weaker than A-B interactions. Carbon disulphide and acetone, Ethanol and acetone. Methanol is a volatile solute and its addition to water increases the total vapour pressure of the solution and hence boiling point decreases.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What type of deviation from Raoult's law is observed by mixing chloroform and acetone? Why is a decrease in vapour pressure observed on mixing chloroform and acetone?
question_text: What type of deviation from Raoult's law is observed by mixing chloroform and acetone? Why is a decrease in vapour pressure observed on mixing chloroform and acetone?
explanation: The mixing of chloroform and acetone shows a negative deviation from Raoult's law. The decrease in vapor pressure is due to strong intermolecular interactions between chloroform and acetone molecules, leading to the formation of hydrogen bonds or dipole-dipole interactions, which reduce the overall vapor pressure of the mixture.

question_text: Calculate the mass of ascorbic acid (Vitamin C, C 6 H 8 O 6 ) to be dissolved in 75 g of acetic acid to lower its melting point by 1.5 o C. K f = 3.9 K kg mol -1 .
explanation: Mass of acetic acid, w 1 = 75 Molar mass of ascorbic acid (C 6 H 8 O 5 ), M 2 = (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12) +( 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 ) + (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 16) = 176 g mol -1 Lowering of melting point, {tex}\Delta T{\text{f}} = 1.5\,K{/tex} We know that: {tex}\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} = \frac{{{K_{\text{f}}} \times {w_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {w_1}}}{/tex} {tex}{w_2} = \frac{{\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} \times {M_2} \times {w_1}}}{{{K_{\text{f}}} \times 1000}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.5 \times 176 \times 75}}{{3.9 \times 1000}}{/tex} = 5.076 g Hence, 5.076 g of ascorbic acid is needed to be dissolved.
solution: Mass of acetic acid, w 1 = 75 Molar mass of ascorbic acid (C 6 H 8 O 5 ), M 2 = (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12) +( 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 ) + (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 16) = 176 g mol -1 Lowering of melting point, {tex}\Delta T{\text{f}} = 1.5\,K{/tex} We know that: {tex}\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} = \frac{{{K_{\text{f}}} \times {w_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {w_1}}}{/tex} {tex}{w_2} = \frac{{\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} \times {M_2} \times {w_1}}}{{{K_{\text{f}}} \times 1000}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.5 \times 176 \times 75}}{{3.9 \times 1000}}{/tex} = 5.076 g Hence, 5.076 g of ascorbic acid is needed to be dissolved.
solution_html:
Mass of acetic acid, w1 = 75
Molar mass of ascorbic acid (C6H8O5), M2 = (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12) +( 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 ) + (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 16)
= 176 g mol-1
Lowering of melting point, {tex}\Delta T{\text{f}} = 1.5\,K{/tex}
We know that:
{tex}\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} = \frac{{{K_{\text{f}}} \times {w_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {w_1}}}{/tex}
{tex}{w_2} = \frac{{\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} \times {M_2} \times {w_1}}}{{{K_{\text{f}}} \times 1000}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1.5 \times 176 \times 75}}{{3.9 \times 1000}}{/tex}
= 5.076 g 
Hence, 5.076 g of ascorbic acid is needed to be dissolved.

question_text: For a 5% solution of urea (Molar mass = 60 g/mol), calculate the osmotic pressure at 300 K. [R = 0·0821 L atm K -1 mol -1 ]
explanation: Osmotic pressure ( {tex}\pi{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{W \times R \times T}{M \times V}{/tex} W = 5 gm, R = 0·0821 L atm K -1 mol -1 V(Volume in litres) = {tex}\frac{100}{1000}{/tex} = 0.1 litres, M = 60 gm/mole, T = 300 K {tex}\pi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \times 0.0821 \times 300}{60 \times 0.1}{/tex} Osmotic pressure at 300K = 20.525 atm

= 0.1 litres, M = 60 gm/mole, T = 300 K
