question_text: Visha took two aqueous solutions — one containing 7·5 g of urea (Molar mass = 60 g/mol) and the other containing 42.75 g of substance Z in 100 g of water, respectively. It was observed that both the solutions froze at the same temperature. Calculate the molar mass of Z.
explanation: {tex}\triangle T_{f}{/tex} = molality {tex}\times{/tex} K f Since {tex}\triangle T_{f}{/tex} is same for both solutions, i.e. {tex}\triangle T_{f_{1}}-\Delta T_{f_{2}}{/tex} (given) molality (urea) {tex}\times{/tex} K f = molality (x) {tex}\times{/tex} K f {tex}\frac{\frac{7}{60} \times 1000}{100}=\frac{\frac{42}{M_{w(x)}} \times 1000}{100}{/tex} M w = {tex}\frac{42 \times 60}{7}{/tex} = 360 gm Hence, molar mass of Z = 360gm.
solution: {tex}\triangle T_{f}{/tex} = molality {tex}\times{/tex} K f Since {tex}\triangle T_{f}{/tex} is same for both solutions, i.e. {tex}\triangle T_{f_{1}}-\Delta T_{f_{2}}{/tex} (given) molality (urea) {tex}\times{/tex} K f = molality (x) {tex}\times{/tex} K f {tex}\frac{\frac{7}{60} \times 1000}{100}=\frac{\frac{42}{M_{w(x)}} \times 1000}{100}{/tex} M w = {tex}\frac{42 \times 60}{7}{/tex} = 360 gm Hence, molar mass of Z = 360gm.
solution_html:
{tex}\triangle T_{f}{/tex} = molality {tex}\times{/tex} Kf 
Since {tex}\triangle T_{f}{/tex} is same for both solutions,
i.e. {tex}\triangle T_{f_{1}}-\Delta T_{f_{2}}{/tex} (given)
molality(urea) {tex}\times{/tex} Kf = molality(x) {tex}\times{/tex} Kf 
{tex}\frac{\frac{7}{60} \times 1000}{100}=\frac{\frac{42}{M_{w(x)}} \times 1000}{100}{/tex} 
Mw = {tex}\frac{42 \times 60}{7}{/tex}  = 360 gm

Hence, molar mass of Z = 360gm.

1. A solution contains 5.85 g NaCl (Molar mass = 58.5 g mol-1) per litre of solution. It has an osmotic pressure of 4.75 atm at 27oC. Calculate the degree of dissociation of NaCl in this solution (Given: R = 0.082 L atm K-1 mol-1)
  2. State Henry’s law. Why is air diluted with helium in the tanks used by scuba divers?
question_text: A solution contains 5.85 g NaCl (Molar mass = 58.5 g mol -1 ) per litre of solution. It has an osmotic pressure of 4.75 atm at 27 o C. Calculate the degree of dissociation of NaCl in this solution (Given: R = 0.082 L atm K -1 mol -1 ) State Henry’s law. Why is air diluted with helium in the tanks used by scuba divers?
explanation: Given: Weight of NaCl given = 5.85g molecular mass of NaCl = 58.5g/mol Osmatic pressure = 4.75 atm Temperature T = 27 C = (27 + 273) K = 300 K degree of dissociation of NaCl = ? We know that, {tex}\pi{/tex} = iCRT, C = {tex}\frac{5.85}{58.5 \times 1} M{/tex} 4.75 = {tex}\frac{i \times 5.85}{58.5 / 1} \times 0.082 \times 300{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{4.75 \times 58.5}{5.85 \times 0.082 \times 300}{/tex} i = 1.93 For dissociation, Here n 2 i = 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (n - 1) 1.93 = 1 + (2 - 1) {tex}\alpha{/tex} 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.93 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.93 - 1 Hence, degree of dissociation of NaCl = 0.93 or 93% Henry’s law: At a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution. Air diluted with the helium (11% helium, 56.2% nitrogen, and 32.1% oxygen) in the tanks used by scuba divers to avoid bends, as well as, the toxic effects of high concentrations of nitrogen in the blood because of an increase in pressure underwater and decreasing pressure towards the water surface.
solution: Given: Weight of NaCl given = 5.85g molecular mass of NaCl = 58.5g/mol Osmatic pressure = 4.75 atm Temperature T = 27 C = (27 + 273) K = 300 K degree of dissociation of NaCl = ? We know that, {tex}\pi{/tex} = iCRT, C = {tex}\frac{5.85}{58.5 \times 1} M{/tex} 4.75 = {tex}\frac{i \times 5.85}{58.5 / 1} \times 0.082 \times 300{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{4.75 \times 58.5}{5.85 \times 0.082 \times 300}{/tex} i = 1.93 For dissociation, Here n 2 i = 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (n - 1) 1.93 = 1 + (2 - 1) {tex}\alpha{/tex} 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.93 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.93 - 1 Hence, degree of dissociation of NaCl = 0.93 or 93% Henry’s law: At a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution. Air diluted with the helium (11% helium, 56.2% nitrogen, and 32.1% oxygen) in the tanks used by scuba divers to avoid bends, as well as, the toxic effects of high concentrations of nitrogen in the blood because of an increase in pressure underwater and decreasing pressure towards the water surface.
solution_html:
1. Given: Weight of NaCl given = 5.85g
    molecular mass of NaCl = 58.5g/mol
    Osmatic pressure = 4.75 atm
    Temperature T = 27 C
    = (27 + 273) K
    = 300 K
    degree of dissociation of NaCl = ?
    We know that, 
    {tex}\pi{/tex} = iCRT, C = {tex}\frac{5.85}{58.5 \times 1} M{/tex} 
    4.75 = {tex}\frac{i \times 5.85}{58.5 / 1} \times 0.082 \times 300{/tex}
    i = {tex}\frac{4.75 \times 58.5}{5.85 \times 0.082 \times 300}{/tex}
    i = 1.93
    For dissociation, Here n 2
    i = 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex}(n - 1)
    1.93 = 1 + (2 - 1){tex}\alpha{/tex}
    1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.93
    {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.93 - 1
    Hence, degree of dissociation of NaCl = 0.93 or 93%
  2. Henry’s law: At a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution.
    Air diluted with the helium (11% helium, 56.2% nitrogen, and 32.1% oxygen) in the tanks used by scuba divers to avoid bends, as well as, the toxic effects of high concentrations of nitrogen in the blood because of an increase in pressure underwater and decreasing pressure towards the water surface.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
What happens when

  1. a pressure greater than osmotic pressure is applied on the solution side separated from solvent by a semipermeable membrane?
  2. acetone is added to pure ethanol?
question_text: What happens when a pressure greater than osmotic pressure is applied on the solution side separated from solvent by a semipermeable membrane? acetone is added to pure ethanol?
explanation: Reverse osmosis (direction of osmosis is reversed) occurs i.e. net flow of the solvent is from solution to solvent. Hence greater pressure is applied to the solvent molecules from the solution side would move into the pure solvent through a semipermeable membrane. When acetone is added to the ethanol, molecules of acetone get in between the molecules of ethanol and break some of the hydrogen bond, which weakens the intermolecular attractive forces resulting in an increase in vapor pressure of the mixture than ethanol in a pure state.
solution: Reverse osmosis (direction of osmosis is reversed) occurs i.e. net flow of the solvent is from solution to solvent. Hence greater pressure is applied to the solvent molecules from the solution side would move into the pure solvent through a semipermeable membrane. When acetone is added to the ethanol, molecules of acetone get in between the molecules of ethanol and break some of the hydrogen bond, which weakens the intermolecular attractive forces resulting in an increase in vapor pressure of the mixture than ethanol in a pure state.
solution_html:
1. Reverse osmosis (direction of osmosis is reversed) occurs i.e. net flow of the solvent is from solution to solvent. Hence greater pressure is applied to the solvent molecules from the solution side would move into the pure solvent through a semipermeable membrane.
  2.  When acetone is added to the ethanol, molecules of acetone get in between the molecules of ethanol and break some of the hydrogen bond, which weakens the intermolecular attractive forces resulting in an increase in vapor pressure of the mixture than ethanol in a pure state.

question_html: State Raoult's law for a solution containing volatile components. What is the similarity between Raoult’s law and Henry's law?
question_text: State Raoult's law for a solution containing volatile components. What is the similarity between Raoult’s law and Henry's law?
explanation: The partial pressures are related to the mole fractions x 1 and x 2 of the two components 1 and 2 this relation is known as Raoult's law which state that For a solution of volatile liquids: the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in solution. Thus, for any component, partial vapour pressure, p {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = p o {tex}\chi{/tex} Where P o is the vapour pressure of pure component and {tex}\chi{/tex} is the mole fraction of that component. The similarity between Raoult's law and Henry's law: Both laws state that the partial pressure of the volatile component is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the solution. In the case of Raoult's law, it is liquid and in the case of Henry's law, it is gas.

Thus, for any component, partial vapour pressure, p {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = p o {tex}\chi{/tex} Where P o is the vapour pressure of pure component and {tex}\chi{/tex} is the mole fraction of that component. The similarity between Raoult's law and Henry's law: Both laws state that the partial pressure of the volatile component is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the solution. In the case of Raoult's law, it is liquid and in the case of Henry's law, it is gas.

p {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = po{tex}\chi{/tex}
Where Po is the vapour pressure of pure component and {tex}\chi{/tex} is the mole fraction of that component.
The similarity between Raoult's law and Henry's law: Both laws state that the partial pressure of the volatile component is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the solution. In the case of Raoult's law, it is liquid and in the case of Henry's law, it is gas.

question_html: An antifreeze solution is prepared by dissolving 31 g of ethylene glycol (Molar mass = 62 g mol-1) in 600 g of water. Calculate the freezing point of the solution. (Kf for water = 1·86 K kg mol-1)
question_text: An antifreeze solution is prepared by dissolving 31 g of ethylene glycol (Molar mass = 62 g mol -1 ) in 600 g of water. Calculate the freezing point of the solution. (K f for water = 1·86 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: The freezing point of pure water is 273.15 K. On dissolving ethylene glycol, freezing point, being a colligative property, will be lowered. {tex}\Delta T_f{/tex} = {tex}K_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{w_{s} \times 1000}{M_{s} \times W}{/tex} We are given that K f for water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 Mass of solute, W s = 31g Molar mass of ethylene glycol = 62 g mol -1 Mass of water, W = 600 g {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_f{/tex} = {tex}1.86 \times \frac{31 \times 1000}{62 \times 600}{/tex} = 1.54 K Hence, the freezing point of the solution, T f = 273.15 K - 1.54 K = 271.61 K
solution: The freezing point of pure water is 273.15 K. On dissolving ethylene glycol, freezing point, being a colligative property, will be lowered. {tex}\Delta T_f{/tex} = {tex}K_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{w_{s} \times 1000}{M_{s} \times W}{/tex} We are given that K f for water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 Mass of solute, W s = 31g Molar mass of ethylene glycol = 62 g mol -1 Mass of water, W = 600 g {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_f{/tex} = {tex}1.86 \times \frac{31 \times 1000}{62 \times 600}{/tex} = 1.54 K Hence, the freezing point of the solution, T f = 273.15 K - 1.54 K = 271.61 K
solution_html:
The freezing point of pure water is 273.15 K. On dissolving ethylene glycol, freezing point, being a colligative property, will be lowered.
{tex}\Delta T_f{/tex} = {tex}K_{\mathrm{f}} \frac{w_{s} \times 1000}{M_{s} \times W}{/tex} 
We are given that
Kf for water = 1.86 K kg mol-1 
Mass of solute, Ws = 31g 
Molar mass of ethylene glycol = 62 g mol-1 
Mass of water, W = 600 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_f{/tex} = {tex}1.86 \times \frac{31 \times 1000}{62 \times 600}{/tex} = 1.54 K
Hence, the freezing point of the solution,
Tf = 273.15 K - 1.54 K
= 271.61 K

question_html: A 0.01 m aqueous solution of AlCl3 freezes at – 0.068 oC. Calculate the percentage of dissociation. [Given: Kf for Water = 1.86 K kg mol-1]
question_text: A 0.01 m aqueous solution of AlCl 3 freezes at – 0.068 o C. Calculate the percentage of dissociation. [Given: K f for Water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 ]
explanation: Freezing point = -0.068 o C K f for water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 {tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = normal boiling point - Actual boiling point {tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = 0 - (-0.068) = 0.068 {tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = {tex}i \times K_{f} \times m{/tex} 0.068 = i {tex}\times{/tex} 1.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01 i = {tex}\frac{0.068}{1.86 \times 0.01}{/tex} = 3.6559 We have formula, i = {tex}\frac{1+(n-1) \alpha}{1} \alpha=\frac{i-1}{n-1}{/tex} For AlCl 3 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Al 3+ + 3Cl - n = 1 + 3 = 4 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3.6559-1}{4-1}{/tex} = 0.8853 percentage of dissociation = {tex}\alpha \times 100{/tex} = 0.8853 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 88.53%
solution: Freezing point = -0.068 o C K f for water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 {tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = normal boiling point - Actual boiling point {tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = 0 - (-0.068) = 0.068 {tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = {tex}i \times K_{f} \times m{/tex} 0.068 = i {tex}\times{/tex} 1.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01 i = {tex}\frac{0.068}{1.86 \times 0.01}{/tex} = 3.6559 We have formula, i = {tex}\frac{1+(n-1) \alpha}{1} \alpha=\frac{i-1}{n-1}{/tex} For AlCl 3 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Al 3+ + 3Cl - n = 1 + 3 = 4 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3.6559-1}{4-1}{/tex} = 0.8853 percentage of dissociation = {tex}\alpha \times 100{/tex} = 0.8853 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 88.53%
solution_html:
Freezing point = -0.068o C
Kf for water = 1.86 K kg mol-1 
{tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = normal boiling point - Actual boiling point
{tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = 0 - (-0.068) = 0.068
{tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = {tex}i \times K_{f} \times m{/tex} 
0.068 = i {tex}\times{/tex} 1.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01
i = {tex}\frac{0.068}{1.86 \times 0.01}{/tex} = 3.6559
We have formula,
i = {tex}\frac{1+(n-1) \alpha}{1} \alpha=\frac{i-1}{n-1}{/tex} 
For AlCl3 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Al3+ + 3Cl- 
n = 1 + 3 = 4
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3.6559-1}{4-1}{/tex} = 0.8853
percentage of dissociation = {tex}\alpha \times 100{/tex} = 0.8853 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 88.53%

question_html: State Henry’s law. Calculate the solubility of CO2 in water at 298 K under 760 mm Hg. (KH for CO2 in water at 298 K is 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 mm Hg)
question_text: State Henry’s law. Calculate the solubility of CO 2 in water at 298 K under 760 mm Hg. (K H for CO 2 in water at 298 K is 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 mm Hg)
explanation: Henry’s Law states that “the partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase (p) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas (x) in the solution” and is expressed as: p = K ​​​​​​H x Here K ​​​​​​H is the Henry’s law constant. Given, T = 298 K, K ​​​​​​H = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 , p = 760 mm Hg We know by Henry's Law, p = K ​​​​​​H {tex}\times{/tex} x, where x is mole fraction of the gas in the solution. 760 = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} x x = {tex}\frac{760}{1.25 \times 10^6}{/tex} x = 6.08 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -4
solution: Henry’s Law states that “the partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase (p) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas (x) in the solution” and is expressed as: p = K ​​​​​​H x Here K ​​​​​​H is the Henry’s law constant. Given, T = 298 K, K ​​​​​​H = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 , p = 760 mm Hg We know by Henry's Law, p = K ​​​​​​H {tex}\times{/tex} x, where x is mole fraction of the gas in the solution. 760 = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 {tex}\times{/tex} x x = {tex}\frac{760}{1.25 \times 10^6}{/tex} x = 6.08 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -4
solution_html:
Henry’s Law states that “the partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase (p) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas (x) in the solution” and is expressed as:
p = K​​​​​​H x 
Here K​​​​​​H is the Henry’s law constant. 
Given, T = 298 K, K​​​​​​H = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 106, p = 760 mm Hg
We know by Henry's Law, p = K​​​​​​H {tex}\times{/tex} x, where x is mole fraction of the gas in the solution.
760 = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 {tex}\times{/tex} x
x = {tex}\frac{760}{1.25 \times 10^6}{/tex} 
x = 6.08 {tex}\times {/tex} 10-4

    2. Meat is preserved for a longer time by salting.
question_text: When 19.5 g of F – CH 2 – COOH (Molar mass = 78 g mol -1 ) is dissolved in 500 g of water, the depression in freezing point is observed to be 1 o C. Calculate the degree of dissociation of F – CH 2 – COOH. [Given: K f for water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 ] Give reasons: 0.1 M KCl has higher boiling point than 0.1 M Glucose. Meat is preserved for a longer time by salting.
explanation: mass of solute = 19.5g molar mass of solute (F- Cl 2 - COOH) = 78 g mol -1 mass of solvent i.e water = 500g; k f value for water = 1.86 k kg mol -1 depression in freezing point = 1 C degree of dissociation of solute = ? No. of moles solute = {tex}\frac{19.5}{78}{/tex} = 0.25 molality is the no. of moles of solute in 1 kg solvent molality = {tex}\frac{0.25}{\frac{500}{1000}}{/tex} = 0.50 m Calculated depression in freezing point; {tex}\Delta T_{f}=K_{f} \times m{/tex} = 1.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.50 = 0.93 K i = {tex}\frac{\text{Observed freezing point}}{\text{ Calculated freezing point}}{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{1.0}{0.93}{/tex} = 1.0753 Let, C is the initial conc. of bluoroacetic acid and {tex}\alpha{/tex} be its degreee of dissociation. {tex}\mathop {C{H_2}FCOOH}\limits_{C(1 - \alpha )} \to \mathop {C{H_3}FCO{O^ - }}\limits_{C\alpha } + \mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_{C\varepsilon }{/tex} Total number of moles = C(1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) + C {tex}\alpha{/tex} + C {tex}\alpha{/tex} = C(1 + 2) 1.0753 = 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} degree of dissociation of solute= 0.0753 KCl dissociates in the solution and forms ions K + and Cl - and glucose does not dissociate. Since the boiling point is a colligative property and depends on the number of particles. Therefore, 0.1 M KCl has a higher boiling point than the 0.1 M glucose. Meat is preserved for a longer time by salting so that it can be protected against bacterial action for longer.
solution: mass of solute = 19.5g molar mass of solute (F- Cl 2 - COOH) = 78 g mol -1 mass of solvent i.e water = 500g; k f value for water = 1.86 k kg mol -1 depression in freezing point = 1 C degree of dissociation of solute = ? No. of moles solute = {tex}\frac{19.5}{78}{/tex} = 0.25 molality is the no. of moles of solute in 1 kg solvent molality = {tex}\frac{0.25}{\frac{500}{1000}}{/tex} = 0.50 m Calculated depression in freezing point; {tex}\Delta T_{f}=K_{f} \times m{/tex} = 1.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.50 = 0.93 K i = {tex}\frac{\text{Observed freezing point}}{\text{ Calculated freezing point}}{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{1.0}{0.93}{/tex} = 1.0753 Let, C is the initial conc. of bluoroacetic acid and {tex}\alpha{/tex} be its degreee of dissociation. {tex}\mathop {C{H_2}FCOOH}\limits_{C(1 - \alpha )} \to \mathop {C{H_3}FCO{O^ - }}\limits_{C\alpha } + \mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_{C\varepsilon }{/tex} Total number of moles = C(1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) + C {tex}\alpha{/tex} + C {tex}\alpha{/tex} = C(1 + 2) 1.0753 = 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} degree of dissociation of solute= 0.0753 KCl dissociates in the solution and forms ions K + and Cl - and glucose does not dissociate. Since the boiling point is a colligative property and depends on the number of particles. Therefore, 0.1 M KCl has a higher boiling point than the 0.1 M glucose. Meat is preserved for a longer time by salting so that it can be protected against bacterial action for longer.
solution_html:
1. mass of solute = 19.5g
    molar mass of solute (F- Cl2 - COOH) = 78 g mol-1 
    mass of solvent i.e water = 500g; kf value for water = 1.86 k kg mol-1 
    depression in freezing point = 1 C
    degree of dissociation of solute = ?
    No. of moles solute = {tex}\frac{19.5}{78}{/tex} = 0.25
    molality is the no. of moles of solute in 1 kg solvent 
    molality = {tex}\frac{0.25}{\frac{500}{1000}}{/tex} = 0.50 m
    Calculated depression in freezing point;
    {tex}\Delta T_{f}=K_{f} \times m{/tex}
    = 1.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.50 = 0.93 K
    i = {tex}\frac{\text{Observed freezing point}}{\text{ Calculated freezing point}}{/tex} 
    i = {tex}\frac{1.0}{0.93}{/tex} = 1.0753
    Let, C is the initial conc. of bluoroacetic acid and {tex}\alpha{/tex} be its degreee of dissociation.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_2}FCOOH}\limits_{C(1 - \alpha )} \to \mathop {C{H_3}FCO{O^ - }}\limits_{C\alpha } + \mathop {{H^ + }}\limits_{C\varepsilon }{/tex} 
    Total number of moles = C(1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) + C{tex}\alpha{/tex} + C{tex}\alpha{/tex} 
    = C(1 + 2)
    1.0753 = 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} 
    {tex}\alpha{/tex} degree of dissociation of solute= 0.0753
    1. KCl dissociates in the solution and forms ions K+ and Cl- and glucose does not dissociate. Since the boiling point is a colligative property and depends on the number of particles. Therefore, 0.1 M KCl has a higher boiling point than the 0.1 M glucose.
    2. Meat is preserved for a longer time by salting so that it can be protected against bacterial action for longer.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: State Henry’s law and write its two applications.
question_text: State Henry’s law and write its two applications.
explanation: Henry's law states that the partial pressure (P) of the gas in the vapour phase is directly proportional to the mole fraction (m) of the gas in the solution. Mathematically, m = KP where K is the proportionality constant known as Henry's constant. Applications of Henry's Law To increase the solubility of CO 2 in soft drinks and soda water, the bottle is sealed under high pressure. To minimise the painful effects of bends or decompression sickness in deep-sea divers, oxygen diluted with less soluble helium gas is used as breathing gas.
solution: Henry's law states that the partial pressure (P) of the gas in the vapour phase is directly proportional to the mole fraction (m) of the gas in the solution. Mathematically, m = KP where K is the proportionality constant known as Henry's constant. Applications of Henry's Law To increase the solubility of CO 2 in soft drinks and soda water, the bottle is sealed under high pressure. To minimise the painful effects of bends or decompression sickness in deep-sea divers, oxygen diluted with less soluble helium gas is used as breathing gas.
solution_html:
Henry's law states that the partial pressure (P) of the gas in the vapour phase is directly proportional to the mole fraction (m) of the gas in the solution.
Mathematically,
m = KP where K is the proportionality constant known as Henry's constant.

Applications of Henry's Law

  1. To increase the solubility of CO2 in soft drinks and soda water, the bottle is sealed under high pressure.
  2. To minimise the painful effects of bends or decompression sickness in deep-sea divers, oxygen diluted with less soluble helium gas is used as breathing gas.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: State Raoult’s law for a solution containing volatile components. Write two characteristics of the solution which obeys Raoult’s law at all concentrations.
question_text: State Raoult’s law for a solution containing volatile components. Write two characteristics of the solution which obeys Raoult’s law at all concentrations.
explanation: Raoult’s law states that for a solution of volatile liquids, the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in solution. Characteristics of the solution which obeys Raoult's law at all concentrations are: The enthalpy of mixing of the pure components to form the solution is zero, i.e. {tex}\Delta _ { mix } H = 0{/tex} The volume of mixing is also zero i.e. {tex}\Delta _ { mix } V = 0{/tex}
solution: Raoult’s law states that for a solution of volatile liquids, the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in solution. Characteristics of the solution which obeys Raoult's law at all concentrations are: The enthalpy of mixing of the pure components to form the solution is zero, i.e. {tex}\Delta _ { mix } H = 0{/tex} The volume of mixing is also zero i.e. {tex}\Delta _ { mix } V = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
Raoult’s law states that for a solution of volatile liquids, the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in solution.
Characteristics of the solution which obeys Raoult's law at all concentrations are:

  1. The enthalpy of mixing of the pure components to form the solution is zero, i.e. {tex}\Delta _ { mix } H = 0{/tex}
  2. The volume of mixing is also zero i.e. {tex}\Delta _ { mix } V = 0{/tex}

question_text: A solution containing 1.9 g per 100 mL of KCl (M = 74.5 g mol –1 ) is isotonic with a solution containing 3 g per 100 mL of urea (M = 60 g mol –1 ). Calculate the degree of dissociation of KCl solution. Assume that both the solutions have same temperature.
explanation: According to the question, a solution containing 1.9 g per 100 mL of KCl (M = 74.5 g mol –1 ) is isotonic with a solution containing 3 g per 100 mL of urea (M = 60 g mol –1 ). As solution is isotonic, {tex}\pi_1{/tex} (urea) = {tex}\pi _2 {/tex} (KCI) {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C 1 RT = iC 2 RT {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\frac { n _ { 1 } } { V 1 } = \mathrm { i } \frac { n _ { 2 } } { V 2 }{/tex} ( {tex}\because {/tex} V 1 = V 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\frac { 3 } { 60 } = \mathrm { i } \times \frac { 1.9 } { 74.5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} i = 1.96 We know that, {tex}\alpha = \frac { i - 1 } { n - 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1.96 - 1 } { 2 - 1 } \\= 0.96 {/tex} ​​​​​​​= 96%
solution: According to the question, a solution containing 1.9 g per 100 mL of KCl (M = 74.5 g mol –1 ) is isotonic with a solution containing 3 g per 100 mL of urea (M = 60 g mol –1 ). As solution is isotonic, {tex}\pi_1{/tex} (urea) = {tex}\pi _2 {/tex} (KCI) {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} C 1 RT = iC 2 RT {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\frac { n _ { 1 } } { V 1 } = \mathrm { i } \frac { n _ { 2 } } { V 2 }{/tex} ( {tex}\because {/tex} V 1 = V 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\frac { 3 } { 60 } = \mathrm { i } \times \frac { 1.9 } { 74.5 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} i = 1.96 We know that, {tex}\alpha = \frac { i - 1 } { n - 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1.96 - 1 } { 2 - 1 } \\= 0.96 {/tex} ​​​​​​​= 96%
solution_html:
According to the question, a solution containing 1.9 g per 100 mL of KCl (M = 74.5 g mol–1 ) is isotonic with a solution containing 3 g per 100 mL of urea (M = 60 g mol–1 ).
As solution is isotonic, 
{tex}\pi_1{/tex} (urea) = {tex}\pi _2 {/tex} (KCI)
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}C1RT = iC2RT
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}\frac { n _ { 1 } } { V 1 } = \mathrm { i } \frac { n _ { 2 } } { V 2 }{/tex} ({tex}\because {/tex}V1 = V2)
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}\frac { 3 } { 60 } = \mathrm { i } \times \frac { 1.9 } { 74.5 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}i = 1.96
We know that, {tex}\alpha = \frac { i - 1 } { n - 1 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1.96 - 1 } { 2 - 1 } \\= 0.96 {/tex}
​​​​​​​= 96%

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Out of 0.1 molal aqueous solution of glucose and 0.1 molal aqueous solution of KCl, which one will have higher boiling point and why?
  2. Predict whether van’t Hoff factor, (i) is less than one or greater than one in the following:
    1. CH3COOH dissolved in water
    2. CH3COOH dissolved in benzene
question_text: Out of 0.1 molal aqueous solution of glucose and 0.1 molal aqueous solution of KCl, which one will have higher boiling point and why? Predict whether van’t Hoff factor, (i) is less than one or greater than one in the following: CH 3 COOH dissolved in water CH 3 COOH dissolved in benzene
explanation: 0.1 molal KCl will have higher boiling point because KCl undergoes dissociation whereas glucose does not undergo dissociation. Acetic acid dissociates in water. Therefore, Van’t Hoff factor(i) > 1 Acetic acid associates in benzene. Therefore, Van’t Hoff factor(i) < 1
solution: 0.1 molal KCl will have higher boiling point because KCl undergoes dissociation whereas glucose does not undergo dissociation. Acetic acid dissociates in water. Therefore, Van’t Hoff factor(i) > 1 Acetic acid associates in benzene. Therefore, Van’t Hoff factor(i) < 1
solution_html:
1. 0.1 molal KCl will have higher boiling point because KCl undergoes dissociation whereas glucose does not undergo dissociation.
    1. Acetic acid dissociates in water. Therefore, Van’t Hoff factor(i) > 1
    2. Acetic acid associates in benzene. Therefore, Van’t Hoff factor(i) < 1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A solution 0.1 M of Na2SO4 is dissolved to the extent of 95%. What would be its osmotic pressure at 27oC? (R = 0.0821 L atm K-1 mol-1)
question_text: A solution 0.1 M of Na 2 SO 4 is dissolved to the extent of 95%. What would be its osmotic pressure at 27 o C? (R = 0.0821 L atm K -1 mol -1 )
explanation: 0.1 M of Na 2 SO 4 is dissolved to the extent of 95%. Given, C=0.1 M, T = 27 o C=300k {tex}Na_2SO_4\rightarrow 2Na^++SO_4^{-2}{/tex} From above reaction, {tex}n=2+1=3.{/tex} So, {tex}\alpha {/tex} = 0.95 We know that, {tex}\alpha ={(i - 1) \over (n - 1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0.95 ={ (i - 1) \over (3 - 1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow i = 2.9{/tex} Osmotic pressure = {tex}\pi = iCRT{/tex} = 2.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 7.143
solution: 0.1 M of Na 2 SO 4 is dissolved to the extent of 95%. Given, C=0.1 M, T = 27 o C=300k {tex}Na_2SO_4\rightarrow 2Na^++SO_4^{-2}{/tex} From above reaction, {tex}n=2+1=3.{/tex} So, {tex}\alpha {/tex} = 0.95 We know that, {tex}\alpha ={(i - 1) \over (n - 1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0.95 ={ (i - 1) \over (3 - 1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow i = 2.9{/tex} Osmotic pressure = {tex}\pi = iCRT{/tex} = 2.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 7.143
solution_html:
0.1 M of Na2SO4 is dissolved to the extent of 95%.
Given, C=0.1 M, T = 27oC=300k
{tex}Na_2SO_4\rightarrow 2Na^++SO_4^{-2}{/tex}
From above reaction, {tex}n=2+1=3.{/tex}
So, {tex}\alpha {/tex} = 0.95
We know that, {tex}\alpha ={(i - 1) \over (n - 1)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0.95 ={ (i - 1) \over (3 - 1)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow i = 2.9{/tex}
Osmotic pressure = {tex}\pi = iCRT{/tex}
= 2.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 300
= 7.143

question_html:
A 4% solution (w/w) of sucrose (M = 342 g mol-1) in water has a freezing point of 271.15 K. Calculate the freezing point of 5% glucose (M = 180 g mol-1) in water.
(Given: Freezing point of pure water = 273.15 K)
question_text: A 4% solution (w/w) of sucrose (M = 342 g mol -1 ) in water has a freezing point of 271.15 K. Calculate the freezing point of 5% glucose (M = 180 g mol -1 ) in water. (Given: Freezing point of pure water = 273.15 K)
explanation: {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = K f m {tex}\mathrm { K_f } = \Delta \mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { f } } \times \frac { M _ { 2 } \times w _ { 1 } } { w _ { 2 } \times 1000 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 342 \times 96 } { 4 \times 1000 }{/tex} = 16.4 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = K f m' {tex}= \mathrm { K } _ { \mathrm { f } } \frac { \mathrm { W } _ { 2 } \times 1000 } { \mathrm { M } _ { 2 } \mathrm { xw } _ { 1 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 16.4 \times 5 \times 1000 } { 95 \times 180 }{/tex} = 4.8 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T f 0 - T f 4.8 = 273.15 - T f T f = 268.35 K
solution: {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = K f m {tex}\mathrm { K_f } = \Delta \mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { f } } \times \frac { M _ { 2 } \times w _ { 1 } } { w _ { 2 } \times 1000 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 342 \times 96 } { 4 \times 1000 }{/tex} = 16.4 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = K f m' {tex}= \mathrm { K } _ { \mathrm { f } } \frac { \mathrm { W } _ { 2 } \times 1000 } { \mathrm { M } _ { 2 } \mathrm { xw } _ { 1 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 16.4 \times 5 \times 1000 } { 95 \times 180 }{/tex} = 4.8 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T f 0 - T f 4.8 = 273.15 - T f T f = 268.35 K
solution_html:
{tex}\Delta{/tex} Tf = Kfm
{tex}\mathrm { K_f } = \Delta \mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { f } } \times \frac { M _ { 2 } \times w _ { 1 } } { w _ { 2 } \times 1000 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 \times 342 \times 96 } { 4 \times 1000 }{/tex}
= 16.4 K
{tex}\Delta{/tex} Tf = Kfm'
{tex}= \mathrm { K } _ { \mathrm { f } } \frac { \mathrm { W } _ { 2 } \times 1000 } { \mathrm { M } _ { 2 } \mathrm { xw } _ { 1 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 16.4 \times 5 \times 1000 } { 95 \times 180 }{/tex}
= 4.8 K
 {tex}\Delta{/tex} Tf = Tf0 - Tf
4.8 = 273.15 - Tf
Tf = 268.35 K

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give reasons:

  1. A decrease in temperature is observed on mixing ethanol and acetone.
  2. Potassium chloride solution freezes at a lower temperature than water.
question_text: Give reasons: A decrease in temperature is observed on mixing ethanol and acetone. Potassium chloride solution freezes at a lower temperature than water.
explanation: When ethanol is mixed with acetone, it shows positive deviation from Raoult's law and acetone molecules get in between the host molecules and break some hydrogen bonds, which requires higher energy than energy released in the formation of new hydrogen bonds. This results fall in temperature. According to Raoult's law, when a non-volatile solid is added to the solvent, its vapour pressure decreases resulting in decrease in freezing point. Also, the freezing point of water is higher than when water contains KCl.
solution: When ethanol is mixed with acetone, it shows positive deviation from Raoult's law and acetone molecules get in between the host molecules and break some hydrogen bonds, which requires higher energy than energy released in the formation of new hydrogen bonds. This results fall in temperature. According to Raoult's law, when a non-volatile solid is added to the solvent, its vapour pressure decreases resulting in decrease in freezing point. Also, the freezing point of water is higher than when water contains KCl.
solution_html:
1. When ethanol is mixed with acetone, it shows positive deviation from Raoult's law and acetone molecules get in between the host molecules and break some hydrogen bonds, which requires higher energy than energy released in the formation of new hydrogen bonds. This results fall in temperature.
  2. According to Raoult's law, when a non-volatile solid is added to the solvent, its vapour pressure decreases resulting in decrease in freezing point. Also, the freezing point of water is higher than when water contains KCl.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Give reasons:

  1. Cooking is faster in pressure cooker than in cooking pan.
  2. Red Blood Cells (RBC) shrink when placed in saline water but swell in distilled water.
question_text: Give reasons: Cooking is faster in pressure cooker than in cooking pan. Red Blood Cells (RBC) shrink when placed in saline water but swell in distilled water.
explanation: In case of liquids, due to increase of pressure inside the cooker, the boiling point of water increases leading to faster cooking than in pan. RBC shrink in saline water due to loss of water owing to exosmosis. In distilled water they swell due to endosmosis as the water enters the RBC.
solution: In case of liquids, due to increase of pressure inside the cooker, the boiling point of water increases leading to faster cooking than in pan. RBC shrink in saline water due to loss of water owing to exosmosis. In distilled water they swell due to endosmosis as the water enters the RBC.
solution_html:
1. In case of liquids, due to increase of pressure inside the cooker, the boiling point of water increases leading to faster cooking than in pan.
  2. RBC shrink in saline water due to loss of water owing to exosmosis. In distilled water they swell due to endosmosis as the water enters the RBC.

question_html: At 300 K, 30 g of glucose present in a litre of its solution has an osmotic pressure of 4.98 bar. If the osmotic pressure of a glucose solution is 1.52 bar at the same temperature, what would be its concentration?
question_text: At 300 K, 30 g of glucose present in a litre of its solution has an osmotic pressure of 4.98 bar. If the osmotic pressure of a glucose solution is 1.52 bar at the same temperature, what would be its concentration?
explanation: {tex}\pi{/tex} = CRT 4.98 = (30/180/1) {tex}\times{/tex} RT 4.98= 0.166 RT …(i) 1.52 = CRT …(ii) Divide eq. (ii) by (i) 0.305 = C/0.166 C = 0.0506 mol l -1 Hence, its concentration would be 0.0506 mol l -1
solution: {tex}\pi{/tex} = CRT 4.98 = (30/180/1) {tex}\times{/tex} RT 4.98= 0.166 RT …(i) 1.52 = CRT …(ii) Divide eq. (ii) by (i) 0.305 = C/0.166 C = 0.0506 mol l -1 Hence, its concentration would be 0.0506 mol l -1
solution_html:
{tex}\pi{/tex} = CRT
4.98 = (30/180/1) {tex}\times{/tex} RT
4.98= 0.166 RT …(i)
1.52 = CRT …(ii)
Divide eq. (ii) by (i)
0.305 = C/0.166
C = 0.0506 mol l-1
Hence, its concentration would be 0.0506 mol l-1

question_html:

  2. 2 g of benzoic (C6H5COOH) dissolved in 25 g of benzene shows a depression in freezing point equal to 1.62 K. Molal depression constant for benzene is 4.9 K kg mol-1. What is the percentage association of acid if it forms dimer in solution?
question_text: Write two characteristics of non-ideal solution. 2 g of benzoic (C 6 H 5 COOH) dissolved in 25 g of benzene shows a depression in freezing point equal to 1.62 K. Molal depression constant for benzene is 4.9 K kg mol -1 . What is the percentage association of acid if it forms dimer in solution?
explanation: The following are features of non-ideal solutions: Raoult's Law does not apply to non-ideal solutions . The interactions between the solute and the solvent (B–B) and the solvent and the solute (A–A) are not the same as the solvent-solute (A–B) interaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H mix {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 n = 2 (dimer) {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = i K f m i = 1.62 {tex}\times{/tex} 122 {tex}\times{/tex} 25/4.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 i = 0.504 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2(1 - i) {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2(1 - 0.504) = 0.992 = 99.2%
solution: The following are features of non-ideal solutions: Raoult's Law does not apply to non-ideal solutions . The interactions between the solute and the solvent (B–B) and the solvent and the solute (A–A) are not the same as the solvent-solute (A–B) interaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H mix {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 n = 2 (dimer) {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = i K f m i = 1.62 {tex}\times{/tex} 122 {tex}\times{/tex} 25/4.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 i = 0.504 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2(1 - i) {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2(1 - 0.504) = 0.992 = 99.2%
solution_html:
1. The following are features of non-ideal solutions:
     Raoult's Law does not apply to non-ideal solutions. The interactions between the solute and the solvent (B–B) and the solvent and the solute (A–A) are not the same as the solvent-solute (A–B) interaction, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hmix{tex}\neq{/tex}0
  2. n = 2 (dimer)
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = i Kfm
    i = 1.62 {tex}\times{/tex} 122 {tex}\times{/tex} 25/4.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000
    i = 0.504
    {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2(1 - i)
    {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2(1 - 0.504)
    = 0.992
    = 99.2%

question_html: Calculate the freezing point of a solution containing 60 g of glucose (Molar mass = 180 g mol-1) in 250 g of water. (Kf of water = 1.86 K kg mol-1)
question_text: Calculate the freezing point of a solution containing 60 g of glucose (Molar mass = 180 g mol -1 ) in 250 g of water. (K f of water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: {tex}\Delta T_f=K_f m{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{K}_f \times \frac{w_2 \times 1000}{M_2 \times w_1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1.86 \times 60 \times 1000}{180 \times 250}{/tex} = 2.48 K {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_f=\mathrm{T}_f^o-\mathrm{T}_f{/tex} 2.48 = 273.15 - T f T f = 273.15 - 2.48 = 270.67 K
solution: {tex}\Delta T_f=K_f m{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{K}_f \times \frac{w_2 \times 1000}{M_2 \times w_1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1.86 \times 60 \times 1000}{180 \times 250}{/tex} = 2.48 K {tex}\Delta \mathrm{T}_f=\mathrm{T}_f^o-\mathrm{T}_f{/tex} 2.48 = 273.15 - T f T f = 273.15 - 2.48 = 270.67 K

= 2.48 K

Tf = 273.15 - 2.48 = 270.67 K

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The vapour pressure of pure liquids A and B are 450 and 700 mm Hg respectively, at 350 K. Find out the composition of the liquid mixture if total vapour pressure is 600 mm Hg. Also find the composition of the vapour phase.
question_text: The vapour pressure of pure liquids A and B are 450 and 700 mm Hg respectively, at 350 K. Find out the composition of the liquid mixture if total vapour pressure is 600 mm Hg. Also find the composition of the vapour phase.
explanation: It is given that: {tex}P_A^0{/tex} = 450 mm of Hg {tex}P_B^0{/tex} = 700 mm of Hg P total = 600 mm of Hg From Raoult's law, we have: {tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex} {tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B} = p_B^0(1 - {x_A}){/tex} Therefore, total pressure, {tex}{p_{total}} = {p_A} + {p_B}{/tex} {tex}{p_{total}} = p_A^0{x_A} + p_B^0(1 - {x_A}){/tex} {tex}{p_{total}} = p_A^0{x_A} + p_B^0 - p_B^0{x_A}{/tex} {tex}{p_{total}} = \left( {p_A^0 - p_B^0} \right){x_A} + p_B^0{/tex} {tex}600 = (450 - 700) {x_A} + 700{/tex} {tex} - 100 = - 250{x_A}{/tex} {tex}{x_A} = 0.4{/tex} Therefore, {tex}{x_B} = 1 - {x_A}{/tex} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6 Now, {tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex} {tex}= 450 \times 0.4{/tex} = 180 mm of Hg {tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B}{/tex} {tex} = 700 \times 0.6{/tex} = 420 mm of Hg Now, in the vapour phase: Mole fraction of liquid {tex}A = \frac{{{p_A}}}{{{p_A} + {p_B}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{180}}{{180 + 420}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{180}}{{600}}{/tex} = 0.30 And, mole fraction of liquid B = 1 - 0.30
solution: It is given that: {tex}P_A^0{/tex} = 450 mm of Hg {tex}P_B^0{/tex} = 700 mm of Hg P total = 600 mm of Hg From Raoult's law, we have: {tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex} {tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B} = p_B^0(1 - {x_A}){/tex} Therefore, total pressure, {tex}{p_{total}} = {p_A} + {p_B}{/tex} {tex}{p_{total}} = p_A^0{x_A} + p_B^0(1 - {x_A}){/tex} {tex}{p_{total}} = p_A^0{x_A} + p_B^0 - p_B^0{x_A}{/tex} {tex}{p_{total}} = \left( {p_A^0 - p_B^0} \right){x_A} + p_B^0{/tex} {tex}600 = (450 - 700) {x_A} + 700{/tex} {tex} - 100 = - 250{x_A}{/tex} {tex}{x_A} = 0.4{/tex} Therefore, {tex}{x_B} = 1 - {x_A}{/tex} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6 Now, {tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex} {tex}= 450 \times 0.4{/tex} = 180 mm of Hg {tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B}{/tex} {tex} = 700 \times 0.6{/tex} = 420 mm of Hg Now, in the vapour phase: Mole fraction of liquid {tex}A = \frac{{{p_A}}}{{{p_A} + {p_B}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{180}}{{180 + 420}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{180}}{{600}}{/tex} = 0.30 And, mole fraction of liquid B = 1 - 0.30
solution_html:
It is given that:
{tex}P_A^0{/tex} = 450 mm of Hg
{tex}P_B^0{/tex} = 700 mm of Hg
Ptotal = 600 mm of Hg
From Raoult's law, we have:
{tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex}
{tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B} = p_B^0(1 - {x_A}){/tex}
Therefore, total pressure, {tex}{p_{total}} = {p_A} + {p_B}{/tex}
{tex}{p_{total}} = p_A^0{x_A} + p_B^0(1 - {x_A}){/tex}
{tex}{p_{total}} = p_A^0{x_A} + p_B^0 - p_B^0{x_A}{/tex}
{tex}{p_{total}} = \left( {p_A^0 - p_B^0} \right){x_A} + p_B^0{/tex}
{tex}600 = (450 - 700) {x_A} + 700{/tex}
{tex} - 100 = - 250{x_A}{/tex}
{tex}{x_A} = 0.4{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}{x_B} = 1 - {x_A}{/tex}
= 1 - 0.4 = 0.6
Now, {tex}{p_A} = p_A^0{x_A}{/tex}
{tex}= 450 \times 0.4{/tex}
= 180 mm of Hg
{tex}{p_B} = p_B^0{x_B}{/tex}
{tex} = 700 \times 0.6{/tex}
= 420 mm of Hg
Now, in the vapour phase:
Mole fraction of liquid {tex}A = \frac{{{p_A}}}{{{p_A} + {p_B}}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{180}}{{180 + 420}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{180}}{{600}}{/tex} = 0.30
And, mole fraction of liquid B = 1 - 0.30

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
What is meant by:

  1. Colligative properties?
  2. Molality of a solution?
question_text: What is meant by: Colligative properties? Molality of a solution?
explanation: Colligative properties: The properties of solution which depend upon the number of solute particles and not upon the nature of the solute particles are known as colligative properties. There are four colligative properties: Relative lowering of vapour pressure. Elevation of boiling point Depression of freezing point Osmotic pressure. Molality (m) : It is the number of moles of solute dissolved per kilograms (kg) of the solvent. It is denoted by m. Molality (m) {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Mole of solute}}}}{{{\text{Mass of solvent in Kg}}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \;m = \frac{{{W_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {W_1}}}{/tex} Where W 1 = Mass of solvent W 2 = Mass of solute M 2 = Molar mass of solute
solution: Colligative properties: The properties of solution which depend upon the number of solute particles and not upon the nature of the solute particles are known as colligative properties. There are four colligative properties: Relative lowering of vapour pressure. Elevation of boiling point Depression of freezing point Osmotic pressure. Molality (m) : It is the number of moles of solute dissolved per kilograms (kg) of the solvent. It is denoted by m. Molality (m) {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Mole of solute}}}}{{{\text{Mass of solvent in Kg}}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \;m = \frac{{{W_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {W_1}}}{/tex} Where W 1 = Mass of solvent W 2 = Mass of solute M 2 = Molar mass of solute
solution_html:
1. Colligative properties: The properties of solution which depend upon the number of solute particles and not upon the nature of the solute particles are known as colligative properties.
    There are four colligative properties:
    1. Relative lowering of vapour pressure.
    2. Elevation of boiling point
    3. Depression of freezing point
    4. Osmotic pressure.
  2. Molality (m) : It is the number of moles of solute dissolved per kilograms (kg) of the solvent. It is denoted by m.
    Molality (m) {tex} = \frac{{{\text{Mole of solute}}}}{{{\text{Mass of solvent in Kg}}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \;m = \frac{{{W_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {W_1}}}{/tex}
    Where W1 = Mass of solvent
    W2 = Mass of solute
    M2 = Molar mass of solute

explanation: Ideal soultion: The solution which obeys Raoult’s Law over the entire range of concentration is called ideal solution. For such solutions, {tex}\triangle{/tex} H mixing = 0 and {tex}\triangle{/tex} V mixing = 0 Molarity: Molarity is defined as the number of moles of solute dissolved in one litre of the solution. Molarity (M) = {tex}\frac{{{\text{No. }{\text {of}}\;{\text{Moles}}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{solute}}}}{{{\text{Volume}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{solution}}\,{\text{in}}\,{\text{litre}}}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Define the following terms:

  1. Abnormal Molar Mass
  2. Van't Hoff Factor
question_text: Define the following terms: Abnormal Molar Mass Van't Hoff Factor
explanation: Abnormal Molar Mass: A substance is said to have an abnormal molecular/molar mass when the molecular mass of a substance that is determined by measuring any of the colligative properties is different from the theoretically expected value. The abnormality in the molecular mass of the solute particles occurs due to either of the following: (a) Association of solute particles or (b) Dissociation of solute particles. e.g. all the molecules of ethanoic acid associate in benzene, then T b or T f for ethanoic acid will be half of the normal value. Van't Hoff Factor: It is defined as the ratio of the experimental value of colligative property to the calculated value of the colligative property and is used to find out the extent of dissociation or association. Mathematically, It is represented as i = {tex}{\text{}}\,{\text{ }}\frac{{{\text{Observed}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{{{\text{Calculated}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{/tex}
solution: Abnormal Molar Mass: A substance is said to have an abnormal molecular/molar mass when the molecular mass of a substance that is determined by measuring any of the colligative properties is different from the theoretically expected value. The abnormality in the molecular mass of the solute particles occurs due to either of the following: (a) Association of solute particles or (b) Dissociation of solute particles. e.g. all the molecules of ethanoic acid associate in benzene, then T b or T f for ethanoic acid will be half of the normal value. Van't Hoff Factor: It is defined as the ratio of the experimental value of colligative property to the calculated value of the colligative property and is used to find out the extent of dissociation or association. Mathematically, It is represented as i = {tex}{\text{}}\,{\text{ }}\frac{{{\text{Observed}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{{{\text{Calculated}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Abnormal Molar Mass: A substance is said to have an abnormal molecular/molar mass when the molecular mass of a substance that is determined by measuring any of the colligative properties is different from the theoretically expected value.
    The abnormality in the molecular mass of the solute particles occurs due to either of the following: (a) Association of solute particles or (b) Dissociation of solute particles. e.g. all the molecules of ethanoic acid associate in benzene, then Tb or Tf for ethanoic acid will be half of the normal value.
  2. Van't Hoff Factor:  It is defined as the ratio of the experimental value of colligative property to the calculated value of the colligative property and is used to find out the extent of dissociation or association. Mathematically, It is represented as
    i = {tex}{\text{}}\,{\text{ }}\frac{{{\text{Observed}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{{{\text{Calculated}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A 10% solution (by mass) of sucrose in water has a freezing point of 269.15 K. Calculate the freezing point of 10% glucose in water, if freezing point of pure water is 273.15 K.
question_text: A 10% solution (by mass) of sucrose in water has a freezing point of 269.15 K. Calculate the freezing point of 10% glucose in water, if freezing point of pure water is 273.15 K.
explanation: For sucrose solution, 10%solution by mass means 10 g of sucrose dissolved in 90g of water. The molality of sucrose solution = {tex}\frac{{{W_2}\, \times 1000}}{{{M_2}\, \times {W_1}}}{/tex} Given, W 2 = 10 g, W 1 = 90 g, M 2 = 342g mol -1 Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{342}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{{1000}}{{90}}{/tex} = 0.324 mol kg -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f for sucrose solution = 273.15 K - 269.15 K = 4K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = Kfm K f = {tex}\frac{4}{{0.324}}{/tex} K kg mol -1 For glucose solution W 2 = 10 g, W 1 = 90 g, M 2 = 180 mol -1 Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{180}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{90}}{/tex} = 0.617 mol kg -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = K f m {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f (glucose) = {tex}\frac{4}{{0.324}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.617 = 7.617 K Hence, freezing point of glucose solution = 273.15 K - 7.617 K = 265.53 K
solution: For sucrose solution, 10%solution by mass means 10 g of sucrose dissolved in 90g of water. The molality of sucrose solution = {tex}\frac{{{W_2}\, \times 1000}}{{{M_2}\, \times {W_1}}}{/tex} Given, W 2 = 10 g, W 1 = 90 g, M 2 = 342g mol -1 Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{342}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{{1000}}{{90}}{/tex} = 0.324 mol kg -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f for sucrose solution = 273.15 K - 269.15 K = 4K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = Kfm K f = {tex}\frac{4}{{0.324}}{/tex} K kg mol -1 For glucose solution W 2 = 10 g, W 1 = 90 g, M 2 = 180 mol -1 Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{180}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{90}}{/tex} = 0.617 mol kg -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = K f m {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f (glucose) = {tex}\frac{4}{{0.324}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.617 = 7.617 K Hence, freezing point of glucose solution = 273.15 K - 7.617 K = 265.53 K
solution_html:
For sucrose solution,
10%solution by mass means 10 g of sucrose dissolved in 90g of water.
The molality of sucrose solution = {tex}\frac{{{W_2}\, \times 1000}}{{{M_2}\, \times {W_1}}}{/tex}
Given, W2 = 10 g, W1 = 90 g, M2 = 342g mol-1
Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{342}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{{1000}}{{90}}{/tex} = 0.324 mol kg-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf for sucrose solution = 273.15 K - 269.15 K
= 4K
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = Kfm
Kf = {tex}\frac{4}{{0.324}}{/tex} K kg mol-1
For glucose solution
W2 = 10 g, W1 = 90 g, M2 = 180 mol-1
Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{{10}}{{180}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{90}}{/tex} = 0.617 mol kg-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = Kfm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf (glucose) = {tex}\frac{4}{{0.324}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.617 = 7.617 K
Hence, freezing point of glucose solution
= 273.15 K - 7.617 K = 265.53 K

question_text: A 10% solution (by mass) of sucrose in water has a freezing point of 269.15 K. Calculate the freezing point of 10% glucose in water if the freezing point of pure water is 273.15 K. (Given, the molar mass of sucrose = 342 g mol -1 and molar mass of glucose = 180 g mol -1 ) Define the following terms: Molality (m) Abnormal molar mass

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Calculate the boiling point of the solution when 4 g of MgSO4 (M = 120 g mol-1) was dissolved in 100 g of water assuming MgSO4 undergoes complete ionization. (Kb for water = 0. 52 K kg mol-1)
question_text: Calculate the boiling point of the solution when 4 g of MgSO 4 (M = 120 g mol -1 ) was dissolved in 100 g of water assuming MgSO 4 undergoes complete ionization. (Kb for water = 0. 52 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: Given, the weight of solute, W 2 = 4 g weight of solvent, W 1 = 100g i = 2 (as MgSO 4 associates completely into 2 ions) MgSO 4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Mg 2+ + SO {tex}_4^{2 - }{/tex} K b = 052 k kg mol -1 M 2 = 120 g mol -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} The elevation in boiling point of solution {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{{1 \times \,{K_b} \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{M_2}\, \times \,{W_1}}}{/tex} where, i = van't Hoff factor W 2 = weight of solute W 1 = weight of solvent M 2 = molar mass of solute {tex}T_{b}^{o}{/tex} = boiling point of pure solvent {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.52 \times 4 \times 1000}{120 \times 100}{/tex} = 0.346 K Boiling point of pure water is 100°Cor 373 K. T b = {tex}\Delta{/tex} Tb + {tex}T_{b}^{o}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} T b = 373 + 0.346 = 373.346 K
solution: Given, the weight of solute, W 2 = 4 g weight of solvent, W 1 = 100g i = 2 (as MgSO 4 associates completely into 2 ions) MgSO 4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Mg 2+ + SO {tex}_4^{2 - }{/tex} K b = 052 k kg mol -1 M 2 = 120 g mol -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} The elevation in boiling point of solution {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{{1 \times \,{K_b} \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{M_2}\, \times \,{W_1}}}{/tex} where, i = van't Hoff factor W 2 = weight of solute W 1 = weight of solvent M 2 = molar mass of solute {tex}T_{b}^{o}{/tex} = boiling point of pure solvent {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.52 \times 4 \times 1000}{120 \times 100}{/tex} = 0.346 K Boiling point of pure water is 100°Cor 373 K. T b = {tex}\Delta{/tex} Tb + {tex}T_{b}^{o}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} T b = 373 + 0.346 = 373.346 K
solution_html:
Given, the weight of solute, W2 = 4 g
weight of solvent, W1 = 100g
i = 2 (as MgSO4 associates completely into 2 ions)
MgSO4 {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Mg2+ + SO{tex}_4^{2 - }{/tex}
Kb = 052 k kg mol-1
M2 = 120 g mol-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The elevation in boiling point of solution
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = {tex}\frac{{1 \times \,{K_b} \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{M_2}\, \times \,{W_1}}}{/tex}
where,
i = van't Hoff factor
W2 = weight of solute
W1 = weight of solvent
M2 = molar mass of solute
{tex}T_{b}^{o}{/tex} = boiling point of pure solvent
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.52 \times 4 \times 1000}{120 \times 100}{/tex} = 0.346 K
Boiling point of pure water is 100°Cor 373 K.
Tb = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb + {tex}T_{b}^{o}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Tb = 373 + 0.346
= 373.346 K

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Calculate the freezing point of the solution when 1.9 g of MgCI2 (M = 95 g mol-1) was dissolved in 50 g of water, assuming MgCI2 undergoes complete ionization.
    (Kf for water = 1.86 K kg mol-1)
    1. Out of 1 M glucose and 2 M glucose, which one has a higher boiling point and why?
    2. What happens when the external pressure applied becomes more than the osmotic pressure of the solution?
question_text: Calculate the freezing point of the solution when 1.9 g of MgCI 2 (M = 95 g mol -1 ) was dissolved in 50 g of water, assuming MgCI 2 undergoes complete ionization. (K f for water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 ) Out of 1 M glucose and 2 M glucose, which one has a higher boiling point and why? What happens when the external pressure applied becomes more than the osmotic pressure of the solution?
explanation: MgCI 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Mg 2+ + 2 Cl - 1 mol of MgCI 2 gives 3 moles of particles. {tex}\therefore{/tex} i = 3 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = iK f m Given, W a = Weight of H 2 O (solvent) = 50 g Wb = Weight of MgCI 2 (solute) = 1.9 g T {tex}_f^o{/tex} = 273.15 K K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 M b = Molar mass of solute = 95 mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{i K_{f} \times 1000 \times W_{b}}{M_{b} \times W_{a}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 1.86 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000 \times 1.9 \ \mathrm{g}}{95 \mathrm{g} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 50 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 2.232 K Also, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T {tex}_f^o{/tex} - T f ; T f = T {tex}_f^o{/tex} - {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 273.15 - 2.232 = 270.918 K 2 M glucose has a higher boiling point because more the concentration, more is the elevation in boiling point. When the external pressure applied becomes more than the osmotic pressure of the solution, reverse osmosis takes place.
solution: MgCI 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Mg 2+ + 2 Cl - 1 mol of MgCI 2 gives 3 moles of particles. {tex}\therefore{/tex} i = 3 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = iK f m Given, W a = Weight of H 2 O (solvent) = 50 g Wb = Weight of MgCI 2 (solute) = 1.9 g T {tex}_f^o{/tex} = 273.15 K K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 M b = Molar mass of solute = 95 mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{i K_{f} \times 1000 \times W_{b}}{M_{b} \times W_{a}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 1.86 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000 \times 1.9 \ \mathrm{g}}{95 \mathrm{g} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 50 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 2.232 K Also, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T {tex}_f^o{/tex} - T f ; T f = T {tex}_f^o{/tex} - {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 273.15 - 2.232 = 270.918 K 2 M glucose has a higher boiling point because more the concentration, more is the elevation in boiling point. When the external pressure applied becomes more than the osmotic pressure of the solution, reverse osmosis takes place.
solution_html:
1. MgCI2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Mg2+ + 2 Cl-
    1 mol of MgCI2 gives 3 moles of particles.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} i = 3
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = iKfm
    Given, Wa = Weight of H2O (solvent) = 50 g
    Wb = Weight of MgCI2 (solute) = 1.9 g
    T{tex}_f^o{/tex} = 273.15 K
    Kf = 1.86 K kg mol-1
    Mb = Molar mass of solute = 95 mol-1
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{i K_{f} \times 1000 \times W_{b}}{M_{b} \times W_{a}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{3 \times 1.86 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000 \times 1.9 \ \mathrm{g}}{95 \mathrm{g} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 50 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 2.232 K
    Also, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = T{tex}_f^o{/tex} - Tf; Tf = T{tex}_f^o{/tex} - {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf
    = 273.15 - 2.232 = 270.918 K
    1. 2 M glucose has a higher boiling point because more the concentration, more is the elevation in boiling point.
    2. When the external pressure applied becomes more than the osmotic pressure of the solution, reverse osmosis takes place.

question_html:

    (Kf for CS2 = 3.83 K kg mol−1
    The atomic mass of sulphur = 32 g mol-1)

    2. 0.4% sodium chloride solution?
question_text: When 2.56 g of sulphur was dissolved in 100 g of CS 2 , the freezing point lowered by 0.383 K. Calculate the formula of sulphur (S x ) (K f for CS 2 = 3.83 K kg mol −1 The atomic mass of sulphur = 32 g mol -1 ) Blood cells are isotonic with 0.9% sodium chloride solution. What happens if we place blood cells in a solution containing 1.2% sodium chloride solution? 0.4% sodium chloride solution?
explanation: Given, Weight of solvent (W a )= 100 g Weight of solute (W b )= 2.56 g {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.383 K K f = 3.83 K kg mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = K f m {tex}\Delta{/tex} T = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times 1000 \times W_{b}}{M_{b} \times W_{a}}{/tex} 0.383 k = {tex}\frac{3.83 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000 \times 2.56 \mathrm{g}}{M_{b} \times 100 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} Mb = {tex}\frac{3.83 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000 \times 2.56 \mathrm{g}}{0.383 \mathrm{K} \times 100 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} M = 256 g/mol Molecular mass = n {tex}\times{/tex} Atomic mass n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { Atomic mass }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{256}{32}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Formula sulphur is S 8 If we place the blood cells in a solution containing more than 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride solution, water will flow out of the cells and they would shrink This process is called plasmolysis. If the salt concentration is less than 0.9% (mass/volume), then the water will flow into the cells and they would swell.
solution: Given, Weight of solvent (W a )= 100 g Weight of solute (W b )= 2.56 g {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.383 K K f = 3.83 K kg mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = K f m {tex}\Delta{/tex} T = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times 1000 \times W_{b}}{M_{b} \times W_{a}}{/tex} 0.383 k = {tex}\frac{3.83 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000 \times 2.56 \mathrm{g}}{M_{b} \times 100 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} Mb = {tex}\frac{3.83 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000 \times 2.56 \mathrm{g}}{0.383 \mathrm{K} \times 100 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} M = 256 g/mol Molecular mass = n {tex}\times{/tex} Atomic mass n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { Atomic mass }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{256}{32}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Formula sulphur is S 8 If we place the blood cells in a solution containing more than 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride solution, water will flow out of the cells and they would shrink This process is called plasmolysis. If the salt concentration is less than 0.9% (mass/volume), then the water will flow into the cells and they would swell.
solution_html:
1. Given, Weight of solvent (Wa)= 100 g
    Weight of solute (Wb)= 2.56 g
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 0.383 K
    Kf = 3.83 K kg mol-1
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = Kfm
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}T  = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times 1000 \times W_{b}}{M_{b} \times W_{a}}{/tex}
    0.383 k = {tex}\frac{3.83 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000 \times 2.56 \mathrm{g}}{M_{b} \times 100 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
    Mb = {tex}\frac{3.83 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000 \times 2.56 \mathrm{g}}{0.383 \mathrm{K} \times 100 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
    M = 256 g/mol
    Molecular mass = n {tex}\times{/tex} Atomic mass
    n = {tex}\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { Atomic mass }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{256}{32}{/tex} = 8
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Formula sulphur is S8
    1. If we place the blood cells in a solution containing more than 0.9% (mass/volume) sodium chloride solution, water will flow out of the cells and they would shrink This process is called plasmolysis.
    2. If the salt concentration is less than 0.9% (mass/volume), then the water will flow into the cells and they would swell.

question_html:
45 g of ethylene glycol (C2H6O2) is mixed with 600 g of water. Calculate

  1. the freezing point depression and
  2. the freezing point of the solution.
question_text: 45 g of ethylene glycol (C 2 H 6 O 2 ) is mixed with 600 g of water. Calculate the freezing point depression and the freezing point of the solution.
explanation: Given, W 1 = 600 g, K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 W 2 = 45 g, M 2 = 62.0 g mol -1 T {tex}_f^o{/tex} (water) = 273 k {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.86 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 45 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{62 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 600 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 2.25 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T {tex}_f^o{/tex} - T f T f = 273 K - 2.25 K = 270.75 K
solution: Given, W 1 = 600 g, K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 W 2 = 45 g, M 2 = 62.0 g mol -1 T {tex}_f^o{/tex} (water) = 273 k {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.86 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 45 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{62 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 600 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 2.25 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T {tex}_f^o{/tex} - T f T f = 273 K - 2.25 K = 270.75 K
solution_html:
Given, W1 = 600 g, Kf = 1.86 K kg mol-1
W2 = 45 g, M2 = 62.0 g mol-1
T{tex}_f^o{/tex} (water) = 273 k

  1. {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1.86 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 45 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{62 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 600 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 2.25 K
  2. {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = T{tex}_f^o{/tex} - Tf
    Tf = 273 K - 2.25 K = 270.75 K

question_html: A 5% solution (by mass) of cane sugar ({tex}M \cdot W{/tex}342) is isotonic with 0.877% solution of substance X. Find the molecular weight of X.
question_text: A 5% solution (by mass) of cane sugar ( {tex}M \cdot W{/tex} 342) is isotonic with 0.877% solution of substance X. Find the molecular weight of X.

solution: Given, W(cane sugar) = 5 g W(X) = 0.877 g M(cane sugar)= 342 g mol -1 {tex}\pi{/tex} (cane sugar) = {tex}\pi{/tex} (X) [ {tex}\because{/tex} solution is isotonic] {tex}\frac{W(\text { cane sugar }) \times 1000}{M(\text { cane sugar }) \times V}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{W(X) \times 1000}{M(X) \times V}{/tex} {tex}\frac{5 \mathrm \ {g}}{342 \mathrm \ {g} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.877 \mathrm{g}}{M(X)}{/tex} m(x) = 59.9 g mol -1
solution_html:
Given, W(cane sugar) = 5 g
W(X) = 0.877 g
M(cane sugar)= 342 g mol-1
{tex}\pi{/tex} (cane sugar) = {tex}\pi{/tex} (X) [{tex}\because{/tex} solution is isotonic]
{tex}\frac{W(\text { cane sugar }) \times 1000}{M(\text { cane sugar }) \times V}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{W(X) \times 1000}{M(X) \times V}{/tex}

question_html: 3.9 g of benzoic acid dissolved in 49 g of benzene shows a depression in freezing point of 1.62 K. Calculate the van't Hoff factor and predict the nature of solute (associated or dissociated). (Given: Molar mass of benzoic acid = 122 g mol-1, Kf for benzene = 4.9 K kg mol-1)
question_text: 3.9 g of benzoic acid dissolved in 49 g of benzene shows a depression in freezing point of 1.62 K. Calculate the van't Hoff factor and predict the nature of solute (associated or dissociated). (Given: Molar mass of benzoic acid = 122 g mol -1 , K f for benzene = 4.9 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: Given, W 2 = 39g, W 1 = 49 g; ΔT f = 1.62K M 2 (benozoic acid) = 122 g mol -1 K f (benzene) = 4.9 K kg mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{\ i \times K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f} \times M_{2} \times W_{1}}{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{1.62 \mathrm{K} \times 122 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 49 \mathrm{g}}{4.9 \mathrm \ {K} \mathrm \ {kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 3.9 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{/tex} i = 0.50 Since, i < 1, therefore solute (benzoic acid) undergoes association.
solution: Given, W 2 = 39g, W 1 = 49 g; ΔT f = 1.62K M 2 (benozoic acid) = 122 g mol -1 K f (benzene) = 4.9 K kg mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{\ i \times K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f} \times M_{2} \times W_{1}}{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{1.62 \mathrm{K} \times 122 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 49 \mathrm{g}}{4.9 \mathrm \ {K} \mathrm \ {kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 3.9 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{/tex} i = 0.50 Since, i < 1, therefore solute (benzoic acid) undergoes association.
solution_html:
Given, W2 = 39g, W1 = 49 g; ΔTf = 1.62K
M2 (benozoic acid) = 122 g mol-1
Kf (benzene) = 4.9 K kg mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{\ i \times K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
i = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f} \times M_{2} \times W_{1}}{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{/tex}
i = {tex}\frac{1.62 \mathrm{K} \times 122 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 49 \mathrm{g}}{4.9 \mathrm \ {K} \mathrm \ {kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 3.9 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{/tex}
i = 0.50
Since, i < 1, therefore solute (benzoic acid) undergoes association.

question_html: Define the osmotic pressure of a solution. How is the osmotic pressure related to the concentration of a solute in a solution?
question_text: Define the osmotic pressure of a solution. How is the osmotic pressure related to the concentration of a solute in a solution?
explanation: Osmotic pressure is the extra pressure which is applied on the solution just to prevent the flow of solvent into the solution through a semipermeable membrane. Osmotic pressure ( {tex}\pi{/tex} ) is related to the concentration (C) of a solute as: {tex}\pi{/tex} = CRT, {tex}\pi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2}{V}{/tex} RT = {tex}\frac{W_2RT}{M_2V}{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} V = W 2 {tex}\frac{RT}{M_2}{/tex} ; M 2 = {tex}\frac{W_2RT}{\pi V}{/tex}
solution: Osmotic pressure is the extra pressure which is applied on the solution just to prevent the flow of solvent into the solution through a semipermeable membrane. Osmotic pressure ( {tex}\pi{/tex} ) is related to the concentration (C) of a solute as: {tex}\pi{/tex} = CRT, {tex}\pi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2}{V}{/tex} RT = {tex}\frac{W_2RT}{M_2V}{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} V = W 2 {tex}\frac{RT}{M_2}{/tex} ; M 2 = {tex}\frac{W_2RT}{\pi V}{/tex}
solution_html:
Osmotic pressure is the extra pressure which is applied on the solution just to prevent the flow of solvent into the solution through a semipermeable membrane.
Osmotic pressure ({tex}\pi{/tex}) is related to the concentration (C) of a solute as:
{tex}\pi{/tex} = CRT, {tex}\pi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2}{V}{/tex} RT = {tex}\frac{W_2RT}{M_2V}{/tex}
{tex}\pi{/tex}V = W2 {tex}\frac{RT}{M_2}{/tex}; M2 = {tex}\frac{W_2RT}{\pi V}{/tex}
