question_html: Calculate the mass of NaCl (molar mass= 58.5 g mol-1) to be dissolved in 37.2 g of water to lower the freezing point by 2°C, assuming that NaCl undergoes complete dissociation. (Kf for water = 1.86 K kg mol-1)
question_text: Calculate the mass of NaCl (molar mass= 58.5 g mol -1 ) to be dissolved in 37.2 g of water to lower the freezing point by 2°C, assuming that NaCl undergoes complete dissociation. (K f for water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: Given, W 1 = 37.2 g, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 2 o C = 2K [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T f - T {tex}_f^o{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0 o C = 2 o C] K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 , i = 2 [for complete dissociation] {tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{i K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} W 2 = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f} \times M_{2} \times W_{1}}{i \times K_{f} \times 1000}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{K} \times 58.5 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 37.2 \mathrm{g}}{2 \times 1.86 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000}{/tex} W 2 = 1.17 g
solution: Given, W 1 = 37.2 g, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 2 o C = 2K [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T f - T {tex}_f^o{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0 o C = 2 o C] K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 , i = 2 [for complete dissociation] {tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{i K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} W 2 = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f} \times M_{2} \times W_{1}}{i \times K_{f} \times 1000}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{K} \times 58.5 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 37.2 \mathrm{g}}{2 \times 1.86 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000}{/tex} W 2 = 1.17 g
solution_html:
Given, W1 = 37.2 g, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 2oC = 2K [{tex}\because{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = Tf - T{tex}_f^o{/tex}  {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 0oC = 2oC]
Kf = 1.86 K kg mol-1, i = 2 [for complete dissociation]
{tex}\Delta T_{f}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{i K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
W2 = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f} \times M_{2} \times W_{1}}{i \times K_{f} \times 1000}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{K} \times 58.5 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 37.2 \mathrm{g}}{2 \times 1.86 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000}{/tex}
W2 = 1.17 g

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Why does a solution containing non-volatile solute have a higher boiling point than the pure solvent? Why is the elevation of boiling point a colligative property?
question_text: Why does a solution containing non-volatile solute have a higher boiling point than the pure solvent? Why is the elevation of boiling point a colligative property?
explanation: The boiling point of the solution containing a non-volatile solute is always higher than that of the pure solvent because the vapour pressure of such a solution is lower than that of the pure solvent and vapour pressure increases with increase in temperature. Hence, the solution has to be heated more to make the vapour pressure equal to the atmospheric pressure. Elevation of boiling point is a colligative property because it depends upon the number of solute particles present in a solution.
solution: The boiling point of the solution containing a non-volatile solute is always higher than that of the pure solvent because the vapour pressure of such a solution is lower than that of the pure solvent and vapour pressure increases with increase in temperature. Hence, the solution has to be heated more to make the vapour pressure equal to the atmospheric pressure. Elevation of boiling point is a colligative property because it depends upon the number of solute particles present in a solution.
solution_html:
The boiling point of the solution containing a non-volatile solute is always higher than that of the pure solvent because the vapour pressure of such a solution is lower than that of the pure solvent and vapour pressure increases with increase in temperature. Hence, the solution has to be heated more to make the vapour pressure equal to the atmospheric pressure.
Elevation of boiling point is a colligative property because it depends upon the number of solute particles present in a solution.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. Gas (A) is more soluble in water than gas (B) at the same temperature. Which one of the two gases will have a higher value of KH (Henry's constant) and why?
  2. In non-ideal solution, what type of deviation shows the formation of maximum boiling azeotropes?
question_text: Gas (A) is more soluble in water than gas (B) at the same temperature. Which one of the two gases will have a higher value of KH (Henry's constant) and why? In non-ideal solution, what type of deviation shows the formation of maximum boiling azeotropes?
explanation: Solubility is inversely proportional to K H , i.e. Henry's constant of the gas. Greater the value of K H , lower is the solubility of the gas. As gas (A) is more soluble in water than gas (B) at the same temperature, hence the gas (A) has a lower value of K H . In other words, gas (B) has a higher value of K H than gas (A) at the same temperature. In non-ideal solutions, the solutions that show large negative deviation from Raoult's law form maximum boiling azeotropes. e.g. Mixture of nitric acid and water.
solution: Solubility is inversely proportional to K H , i.e. Henry's constant of the gas. Greater the value of K H , lower is the solubility of the gas. As gas (A) is more soluble in water than gas (B) at the same temperature, hence the gas (A) has a lower value of K H . In other words, gas (B) has a higher value of K H than gas (A) at the same temperature. In non-ideal solutions, the solutions that show large negative deviation from Raoult's law form maximum boiling azeotropes. e.g. Mixture of nitric acid and water.
solution_html:
Solubility is inversely proportional to KH, i.e. Henry's constant of the gas.

  1. Greater the value of KH, lower is the solubility of the gas. As gas (A) is more soluble in water than gas (B) at the same temperature, hence the gas (A) has a lower value of KH. In other words, gas (B) has a higher value of KH than gas (A) at the same temperature.
  2. In non-ideal solutions, the solutions that show large negative deviation from Raoult's law form maximum boiling azeotropes. e.g. Mixture of nitric acid and water.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What is meant by negative deviation from Raoult's law? Give an example. What is the sign of {tex}\Delta{/tex}mixH for negative deviation?
question_text: What is meant by negative deviation from Raoult's law? Give an example. What is the sign of {tex}\Delta{/tex} mix H for negative deviation?
explanation: Positive deviation means A-8 interactions are weaker than A-A and 8-8 interactions while the opposite is true in case of negative deviation. For non-ideal solutions, vapour pressure is either higher or lower than that predicted by Raoult's law. If it is higher, the solution exhibits a positive deviation and if it is lower, it exhibits a negative deviation from Raoult's law. In the case of positive deviation, A-B interactions are weaker than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure increases which result in positive deviation. e g Ethanol + Acetone and CS 2 + Acetone show positive deviation. For positive deviation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H (mixing) = Positive In the case of negative deviation, A-B interactions are stronger than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure decreases which result in negative deviation. e.g. Phenol + Anime and Chloroform + Acetone show negative deviation. For negative deviation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H (mixing) = Negative Plat for non-ideal solution showing negative deviation.
solution: Positive deviation means A-8 interactions are weaker than A-A and 8-8 interactions while the opposite is true in case of negative deviation. For non-ideal solutions, vapour pressure is either higher or lower than that predicted by Raoult's law. If it is higher, the solution exhibits a positive deviation and if it is lower, it exhibits a negative deviation from Raoult's law. In the case of positive deviation, A-B interactions are weaker than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure increases which result in positive deviation. e g Ethanol + Acetone and CS 2 + Acetone show positive deviation. For positive deviation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H (mixing) = Positive In the case of negative deviation, A-B interactions are stronger than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure decreases which result in negative deviation. e.g. Phenol + Anime and Chloroform + Acetone show negative deviation. For negative deviation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H (mixing) = Negative Plat for non-ideal solution showing negative deviation.
solution_html:
Positive deviation means A-8 interactions are weaker than A-A and 8-8 interactions while the opposite is true in case of negative deviation.
For non-ideal solutions, vapour pressure is either higher or lower than that predicted by Raoult's law. If it is higher, the solution exhibits a positive deviation and if it is lower, it exhibits a negative deviation from Raoult's law.
In the case of positive deviation, A-B interactions are weaker than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure increases which result in positive deviation.
e g Ethanol + Acetone and CS2 + Acetone show positive deviation.
For positive deviation, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H (mixing) = Positive
In the case of negative deviation, A-B interactions are stronger than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure decreases which result in negative deviation. e.g. Phenol + Anime and Chloroform + Acetone show negative deviation.
For negative deviation, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H (mixing) =  Negative


Plat for non-ideal solution showing negative deviation.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
1. On mixing liquid X and liquid Y, the volume of the resulting solution decreases. What type of deviation from Raoult's law is shown by the resulting solution? What change in temperature would you observe after mixing liquids X and Y?
  2. What happens when we place the blood cell in water (hypertonic solution)? Give reason.
question_text: On mixing liquid X and liquid Y, the volume of the resulting solution decreases. What type of deviation from Raoult's law is shown by the resulting solution? What change in temperature would you observe after mixing liquids X and Y? What happens when we place the blood cell in water (hypertonic solution)? Give reason.
explanation: On mixing two liquids X and Y, the decrease in volume of the resulting solution suggests that a non-ideal solution is formed which shows negative deviation from Raoult's law. In case of the solutions showing negative deviations from Raoult's law, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H mixing (i.e enthalpy of the mixture) is negative. Thus, on mixing X and Y liquids, the evolution of heat takes place, i.e. the temperature of the resulting solution increases. When we placed the blood cell in hypertonic solution then due to osmosis, water will flow out of the cell and it would shrink.
solution: On mixing two liquids X and Y, the decrease in volume of the resulting solution suggests that a non-ideal solution is formed which shows negative deviation from Raoult's law. In case of the solutions showing negative deviations from Raoult's law, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H mixing (i.e enthalpy of the mixture) is negative. Thus, on mixing X and Y liquids, the evolution of heat takes place, i.e. the temperature of the resulting solution increases. When we placed the blood cell in hypertonic solution then due to osmosis, water will flow out of the cell and it would shrink.
solution_html:
1. On mixing two liquids X and Y, the decrease in volume of the resulting solution suggests that a non-ideal solution is formed which shows negative deviation from Raoult's law. In case of the solutions showing negative deviations from Raoult's law, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H mixing (i.e enthalpy of the mixture) is negative. Thus, on mixing X and Y liquids, the evolution of heat takes place, i.e. the temperature of the resulting solution increases.
  2. When we placed the blood cell in hypertonic solution then due to osmosis, water will flow out of the cell and it would shrink.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Differentiate between molarity and molality of a solution. How can we change the molality value of a solution into molarity value?
question_text: Differentiate between molarity and molality of a solution. How can we change the molality value of a solution into molarity value?
explanation: Molality is defined as the number of moles of the solute per kilogram of the solvent. It is represented by 'm'. Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of moles of solute } \times 1000}{\text { mass of solvent (in g) }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_B \times 1000}{w_A}{/tex} It does not change with a change in temperature. Molarity is defined as the number of moles of solute dissolved in one litre of the solution. Molarity (M) = {tex}\frac { Number \;of \;moles \;of\; solute, \;n_B } {{ Volume\; of \;solution }}{/tex} It changes with a change in temperature. Relation between molality and molarity: {tex}\frac1m=\;\frac dM-\;\frac{M_B}{1000}{/tex} where d is the density of the solution in gcm -3 , M B is the molar mass of solute We can change the molality value of a solution into molarity value by using the following relation: Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{M \times 1000}{(1000 \times d)-\left(M \times M_{B}\right)}{/tex}
solution: Molality is defined as the number of moles of the solute per kilogram of the solvent. It is represented by 'm'. Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of moles of solute } \times 1000}{\text { mass of solvent (in g) }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_B \times 1000}{w_A}{/tex} It does not change with a change in temperature. Molarity is defined as the number of moles of solute dissolved in one litre of the solution. Molarity (M) = {tex}\frac { Number \;of \;moles \;of\; solute, \;n_B } {{ Volume\; of \;solution }}{/tex} It changes with a change in temperature. Relation between molality and molarity: {tex}\frac1m=\;\frac dM-\;\frac{M_B}{1000}{/tex} where d is the density of the solution in gcm -3 , M B is the molar mass of solute We can change the molality value of a solution into molarity value by using the following relation: Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{M \times 1000}{(1000 \times d)-\left(M \times M_{B}\right)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Molality is defined as the number of moles of the solute per kilogram of the solvent. It is represented by 'm'.
Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of moles of solute } \times 1000}{\text { mass of solvent (in g) }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_B \times 1000}{w_A}{/tex}
It does not change with a change in temperature.
Molarity is defined as the number of moles of solute dissolved in one litre of the solution.
Molarity (M) = {tex}\frac { Number \;of \;moles \;of\; solute, \;n_B } {{ Volume\; of \;solution }}{/tex}
It changes with a change in temperature.
Relation between molality and molarity: {tex}\frac1m=\;\frac dM-\;\frac{M_B}{1000}{/tex}
where d is the density of the solution in gcm-3, MB is the molar mass of solute
We can change the molality value of a solution into molarity value by using the following relation:
Molality (m) = {tex}\frac{M \times 1000}{(1000 \times d)-\left(M \times M_{B}\right)}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Some liquids on mixing form "Azeotropes". What are azeotropes?
question_text: Some liquids on mixing form "Azeotropes". What are azeotropes?
explanation: Azeotropes are binary mixtures having same composition in liquid and vapour phase and boil at a constant temperature. So the solutions (liquid mixtures) which boil at constant temperature and can distill unchanged in composition are called azeotropes.
solution: Azeotropes are binary mixtures having same composition in liquid and vapour phase and boil at a constant temperature. So the solutions (liquid mixtures) which boil at constant temperature and can distill unchanged in composition are called azeotropes.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: State how the vapour pressure of a solvent is affected when a non-volatile solute is dissolved in it.
question_text: State how the vapour pressure of a solvent is affected when a non-volatile solute is dissolved in it.
explanation: The vapour pressure of a liquid depends on the escape of solvent molecules from the surface of the liquid. On addition of a non-volatile solute vapour pressure of solvent gets reduced because the surface contains solute molecule as well as solvent molecules. Consequently, the number of solvent (volatile) molecules escaping from the surface correspondingly reduced, thus the vapour pressure of solvent gets reduced.
solution: The vapour pressure of a liquid depends on the escape of solvent molecules from the surface of the liquid. On addition of a non-volatile solute vapour pressure of solvent gets reduced because the surface contains solute molecule as well as solvent molecules. Consequently, the number of solvent (volatile) molecules escaping from the surface correspondingly reduced, thus the vapour pressure of solvent gets reduced.
solution_html: The vapour pressure of a liquid depends on the escape of solvent molecules from the surface of the liquid. On addition of a non-volatile solute vapour pressure of solvent gets reduced because the surface contains solute molecule as well as solvent molecules. Consequently, the number of solvent (volatile) molecules escaping from the surface correspondingly reduced, thus the vapour pressure of solvent gets reduced.

question_text: Calculate the boiling point elevation for a solution prepared by adding 10 g of CaCI 2 to 200 g of water. (K b for water= 0.512K kg mol -1 , molar mass of CaCI 2 = 111 g mol -1 )
explanation: Mass of CaCl 2 (W 2 ) = 10 g Mass of water (W 1 ) = 200 g Molar mass of CaCl 2 (M 2 ) = 111 g mol −1 Kb = 0.512 K kg mol -1 i for CaCl 2 = 3 m = {tex}\frac{w_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{10}{111} \times \frac{1000}{200}{/tex} m = 0.450 kg mol -1 , {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = iK b {tex}\times{/tex} m = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 K kg mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.450 m = 0.6912 K
solution: Mass of CaCl 2 (W 2 ) = 10 g Mass of water (W 1 ) = 200 g Molar mass of CaCl 2 (M 2 ) = 111 g mol −1 Kb = 0.512 K kg mol -1 i for CaCl 2 = 3 m = {tex}\frac{w_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{10}{111} \times \frac{1000}{200}{/tex} m = 0.450 kg mol -1 , {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = iK b {tex}\times{/tex} m = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 K kg mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.450 m = 0.6912 K
solution_html:
Mass of CaCl2 (W2) = 10 g
Mass of water (W1) = 200 g
Molar mass of CaCl2 (M2) = 111 g mol−1 Kb = 0.512 K kg mol-1
i for CaCl2 = 3
m = {tex}\frac{w_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{10}{111} \times \frac{1000}{200}{/tex}
m = 0.450 kg mol-1, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = iKb {tex}\times{/tex} m
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 K kg mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.450 m = 0.6912 K

question_text: Calculate the mass of compound (molar mass = 256 g mol -1 ) to be dissolved in 75 g of benzene to lower its freezing point by 0.48K (K f = 5.12 K kg mol -1 ).
explanation: Given that K f = 5.12K kg mol -1 , W 1 = 75g, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.48 K, M 2 = 256 g mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times 1000}}{{{W_1}\, \times \,{M_2}}}{/tex} Or 0.48 = {tex}\frac{{512\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times 1000}}{{75\, \times \,256}}{/tex} W 1 = {tex}\frac{{0.48\, \times \,75\, \times \,256}}{{5.12\, \times \,1000}}{/tex} = 1.8g
solution: Given that K f = 5.12K kg mol -1 , W 1 = 75g, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.48 K, M 2 = 256 g mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times 1000}}{{{W_1}\, \times \,{M_2}}}{/tex} Or 0.48 = {tex}\frac{{512\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times 1000}}{{75\, \times \,256}}{/tex} W 1 = {tex}\frac{{0.48\, \times \,75\, \times \,256}}{{5.12\, \times \,1000}}{/tex} = 1.8g
solution_html:
Given that Kf = 5.12K kg mol-1, W1 = 75g,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 0.48 K, M2 = 256 g mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times 1000}}{{{W_1}\, \times \,{M_2}}}{/tex}
Or 0.48 = {tex}\frac{{512\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times 1000}}{{75\, \times \,256}}{/tex}
W1 = {tex}\frac{{0.48\, \times \,75\, \times \,256}}{{5.12\, \times \,1000}}{/tex} = 1.8g

V = {tex}\frac{m}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{100 \mathrm{g}}{1.2 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mL}^{-1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1000}{12}{/tex}mL = {tex}\frac{1}{12}{/tex}L
Molarity = {tex}\frac{w_{2}}{M_{2} \times \text { volume }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10 \times 12}{180 \times 1}{/tex}
= 0.667M

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Some ethylene glycol, HOCH2CH2OH, is added to your car's cooling system along with 5 kg of water. If the freezing point of a water-glycol solution is -15.0°C, what is the boiling point of the solution?
(Kb = 0.52 K kg mol-1 and Kf = 1.86 K kg mol-1 for water)
question_text: Some ethylene glycol, HOCH 2 CH 2 OH, is added to your car's cooling system along with 5 kg of water. If the freezing point of a water-glycol solution is -15.0°C, what is the boiling point of the solution? (Kb = 0.52 K kg mol -1 and K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 for water)
explanation: Given, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T {tex}_f^o{/tex} - T f = 0 - (-15 0 o C) = 15 o C {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 15.0 o C K f = 1.86 K / m = 1.86 o C / m Molality (m) of HOCH 2 CH 2 OH = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f}}{K_{f}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15.0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 8.06 m {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = K b {tex}\times{/tex} molality (m) of HOCH 2 CH 2 OH = 0.522 C / m {tex}\times{/tex} 8.06m = 4.19 ∘ C Boiling point of pure water is 100 o C T b = {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b + {tex}T_{b}^{o}{/tex} T b = 4.19 ∘ C + 100.00 ∘ C = 104.19 ∘ C
solution: Given, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = T {tex}_f^o{/tex} - T f = 0 - (-15 0 o C) = 15 o C {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 15.0 o C K f = 1.86 K / m = 1.86 o C / m Molality (m) of HOCH 2 CH 2 OH = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f}}{K_{f}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15.0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 8.06 m {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = K b {tex}\times{/tex} molality (m) of HOCH 2 CH 2 OH = 0.522 C / m {tex}\times{/tex} 8.06m = 4.19 ∘ C Boiling point of pure water is 100 o C T b = {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b + {tex}T_{b}^{o}{/tex} T b = 4.19 ∘ C + 100.00 ∘ C = 104.19 ∘ C
solution_html:
Given,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = T{tex}_f^o{/tex} - Tf = 0 - (-15 0oC) = 15oC
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 15.0oC
Kf = 1.86 K / m = 1.86o C / m
Molality (m) of HOCH2CH2OH = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f}}{K_{f}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{15.0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 8.06 m 
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = Kb {tex}\times{/tex} molality (m) of HOCH2CH2OH
= 0.522 C / m {tex}\times{/tex} 8.06m = 4.19∘C
Boiling point of pure water is 100oC
Tb = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb + {tex}T_{b}^{o}{/tex}
Tb = 4.19∘C + 100.00∘C = 104.19∘C

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A 5% solution (by mass) of cane sugar in water has a freezing point of 271K. Calculate the freezing point of 5% glucose in water if the freezing point of pure water is 273.15 K.
question_text: A 5% solution (by mass) of cane sugar in water has a freezing point of 271K. Calculate the freezing point of 5% glucose in water if the freezing point of pure water is 273.15 K.
explanation: Given,5% sugar solution means, w B = 5g w A = 95g,M B = 342g mol -1 Similarly, for 5% glucose solution, w B (glucose) = 5g w A = 95 g and M B (glucose) = 180 g mol -1 {tex}\Delta {T_f}{/tex} (canesugar) = 273.15K - 271K = 2.15K {tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B}{w_A}}}{/tex} {tex}2.15 = \frac{{{K_f} \times 5 \times 1000}}{{342 \times 95}}{/tex} {tex}{k_f} = \frac{{2.15 \times 342 \times 95}}{{5 \times 1000}} = 13.9707{/tex} similarly, for glucose {tex}\Delta {T_f}(glucose) = \frac{{13.9707 \times 5 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 95}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_f} = 4.085K{/tex} Freezing point of the solution = 273.15K - 4.085K = 269.065 K
solution: Given,5% sugar solution means, w B = 5g w A = 95g,M B = 342g mol -1 Similarly, for 5% glucose solution, w B (glucose) = 5g w A = 95 g and M B (glucose) = 180 g mol -1 {tex}\Delta {T_f}{/tex} (canesugar) = 273.15K - 271K = 2.15K {tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B}{w_A}}}{/tex} {tex}2.15 = \frac{{{K_f} \times 5 \times 1000}}{{342 \times 95}}{/tex} {tex}{k_f} = \frac{{2.15 \times 342 \times 95}}{{5 \times 1000}} = 13.9707{/tex} similarly, for glucose {tex}\Delta {T_f}(glucose) = \frac{{13.9707 \times 5 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 95}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_f} = 4.085K{/tex} Freezing point of the solution = 273.15K - 4.085K = 269.065 K
solution_html:
Given,5% sugar solution means, wB = 5g
wA = 95g,MB = 342g mol-1
Similarly, for 5% glucose solution, wB(glucose) = 5g
wA = 95 g and MB(glucose) = 180 g mol-1
{tex}\Delta {T_f}{/tex}(canesugar) = 273.15K - 271K = 2.15K
{tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B}{w_A}}}{/tex}
{tex}2.15 = \frac{{{K_f} \times 5 \times 1000}}{{342 \times 95}}{/tex}
{tex}{k_f} = \frac{{2.15 \times 342 \times 95}}{{5 \times 1000}} = 13.9707{/tex}
similarly, for glucose
{tex}\Delta {T_f}(glucose) = \frac{{13.9707 \times 5 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 95}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {T_f} = 4.085K{/tex}
Freezing point of the solution
= 273.15K - 4.085K = 269.065 K

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Define the term osmotic pressure.
question_text: Define the term osmotic pressure.
explanation: Osmotic pressure is the hydrostatic pressure applied on solution which just prevents the flow of solvent molecules through semipermeable membrane. It may be also defined as the excess pressure which must be applied to a solution to prevent the passage of solvent into it through a semipermeable membrane.
solution: Osmotic pressure is the hydrostatic pressure applied on solution which just prevents the flow of solvent molecules through semipermeable membrane. It may be also defined as the excess pressure which must be applied to a solution to prevent the passage of solvent into it through a semipermeable membrane.
solution_html: Osmotic pressure is the hydrostatic pressure applied on solution which just prevents the flow of solvent molecules through semipermeable membrane. It may be also defined as the excess pressure which must be applied to a solution to prevent the passage of solvent into it through a semipermeable membrane.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What is meant by reverse osmosis?
question_text: What is meant by reverse osmosis?
explanation: Reverse osmosis is the process of movement of solvent through a semipermeable membrane from the solution to the pure solvent by applying pressure greater than osmotic pressure on the solution side.
solution: Reverse osmosis is the process of movement of solvent through a semipermeable membrane from the solution to the pure solvent by applying pressure greater than osmotic pressure on the solution side.
solution_html: Reverse osmosis is the process of movement of solvent through a semipermeable membrane from the solution to the pure solvent by applying pressure greater than osmotic pressure on the solution side.

question_html: Define ideal solution.
question_text: Define ideal solution.
explanation: The solution which obeys Raoult’s law exactly over the entire range of concentration is called ideal solution. For such solution. {tex}\Delta {H_{{\rm{mix}}}} = 0{\rm{ and }}\Delta {V_{{\rm{mix}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{/tex} . Such solutions are formed by mixing the two components which are identical in molecular size, in structure and have almost identical intermolecular forces.

Such solutions are formed by mixing the two components which are identical in molecular size, in structure and have almost identical intermolecular forces.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The partial pressure of ethane over a solution containing 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 of ethane is 1 bar. If the solution contains 5.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 g of ethane, then what shall be the partial pressure of the gas?
question_text: The partial pressure of ethane over a solution containing 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 of ethane is 1 bar. If the solution contains 5.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g of ethane, then what shall be the partial pressure of the gas?
explanation: Applying the relationship m = K H p In the first case, 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g = K H {tex}\times{/tex} 1 bar K H = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g/bar In second case, 5.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g/bar {tex}\times{/tex} p P = {tex}\frac{{5.00 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}g}}{{6.56 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}g\,ba{r^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 0.7621 bar
solution: Applying the relationship m = K H p In the first case, 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g = K H {tex}\times{/tex} 1 bar K H = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g/bar In second case, 5.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 g/bar {tex}\times{/tex} p P = {tex}\frac{{5.00 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}g}}{{6.56 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}g\,ba{r^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 0.7621 bar
solution_html:
Applying the relationship
m = KHp
In the first case,
6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 g = KH {tex}\times{/tex} 1 bar
KH = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 g/bar
In second case,
5.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 g = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 g/bar {tex}\times{/tex} p
P = {tex}\frac{{5.00 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}g}}{{6.56 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}g\,ba{r^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} = 0.7621 bar

explanation: Given, the mass of K 2 SO 4 , W b = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −2 g The molar mass of K 2 SO 4 , M b = 174 g mol −1 i = n = 3 ( {tex}\because{/tex} for complete dissociation, t = n) V = 2L, T = 25 o C = 298 K R = 0.0821 L atm K -1 mol -1 we know, osmotic pressure {tex}\pi{/tex} = i {tex}\frac{W_{b} R T}{M_{b} V}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.0821 \times 298}{174 \times 2}{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} = 52.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 atm

i = n = 3 ({tex}\because{/tex} for complete dissociation, t = n)
V = 2L, T = 25oC = 298 K
R = 0.0821 L atm K-1 mol-1
we know, osmotic pressure
{tex}\pi{/tex} = i{tex}\frac{W_{b} R T}{M_{b} V}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.0821 \times 298}{174 \times 2}{/tex}

question_html: 1.00 g of a non-electrolyte solute dissolved in 50 g of benzene lowered the freezing point of benzene by 0.40 K. The freezing point depression constant of benzene is 5.12 K kg mol–1. Find the molar mass of the solute.
question_text: 1.00 g of a non-electrolyte solute dissolved in 50 g of benzene lowered the freezing point of benzene by 0.40 K. The freezing point depression constant of benzene is 5.12 K kg mol –1 . Find the molar mass of the solute.
explanation: Given, W 2 = 1.00 g, W 1 = 50 g, K f = 5.12 K kg mo1 -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.40 K, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{M_2}\, \times \,{W_1}}}{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{W_1}\, \times \,{\Delta T_f}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{5.12\, \times \,1\, \times \,1000}}{{50\, \times \,0.40}}{/tex} = 256 g mol -1
solution: Given, W 2 = 1.00 g, W 1 = 50 g, K f = 5.12 K kg mo1 -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.40 K, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{M_2}\, \times \,{W_1}}}{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{W_1}\, \times \,{\Delta T_f}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{5.12\, \times \,1\, \times \,1000}}{{50\, \times \,0.40}}{/tex} = 256 g mol -1
solution_html:
Given, W2 = 1.00 g, W1 = 50 g, Kf = 5.12 K kg mo1-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 0.40 K, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{M_2}\, \times \,{W_1}}}{/tex}
M2 = {tex}\frac{{{K_f}\, \times \,{W_2}\, \times \,1000}}{{{W_1}\, \times \,{\Delta T_f}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{5.12\, \times \,1\, \times \,1000}}{{50\, \times \,0.40}}{/tex}
= 256 g mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: State Raoult's law for a solution containing volatile components. How does Raoult's law become a special case of Henry's law?
question_text: State Raoult's law for a solution containing volatile components. How does Raoult's law become a special case of Henry's law?
explanation: Raoult's law For a solution of volatile liquids. the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in solution. Thus, for any component, partial vapour pressure, p {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = p o {tex}\chi{/tex} Where P o is the vapour pressure of pure component and {tex}\chi{/tex} is the mole fraction of that component. Raoult's law as a special case of Henry's law In the solution of a gas in a liquid, one of the components is so volatile that it exists as a gas and its solubility is given by Henry's law which states that, p = K H {tex}\chi{/tex} . i.e. partial pressure of the volatile component (gas) is directly proportional to the mole fraction of that component (gas) in the solution. When the equations of Raoult's law and Henry's law are compared, it can be seen that the partial pressure of the volatile component or gas is directly proportional to its mole fraction in solution. Only the proportionality constant K H differs from p o . Hence, Raoult's law and Henry's law has been identical except that their proportionality constants are different. Therefore, Raoult's law becomes a special case of Henry's law in which KH becomes equal to the vapour pressure of pure component p o .
solution: Raoult's law For a solution of volatile liquids. the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in solution. Thus, for any component, partial vapour pressure, p {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = p o {tex}\chi{/tex} Where P o is the vapour pressure of pure component and {tex}\chi{/tex} is the mole fraction of that component. Raoult's law as a special case of Henry's law In the solution of a gas in a liquid, one of the components is so volatile that it exists as a gas and its solubility is given by Henry's law which states that, p = K H {tex}\chi{/tex} . i.e. partial pressure of the volatile component (gas) is directly proportional to the mole fraction of that component (gas) in the solution. When the equations of Raoult's law and Henry's law are compared, it can be seen that the partial pressure of the volatile component or gas is directly proportional to its mole fraction in solution. Only the proportionality constant K H differs from p o . Hence, Raoult's law and Henry's law has been identical except that their proportionality constants are different. Therefore, Raoult's law becomes a special case of Henry's law in which KH becomes equal to the vapour pressure of pure component p o .
solution_html:
Raoult's law For a solution of volatile liquids. the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in solution. Thus, for any component, partial vapour pressure,
p {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = po{tex}\chi{/tex}
Where Pois the vapour pressure of pure component and {tex}\chi{/tex} is the mole fraction of that component.
Raoult's law as a special case of Henry's law
In the solution of a gas in a liquid, one of the components is so volatile that it exists as a gas and its solubility is given by Henry's law which states that, p = KH{tex}\chi{/tex}.
i.e. partial pressure of the volatile component (gas) is directly proportional to the mole fraction of that component (gas) in the solution.
When the equations of Raoult's law and Henry's law are compared, it can be seen that the partial pressure of the volatile component or gas is directly proportional to its mole fraction in solution. Only the proportionality constant K H differs from po.
Hence, Raoult's law and Henry's law has been identical except that their proportionality constants are different.
Therefore, Raoult's law becomes a special case of Henry's law in which KH becomes equal to the vapour pressure of pure component po.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
18 g glucose, C6H126 (molar mass = 180 g mol-1) is dissolved in 1 kg of water in a saucepan. At what temperature, will this solution boil?
(Kb for water = 0.52K kg mol-1 boiling point of pure water = 373.15K)
question_text: 18 g glucose, C 6 H 12 6 (molar mass = 180 g mol -1 ) is dissolved in 1 kg of water in a saucepan. At what temperature, will this solution boil? (K b for water = 0.52K kg mol -1 boiling point of pure water = 373.15K)
explanation: Given, wa = weight of H 2 O (solvent) = I kg W b = weight of C 6 H 12 O (glucose-solute) = 18 g {tex}T_b^o{/tex} = 37315 K K b = 0.52 K kg mol -1 M b = Molar mass of solute (glucose) = 180 g mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{{{K_b} \times \,1000 \times \,{W_b}}}{{{M_b}\, \times \,{W_a}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{(0.52\,K\,kg\,mo{l^{_{ - 1}}}) \times \,1000 \times \,18\,g}}{{(180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}})\, \times \,1000\,g}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{9360}}{{180000}}{/tex} = 0.052 K As we know that, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = T b - {tex}T_b^o{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.052 = T b - 373.15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Tb = 373.15 + 0.052 = 373.202 K = 373.20 K (approx).
solution: Given, wa = weight of H 2 O (solvent) = I kg W b = weight of C 6 H 12 O (glucose-solute) = 18 g {tex}T_b^o{/tex} = 37315 K K b = 0.52 K kg mol -1 M b = Molar mass of solute (glucose) = 180 g mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{{{K_b} \times \,1000 \times \,{W_b}}}{{{M_b}\, \times \,{W_a}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{(0.52\,K\,kg\,mo{l^{_{ - 1}}}) \times \,1000 \times \,18\,g}}{{(180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}})\, \times \,1000\,g}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{9360}}{{180000}}{/tex} = 0.052 K As we know that, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = T b - {tex}T_b^o{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.052 = T b - 373.15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Tb = 373.15 + 0.052 = 373.202 K = 373.20 K (approx).
solution_html:
Given, wa = weight of H2O (solvent) = I kg
Wb = weight of C6H12O (glucose-solute)
= 18 g
{tex}T_b^o{/tex} =  37315 K
Kb = 0.52 K kg mol-1
Mb = Molar mass of solute (glucose)
= 180 g mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = {tex}\frac{{{K_b} \times \,1000 \times \,{W_b}}}{{{M_b}\, \times \,{W_a}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{{(0.52\,K\,kg\,mo{l^{_{ - 1}}}) \times \,1000 \times \,18\,g}}{{(180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}})\, \times \,1000\,g}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{{9360}}{{180000}}{/tex} = 0.052 K
As we know that, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = Tb - {tex}T_b^o{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.052 = Tb - 373.15
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Tb = 373.15 + 0.052 = 373.202 K
= 373.20 K (approx).

subject: Chemistry
question_html: If the density of some lake water is 1.25 g mL-1 and contains 92 g of Na+ ions per kg of water, calculate the molarity of Na+ ions in the lake.
question_text: If the density of some lake water is 1.25 g mL -1 and contains 92 g of Na + ions per kg of water, calculate the molarity of Na + ions in the lake.
explanation: Molar mass of {tex}Na = 23\;g\;{mol}^{-1}{/tex} No. of moles of {tex}{Na}^{+} = \frac{{92g}}{{23{\rm{ g mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}}} = 4{\rm{ mole}}{/tex} {tex}\text { Density }=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { density }}=\frac{1000}{1.25}(\mathrm{~g}){/tex} {tex}V=800 \mathrm{ml}{/tex} {tex}\text { Molarity }=\frac{\text { Number of moles }}{\text { Volume }(\mathrm{ml})} \times 1000{/tex} {tex}\text { Molarity }=\frac{4 \times 1000}{800}=5 \text { moles }{/tex}
solution: Molar mass of {tex}Na = 23\;g\;{mol}^{-1}{/tex} No. of moles of {tex}{Na}^{+} = \frac{{92g}}{{23{\rm{ g mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}}} = 4{\rm{ mole}}{/tex} {tex}\text { Density }=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { density }}=\frac{1000}{1.25}(\mathrm{~g}){/tex} {tex}V=800 \mathrm{ml}{/tex} {tex}\text { Molarity }=\frac{\text { Number of moles }}{\text { Volume }(\mathrm{ml})} \times 1000{/tex} {tex}\text { Molarity }=\frac{4 \times 1000}{800}=5 \text { moles }{/tex}
solution_html:
Molar mass of {tex}Na = 23\;g\;{mol}^{-1}{/tex}
No. of moles of {tex}{Na}^{+} = \frac{{92g}}{{23{\rm{ g mo}}{{\rm{l}}^{ - 1}}}} = 4{\rm{ mole}}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Density }=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }}{/tex}
{tex}V=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { density }}=\frac{1000}{1.25}(\mathrm{~g}){/tex}
{tex}V=800 \mathrm{ml}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Molarity }=\frac{\text { Number of moles }}{\text { Volume }(\mathrm{ml})} \times 1000{/tex}
{tex}\text { Molarity }=\frac{4 \times 1000}{800}=5 \text { moles }{/tex}

question_html: Define the term: Mole fraction.
question_text: Define the term: Mole fraction.
explanation: Mole fraction: It is the ratio of number of moles of a particular component to the total number of moles of all the components in a solution. Example: Mole fraction of component A {tex}{x_A} = \frac{{{x_A}}}{{{x_A} + {x_B}}}{/tex} Where x A is the number of moles of component 'A' and x B is the number of moles of component B.
solution: Mole fraction: It is the ratio of number of moles of a particular component to the total number of moles of all the components in a solution. Example: Mole fraction of component A {tex}{x_A} = \frac{{{x_A}}}{{{x_A} + {x_B}}}{/tex} Where x A is the number of moles of component 'A' and x B is the number of moles of component B.
solution_html:
Mole fraction: It is the ratio of number of moles of a particular component to the total number of moles of all the components in a solution.
Example: Mole fraction of component A
{tex}{x_A} = \frac{{{x_A}}}{{{x_A} + {x_B}}}{/tex}
Where xA is the number of moles of component 'A' and xB is the number of moles of component B.

question_html:
15.0 g of unknown molecular material is dissolved in 450 g of water. The resulting solution freezes at - 0.34°C. What is the molar mass of the material?
(Kf for water = 1.86 K kg mol-1)
question_text: 15.0 g of unknown molecular material is dissolved in 450 g of water. The resulting solution freezes at - 0.34°C. What is the molar mass of the material? (K f for water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: Given, the mass of solute (W 2 ) = 15.0 g Mass of solvent (water) (W 1 ) = 450 g {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0 o C - (-0.34 o C) = 0.34 o C = 0.34 K K f = 10.86 K kg mol -1 To find molar mass of solute (M 2 ) = ? {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = K f {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{W_2}}}{{{M_2}}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{{W_1}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{{{K_f} \times {W_2}\, \times 1000\,}}{{\Delta {T_f}\, \times \,{W_1}}}{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{{1.86 \times 15\, \times 1000\,}}{{0.34 \times \,450}}{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{{27900}}{{153}}{/tex} = 182.35 g mol -1
solution: Given, the mass of solute (W 2 ) = 15.0 g Mass of solvent (water) (W 1 ) = 450 g {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0 o C - (-0.34 o C) = 0.34 o C = 0.34 K K f = 10.86 K kg mol -1 To find molar mass of solute (M 2 ) = ? {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = K f {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{W_2}}}{{{M_2}}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{{W_1}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{{{K_f} \times {W_2}\, \times 1000\,}}{{\Delta {T_f}\, \times \,{W_1}}}{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{{1.86 \times 15\, \times 1000\,}}{{0.34 \times \,450}}{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{{27900}}{{153}}{/tex} = 182.35 g mol -1
solution_html:
Given, the mass of solute (W2) = 15.0 g
Mass of solvent (water) (W1) = 450 g
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 0oC - (-0.34oC) = 0.34oC = 0.34 K
Kf = 10.86 K kg mol-1
To find molar mass of solute (M2) = ?
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = Kf {tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\frac{{{W_2}}}{{{M_2}}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{{W_1}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} M2 = {tex}\frac{{{K_f} \times {W_2}\, \times 1000\,}}{{\Delta {T_f}\, \times \,{W_1}}}{/tex}
M2 = {tex}\frac{{1.86 \times 15\, \times 1000\,}}{{0.34 \times \,450}}{/tex}
M2 = {tex}\frac{{27900}}{{153}}{/tex} = 182.35 g mol-1

question_html:
A solution of glycerol (C3H8O3) in water was prepared by dissolving some glycerol in 500 g of water. This solution has a boiling point of 100.42°C, what mass of glycerol was dissolved to make this solution?
(Kb for water= 0.512 K kg mol-1)
question_text: A solution of glycerol (C 3 H 8 O 3 ) in water was prepared by dissolving some glycerol in 500 g of water. This solution has a boiling point of 100.42°C, what mass of glycerol was dissolved to make this solution? (K b for water= 0.512 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: Given, Mass of solute (glycerol) (W 2 ) =? Molar mass of solute (M 2 ) = 92 g mol -1 Mass of solvent (water) (W 1 ) = 500g {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = 100.42 o C - 100 o C = 0.42 o C = 0.42 k K b = 0.512 k kg mol -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = K b {tex} \times \frac{{{W_2}}}{{{M_2}}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{100}{W_1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} W 2 = {tex}\frac{{{\Delta T_b} \times {M_2}\, \times {W_1}\,}}{{{K_b} \times \,100}}{/tex} W 2 = {tex}\frac{{0.42 \times 92\, \times 500\,}}{{0.512 \times 1000}}{/tex} W 2 = 37.73 g Hence, the mass of glycerol dissolved = 37.73 g.
solution: Given, Mass of solute (glycerol) (W 2 ) =? Molar mass of solute (M 2 ) = 92 g mol -1 Mass of solvent (water) (W 1 ) = 500g {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = 100.42 o C - 100 o C = 0.42 o C = 0.42 k K b = 0.512 k kg mol -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = K b {tex} \times \frac{{{W_2}}}{{{M_2}}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{100}{W_1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} W 2 = {tex}\frac{{{\Delta T_b} \times {M_2}\, \times {W_1}\,}}{{{K_b} \times \,100}}{/tex} W 2 = {tex}\frac{{0.42 \times 92\, \times 500\,}}{{0.512 \times 1000}}{/tex} W 2 = 37.73 g Hence, the mass of glycerol dissolved = 37.73 g.
solution_html:
Given, Mass of solute (glycerol) (W2) =?
Molar mass of solute (M2) = 92 g mol-1
Mass of solvent (water) (W1) = 500g
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = 100.42oC - 100oC
= 0.42oC = 0.42 k
Kb = 0.512 k kg mol-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = Kb {tex} \times \frac{{{W_2}}}{{{M_2}}}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{100}{W_1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} W2 = {tex}\frac{{{\Delta T_b} \times {M_2}\, \times {W_1}\,}}{{{K_b} \times \,100}}{/tex}
W2 = {tex}\frac{{0.42 \times 92\, \times 500\,}}{{0.512 \times 1000}}{/tex}
W2 = 37.73 g
Hence, the mass of glycerol dissolved = 37.73 g.

question_html: Explain the Henry's Law about dissolution of a gas in a liquid.
question_text: Explain the Henry's Law about dissolution of a gas in a liquid.
explanation: Henry’s law states that the partial vapour pressure (p) of the gas is proportional to the mole fraction (x) of the gas dissolved in the solution. p= K H x K H is called Henry's law constant. When a mixture of gases is brought in contact with the solvent, each constituent gas dissolves in proportion to its own partial pressure. Therefore ,Henry's law is applied to each gas independent of presence of other gas. A graph of partial pressure of gas against mole fraction of the gas in solution at a given temperature is drawn, a straight line passing through the origin is obtained. The slope of the line gives the Henry's law constant.
solution: Henry’s law states that the partial vapour pressure (p) of the gas is proportional to the mole fraction (x) of the gas dissolved in the solution. p= K H x K H is called Henry's law constant. When a mixture of gases is brought in contact with the solvent, each constituent gas dissolves in proportion to its own partial pressure. Therefore ,Henry's law is applied to each gas independent of presence of other gas. A graph of partial pressure of gas against mole fraction of the gas in solution at a given temperature is drawn, a straight line passing through the origin is obtained. The slope of the line gives the Henry's law constant.

Henry’s law states that the partial vapour pressure (p) of the gas is proportional to the mole fraction (x) of the gas dissolved in the solution.
p= KHx    KH is called Henry's law constant.
When a mixture of gases is brought in contact with the solvent, each constituent gas dissolves in proportion to its own partial pressure. Therefore ,Henry's law is applied to each gas independent of presence of other gas.
A graph of partial pressure of gas against mole fraction of the gas in solution at a given temperature is drawn, a straight line passing through the origin is obtained. The slope of the line gives the Henry's law constant.

question_text: Explain boiling point elevation constant for a solvent.
explanation: Boiling point elevation constant ( {tex}K_b{/tex} ) of a solvent is elevation in boiling point when concentration of solution is taken as 1 molal solution. that is a solution containing 1 gram mole of solute dissolved in 1000g of the solvent. subject: Chemistry
question_html: Explain why aquatic species are more comfortable in cold water rather than in warm water.
question_text: Explain why aquatic species are more comfortable in cold water rather than in warm water.
explanation: Increase in temperature decreases the solubility of oxygen in the water. The amount of oxygen dissolved in water decreases at a higher temperature. As a result, it becomes more difficult to breathe less dissolved oxygen. Hence, the aquatic species are not comfortable in warm water.
solution: Increase in temperature decreases the solubility of oxygen in the water. The amount of oxygen dissolved in water decreases at a higher temperature. As a result, it becomes more difficult to breathe less dissolved oxygen. Hence, the aquatic species are not comfortable in warm water.
solution_html: Increase in temperature decreases the solubility of oxygen in the water. The amount of oxygen dissolved in water decreases at a higher temperature. As a result, it becomes more difficult to breathe less dissolved oxygen. Hence, the aquatic species are not comfortable in warm water.

question_html:
Define the following terms.

  1. Isotonic solutions
  2. van't Hoff factor
question_text: Define the following terms. Isotonic solutions van't Hoff factor
explanation: Two solutions having the same osmotic pressure at a given temperature are called isotonic solutions. Van't Hoff factor: It is defined as the ratio of the experimental value of colligative property to the calculated value of the colligative property and is used to find out the extent of dissociation or association. Mathematically, It is represented as r = {tex}{\text{}}\,{\text{ }}\frac{{{\text{Observed}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{preperty}}}}{{{\text{Calculated}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{/tex}
solution: Two solutions having the same osmotic pressure at a given temperature are called isotonic solutions. Van't Hoff factor: It is defined as the ratio of the experimental value of colligative property to the calculated value of the colligative property and is used to find out the extent of dissociation or association. Mathematically, It is represented as r = {tex}{\text{}}\,{\text{ }}\frac{{{\text{Observed}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{preperty}}}}{{{\text{Calculated}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Two solutions having the same osmotic pressure at a given temperature are called isotonic solutions.
  2. Van't Hoff factor: It is defined as the ratio of the experimental value of colligative property to the calculated value of the colligative property and is used to find out the extent of dissociation or association. Mathematically, It is represented as
    r = {tex}{\text{}}\,{\text{ }}\frac{{{\text{Observed}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{preperty}}}}{{{\text{Calculated}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A 1.00 molal aqueous solution of trichloroacetic acid (CCl3COOH) is heated to its boiling point. The solution has the boiling point 100.18°C. Determine the van't Hoff factor for trichloroacetic acid. (Kb for water = 0.512 K kg mol-1).
question_text: A 1.00 molal aqueous solution of trichloroacetic acid (CCl 3 COOH) is heated to its boiling point. The solution has the boiling point 100.18°C. Determine the van't Hoff factor for trichloroacetic acid. (K b for water = 0.512 K kg mol -1 ).
explanation: Consider the relation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = iKbm Molality of solution, m = 1.00 m Boiling point of solution, T b = 100.18°C = 373.18 K Boiling point of water (solvent) {tex}T_b^o{/tex} = 100.00°C = 373 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = Tb - {tex}T_b^o{/tex} = 373.18 K - 373 K = 0.18 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = i K b m 0.18 K = i {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 K kg mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1mol kg -1 i = {tex}\frac{0.13 \mathrm{K}}{0.512 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}}{/tex} i = 0.35
solution: Consider the relation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = iKbm Molality of solution, m = 1.00 m Boiling point of solution, T b = 100.18°C = 373.18 K Boiling point of water (solvent) {tex}T_b^o{/tex} = 100.00°C = 373 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = Tb - {tex}T_b^o{/tex} = 373.18 K - 373 K = 0.18 K {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = i K b m 0.18 K = i {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 K kg mol -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1mol kg -1 i = {tex}\frac{0.13 \mathrm{K}}{0.512 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}}{/tex} i = 0.35
solution_html:
Consider the relation, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = iKbm
Molality of solution, m = 1.00 m
Boiling point of solution,
Tb = 100.18°C = 373.18 K
Boiling point of water (solvent)
{tex}T_b^o{/tex} = 100.00°C = 373 K
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = Tb - {tex}T_b^o{/tex} = 373.18 K - 373 K = 0.18 K
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = i Kbm
0.18 K = i {tex}\times{/tex} 0.512 K kg mol-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1mol kg-1
i = {tex}\frac{0.13 \mathrm{K}}{0.512 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}}{/tex}
i = 0.35

subject: Chemistry
question_html: At 25°C, the saturated vapor pressure of water is 3.165 K Pa (23.75 mm Hg). Find the saturated vapor pressure of a 5% aqueous solution of urea (carbamide) at the same temperature. (Molar mass of urea = 60.05 g mol-1)
question_text: At 25°C, the saturated vapor pressure of water is 3.165 K Pa (23.75 mm Hg). Find the saturated vapor pressure of a 5% aqueous solution of urea (carbamide) at the same temperature. (Molar mass of urea = 60.05 g mol -1 )
explanation: From Raoult's law, {tex}\frac{{{p^o}\, - \,p}}{{{p^o}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{p^o}\, - \,p}}{{{p^o}}}{/tex} = x 2 = {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}}{/tex} [For a dilute solution, n 2 << n 1 ] {tex}\frac{{{p^o}\, - \,p}}{{{p^o}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{W_2} \times \,{M_1}}}{{{M_2} \times {W_1}}}{/tex} Given, p o = 3.165 Kpa, W 2 = 5 g, W 1 = 95 g Molar mass of urea, M 2 = 60.05 g mol -1 Molar mass of water, M 1 = 18 g mol -1 {tex}\frac{{3165\, - p}}{{3165}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{5 \times \,18}}{{60.05\, \times \,95}}{/tex} = 0.0158 3165 - p = 0.050 p = 3.115 K Pa
solution: From Raoult's law, {tex}\frac{{{p^o}\, - \,p}}{{{p^o}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{p^o}\, - \,p}}{{{p^o}}}{/tex} = x 2 = {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}}{/tex} [For a dilute solution, n 2 << n 1 ] {tex}\frac{{{p^o}\, - \,p}}{{{p^o}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{W_2} \times \,{M_1}}}{{{M_2} \times {W_1}}}{/tex} Given, p o = 3.165 Kpa, W 2 = 5 g, W 1 = 95 g Molar mass of urea, M 2 = 60.05 g mol -1 Molar mass of water, M 1 = 18 g mol -1 {tex}\frac{{3165\, - p}}{{3165}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{5 \times \,18}}{{60.05\, \times \,95}}{/tex} = 0.0158 3165 - p = 0.050 p = 3.115 K Pa
solution_html:
From Raoult's law,
{tex}\frac{{{p^o}\, - \,p}}{{{p^o}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{{p^o}\, - \,p}}{{{p^o}}}{/tex} = x2 = {tex}\frac{{{n_2}}}{{{n_1}}}{/tex} [For a dilute solution, n2 << n1]
{tex}\frac{{{p^o}\, - \,p}}{{{p^o}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{W_2} \times \,{M_1}}}{{{M_2} \times {W_1}}}{/tex}
Given, po = 3.165 Kpa, W2 = 5 g, W1 = 95 g
Molar mass of urea, M2 = 60.05 g mol-1
Molar mass of water, M1 = 18 g mol-1
{tex}\frac{{3165\, - p}}{{3165}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{5 \times \,18}}{{60.05\, \times \,95}}{/tex} = 0.0158
3165 - p = 0.050
p = 3.115 K Pa

subject: Chemistry
question_html: If N2 gas is bubbled through water at 293 K, how many millimoles of N2 gas would dissolve in 1 L of water? Assume that N2 exerts a partial pressure of 0.987 bar. Given that Henry's law constant for N2 at 293 K is 76.48 K bar.
question_text: If N 2 gas is bubbled through water at 293 K, how many millimoles of N 2 gas would dissolve in 1 L of water? Assume that N 2 exerts a partial pressure of 0.987 bar. Given that Henry's law constant for N 2 at 293 K is 76.48 K bar.
explanation: According to Henry's law, p N2 = K H {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} N 2 {tex}\chi{/tex} N 2 = {tex}\frac{p_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{K_{\mathrm{H}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.987 \mathrm \ {bar}}{76480 \mathrm \ {bar}}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 If n moles of N 2 are present in 1L or 1000 g of water (or {tex}\frac{10008}{188 \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 55.5 moles) {tex}\chi{/tex} 2 = {tex}\frac{n}{n+55.5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n}{55.5}{/tex} (as n < < < 55.5) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{n}{55.5}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 {tex}\times{/tex} 55.5 moles = 71.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 moles = 0.716 moles
solution: According to Henry's law, p N2 = K H {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} N 2 {tex}\chi{/tex} N 2 = {tex}\frac{p_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{K_{\mathrm{H}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.987 \mathrm \ {bar}}{76480 \mathrm \ {bar}}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 If n moles of N 2 are present in 1L or 1000 g of water (or {tex}\frac{10008}{188 \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 55.5 moles) {tex}\chi{/tex} 2 = {tex}\frac{n}{n+55.5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n}{55.5}{/tex} (as n < < < 55.5) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{n}{55.5}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 {tex}\times{/tex} 55.5 moles = 71.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 moles = 0.716 moles
solution_html:
According to Henry's law,
pN2 = KH {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex}N2
{tex}\chi{/tex}N2 = {tex}\frac{p_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{K_{\mathrm{H}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.987 \mathrm \ {bar}}{76480 \mathrm \ {bar}}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
If n moles of N2 are present in 1L or 1000 g of
water (or {tex}\frac{10008}{188 \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{/tex} = 55.5 moles)
{tex}\chi{/tex}2 = {tex}\frac{n}{n+55.5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n}{55.5}{/tex} (as n < < < 55.5)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{n}{55.5}{/tex} = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 1.29 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 55.5 moles
= 71.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 moles = 0.716 moles

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Explain why a solution of chloroform and acetone shows negative deviation from Raoult's law?
question_text: Explain why a solution of chloroform and acetone shows negative deviation from Raoult's law?
explanation: Because chloroform molecule is able to form hydrogen bond with acetone molecule as they increase the intermolecular attraction between them. It decreases the escaping tendency of molecules for each component and consequently the vapour pressure decreases and causes negative deviation from Raoult's law. For this mixture. {tex}\Delta {H_{mix}} < 0\,\,and\,\Delta {V_{mix}} < 0.{/tex}
solution: Because chloroform molecule is able to form hydrogen bond with acetone molecule as they increase the intermolecular attraction between them. It decreases the escaping tendency of molecules for each component and consequently the vapour pressure decreases and causes negative deviation from Raoult's law. For this mixture. {tex}\Delta {H_{mix}} < 0\,\,and\,\Delta {V_{mix}} < 0.{/tex}
solution_html:
Because chloroform molecule is able to form hydrogen bond with acetone molecule as they increase the intermolecular attraction between them.
It decreases the escaping tendency of molecules for each component and consequently the vapour pressure decreases and causes negative deviation from Raoult's law. For this mixture.
{tex}\Delta {H_{mix}} < 0\,\,and\,\Delta {V_{mix}} < 0.{/tex}

question_html: At 300 K, 36 g of glucose present in a litre of its solution has an osmotic pressure of 4.98 bar. If the osmotic pressure of the solution is 1.52 bars at the same temperature, what would be its concentration?
question_text: At 300 K, 36 g of glucose present in a litre of its solution has an osmotic pressure of 4.98 bar. If the osmotic pressure of the solution is 1.52 bars at the same temperature, what would be its concentration?
explanation: Here, T = 300 K {tex}\pi {/tex} = 1.52 bar R = 0.083 bar L {tex}{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Applying the relation, {tex}\pi {/tex} = CRT {tex}C = \frac{\pi }{{RT}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.52\,bar}}{{0.083\,bar\,L\,{K^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 300\,k}}{/tex} = 0.061 mol Since the volume of the solution is 1 L, the concentration of the solution would be 0.061 M.
solution: Here, T = 300 K {tex}\pi {/tex} = 1.52 bar R = 0.083 bar L {tex}{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} Applying the relation, {tex}\pi {/tex} = CRT {tex}C = \frac{\pi }{{RT}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.52\,bar}}{{0.083\,bar\,L\,{K^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 300\,k}}{/tex} = 0.061 mol Since the volume of the solution is 1 L, the concentration of the solution would be 0.061 M.
solution_html:
Here,
T = 300 K
{tex}\pi {/tex} = 1.52 bar
R = 0.083 bar L {tex}{K^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
Applying the relation,
{tex}\pi {/tex} = CRT
{tex}C = \frac{\pi }{{RT}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1.52\,bar}}{{0.083\,bar\,L\,{K^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 300\,k}}{/tex}
= 0.061 mol
Since the volume of the solution is 1 L, the concentration of the solution would be 0.061 M.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The molecular masses of polymers are determined by the osmotic pressure method and not by measuring other colligative properties. Give two reasons.
question_text: The molecular masses of polymers are determined by the osmotic pressure method and not by measuring other colligative properties. Give two reasons.
explanation: The osmotic pressure method has the advantage over other colligative properties because pressure measurement is around the room temperature and the molarity of the solution is used instead of molality. its magnitude is large as compared to other colligative properties even for very dilute solutions.
solution: The osmotic pressure method has the advantage over other colligative properties because pressure measurement is around the room temperature and the molarity of the solution is used instead of molality. its magnitude is large as compared to other colligative properties even for very dilute solutions.
solution_html:
The osmotic pressure method has the advantage over other colligative properties because

  1. pressure measurement is around the room temperature and the molarity of the solution is used instead of molality.
  2. its magnitude is large as compared to other colligative properties even for very dilute solutions.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Calculate the boiling point of a solution prepared by adding 15.00 g of NaCl to 250.0 g of water. (Kb for water = 0.512 K kg mol-1, molar mass of NaCl= 58.44 g mol-1).
question_text: Calculate the boiling point of a solution prepared by adding 15.00 g of NaCl to 250.0 g of water. (K b for water = 0.512 K kg mol -1 , molar mass of NaCl= 58.44 g mol -1 ).
explanation: Given: W 2 = 15.00 g, W 1 = 250.0 g M 2 = 58.44 g mol -1 , Kb = 0.512 K kg mol -1 NaCI (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na + (aq) + CI - (aq) i = 2 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = iK b .m} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{i \times K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.512 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 15.0 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{58.44 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 250 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = 1.051 K Boiling point of solution = 373 K + 1.051 K = 374.051 K
solution: Given: W 2 = 15.00 g, W 1 = 250.0 g M 2 = 58.44 g mol -1 , Kb = 0.512 K kg mol -1 NaCI (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na + (aq) + CI - (aq) i = 2 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = iK b .m} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{i \times K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.512 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 15.0 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{58.44 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 250 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = 1.051 K Boiling point of solution = 373 K + 1.051 K = 374.051 K
solution_html:
Given: W2 = 15.00 g, W1 = 250.0 g
M2 = 58.44 g mol-1, Kb = 0.512 K kg mol-1
NaCI (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na+ (aq) + CI-(aq)
i = 2
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = iKb.m}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = {tex}\frac{i \times K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.512 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 15.0 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{58.44 \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 250 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = 1.051 K
Boiling point of solution = 373 K + 1.051 K
= 374.051 K

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What would be the molar mass of a compound if 6.21 g of it dissolved in 24.0 g of chloroform to form a solution that has a boiling point of 68.04oC. The boiling point of pure chloroform is 61.7oC and the boiling point elevation constant, Kb for chloroform is 3.63oC/m.
question_text: What would be the molar mass of a compound if 6.21 g of it dissolved in 24.0 g of chloroform to form a solution that has a boiling point of 68.04 o C. The boiling point of pure chloroform is 61.7 o C and the boiling point elevation constant, K b for chloroform is 3.63 o C/m.
explanation: Given, W 2 = 6.21 g, W 1 = 24.0 g T b = 68.04 o C T {tex}_b^o{/tex} = 61.7 o C and K b = 3.63 o C / m; {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} or M 2 = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = T b - T {tex}_b^o{/tex} = 68.04 o C - 61.7 o C = 6.34 o C M 2 = {tex}\frac{3.63^{\circ} \mathrm{Cm}^{-1} \times 6.21 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{6.34^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \times 24.0 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 178.15 g mol -1
solution: Given, W 2 = 6.21 g, W 1 = 24.0 g T b = 68.04 o C T {tex}_b^o{/tex} = 61.7 o C and K b = 3.63 o C / m; {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} or M 2 = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = T b - T {tex}_b^o{/tex} = 68.04 o C - 61.7 o C = 6.34 o C M 2 = {tex}\frac{3.63^{\circ} \mathrm{Cm}^{-1} \times 6.21 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{6.34^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \times 24.0 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 178.15 g mol -1
solution_html:
Given, W2 = 6.21 g, W1 = 24.0 g
Tb = 68.04oC
T{tex}_b^o{/tex}= 61.7oC
and Kb = 3.63oC / m; {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
or M2 = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = Tb - T{tex}_b^o{/tex} = 68.04oC - 61.7oC
= 6.34oC
M2 = {tex}\frac{3.63^{\circ} \mathrm{Cm}^{-1} \times 6.21 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{6.34^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \times 24.0 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
= 178.15 g mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A 0.561m solution of unknown electrolyte depresses the freezing point of water by 2.93°C. What is van't Hoff factor for this electrolyte? The freezing point depression constant (Kf) for water is 1.86°C kg mol-1.
question_text: A 0.561m solution of unknown electrolyte depresses the freezing point of water by 2.93°C. What is van't Hoff factor for this electrolyte? The freezing point depression constant (K f ) for water is 1.86°C kg mol -1 .
explanation: Given, m = 0.561 m {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 2.93 o C and K f = 1.86 o C kg mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = iK f m i = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f}}{K_{f} m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.93^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{186^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 0.561 \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 2.807
solution: Given, m = 0.561 m {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 2.93 o C and K f = 1.86 o C kg mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = iK f m i = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f}}{K_{f} m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.93^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{186^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 0.561 \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 2.807
solution_html:
Given, m = 0.561 m {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 2.93o C and Kf = 1.86o C kg mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = iKfm
i = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f}}{K_{f} m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.93^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{186^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 0.561 \mathrm{m}}{/tex}
= 2.807

subject: Chemistry
question_html: A solution prepared by dissolving 8.95 mg of a gene fragment in 35.0 mL of water has an osmotic pressure of 0.335 torrs at 25°C. Assuming the gene fragment is non-electrolyte, determine its molar mass.
question_text: A solution prepared by dissolving 8.95 mg of a gene fragment in 35.0 mL of water has an osmotic pressure of 0.335 torrs at 25°C. Assuming the gene fragment is non-electrolyte, determine its molar mass.
explanation: Given, W 2 = 8.95 mg= 8.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 g, V = 35.0 mL= {tex}\frac{35}{1000}{/tex} L {tex}\pi{/tex} = 0.335 torr = {tex}\frac{0.335}{760}{/tex} atm ( {tex}\because{/tex} 1 atm = 760 torr) T = 273 + 25 = 298 K {tex}\pi V{/tex} = nRt or {tex}\pi{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{W_{2} R T}{M_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{W_{2} R T}{\pi V}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8.95 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{g} \times 0.082 \mathrm \ {L \ atm} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \times 298 \mathrm{K}}{\frac{0.335}{760} \mathrm{atm} \times \frac{35.0}{1000} \mathrm{L}}{/tex} M 2 = 14174 g mol -1 M 2 = 1.14174 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 g mol -1
solution: Given, W 2 = 8.95 mg= 8.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -1 g, V = 35.0 mL= {tex}\frac{35}{1000}{/tex} L {tex}\pi{/tex} = 0.335 torr = {tex}\frac{0.335}{760}{/tex} atm ( {tex}\because{/tex} 1 atm = 760 torr) T = 273 + 25 = 298 K {tex}\pi V{/tex} = nRt or {tex}\pi{/tex} V = {tex}\frac{W_{2} R T}{M_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{W_{2} R T}{\pi V}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8.95 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{g} \times 0.082 \mathrm \ {L \ atm} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \times 298 \mathrm{K}}{\frac{0.335}{760} \mathrm{atm} \times \frac{35.0}{1000} \mathrm{L}}{/tex} M 2 = 14174 g mol -1 M 2 = 1.14174 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 g mol -1
solution_html:
Given, W2 = 8.95 mg= 8.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-1 g,
V = 35.0 mL= {tex}\frac{35}{1000}{/tex}L
{tex}\pi{/tex} = 0.335 torr = {tex}\frac{0.335}{760}{/tex}atm ({tex}\because{/tex} 1 atm = 760 torr)
T = 273 + 25 = 298 K
{tex}\pi V{/tex} = nRt or {tex}\pi{/tex}V = {tex}\frac{W_{2} R T}{M_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M2 = {tex}\frac{W_{2} R T}{\pi V}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{8.95 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{g} \times 0.082 \mathrm \ {L \ atm} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \times 298 \mathrm{K}}{\frac{0.335}{760} \mathrm{atm} \times \frac{35.0}{1000} \mathrm{L}}{/tex}
M2 = 14174 g mol-1
M2 = 1.14174 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 g mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Define the terms Osmosis and Osmotic pressure. Is the osmotic pressure of a solution a colligative property? Explain.
question_text: Define the terms Osmosis and Osmotic pressure. Is the osmotic pressure of a solution a colligative property? Explain.
explanation: Osmosis: The movement of solvent molecules from a less concentrated solution to a more concentrated solution through a semipermeable membrane is called osmosis. Osmotic pressure: Osmotic pressure is the extra pressure that is applied to the solution just to prevent the flow of solvent into the solution through a semipermeable membrane. Yes, osmotic pressure is a colligative property because it depends upon the number of solute particles but not on their chemical nature, e.g. 0.1 molar KCl and NaCl have the same osmotic pressure under same conditions of temperature and pressure.
solution: Osmosis: The movement of solvent molecules from a less concentrated solution to a more concentrated solution through a semipermeable membrane is called osmosis. Osmotic pressure: Osmotic pressure is the extra pressure that is applied to the solution just to prevent the flow of solvent into the solution through a semipermeable membrane. Yes, osmotic pressure is a colligative property because it depends upon the number of solute particles but not on their chemical nature, e.g. 0.1 molar KCl and NaCl have the same osmotic pressure under same conditions of temperature and pressure.
solution_html:
Osmosis: The movement of solvent molecules from a less concentrated solution to a more concentrated solution through a semipermeable membrane is called osmosis.
Osmotic pressure: Osmotic pressure is the extra pressure that is applied to the solution just to prevent the flow of solvent into the solution through a semipermeable membrane.
Yes, osmotic pressure is a colligative property because it depends upon the number of solute particles but not on their chemical nature, e.g. 0.1 molar KCl and NaCl have the same osmotic pressure under same conditions of temperature and pressure.

question_html: Calculate the freezing point of an aqueous solution containing 10.50 g of MgBr2 in 200 g of water (molar mass of MgBr2 = 184 g mol-1, Kf for water is 1.86 K kg mol-1).
question_text: Calculate the freezing point of an aqueous solution containing 10.50 g of MgBr 2 in 200 g of water (molar mass of MgBr 2 = 184 g mol -1 , K f for water is 1.86 K kg mol -1 ).
explanation: Given: mass of MgBr 2 , W 2 = 10.50g, Mass of water, W 1 = 200 g Molar mass of MgBr 2 , M 2 = 184 g mol −1 K f = 1.86 K kg mol −1 MgBr 2 (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Mg 2+ (aq) + 2Br - (aq) Here, van't hoff factor, i = 3 Applying Van't Hoff equation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = i K f m {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{i \times K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{3 \times 1.86 \times 10.50 \times 1000}{184 \times 200}{/tex} = 1.592 K The freezing point of solution, T f = 273 K - 1.592 K T f = 271.408 K
solution: Given: mass of MgBr 2 , W 2 = 10.50g, Mass of water, W 1 = 200 g Molar mass of MgBr 2 , M 2 = 184 g mol −1 K f = 1.86 K kg mol −1 MgBr 2 (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Mg 2+ (aq) + 2Br - (aq) Here, van't hoff factor, i = 3 Applying Van't Hoff equation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = i K f m {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{i \times K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{3 \times 1.86 \times 10.50 \times 1000}{184 \times 200}{/tex} = 1.592 K The freezing point of solution, T f = 273 K - 1.592 K T f = 271.408 K
solution_html:
Given: mass of MgBr2, W2 = 10.50g, Mass of water, W1 = 200 g
Molar mass of MgBr2, M2 = 184 g mol−1
Kf = 1.86 K kg mol−1
MgBr2 (aq) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Mg2+ (aq) + 2Br-(aq)
Here, van't hoff factor, i = 3
Applying Van't Hoff equation,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = i Kf m
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{i \times K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{3 \times 1.86 \times 10.50 \times 1000}{184 \times 200}{/tex} = 1.592 K
The freezing point of solution, Tf  = 273 K - 1.592 K
Tf = 271.408 K

question_html:
Phenol associates in benzene to a certain extent to form a dimer. A solution containing 20 g of phenol in 1.0 kg of benzene has its freezing point lowered by 0.69 K. Calculate the fraction of phenol that has dimerized.
(Given, Kf for benzene = 5.1 K kg mol-1)
question_text: Phenol associates in benzene to a certain extent to form a dimer. A solution containing 20 g of phenol in 1.0 kg of benzene has its freezing point lowered by 0.69 K. Calculate the fraction of phenol that has dimerized. (Given, K f for benzene = 5.1 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: Here, n = 2 because of phenol forms dimer on association. Given, mass of phenol, W 2 = 20 g , mass of benzene, W 1 = 1 kg = 1000 g {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.69 K, K f = 5.1 K kg mol -1 We have, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} W_{1}}{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{f} \times W_{1}}{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{5.1 \times 20 \times 1000}{0.69 \times 1000 }{/tex} = 147.82 g mol -1 M 2 (Calculated), C 6 H 5 OH = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 16 = 94 g mol -1 Van't Hoff factor, i = {tex}\frac{M_{2}(\text { Calculated })}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{94}{147.82}{/tex} = 0.635 Phenol undergoes association as follows: 2C 6 H 5 OH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} (C 6 H 5 OH) 2 We, know that, dissociation constant and van't Hoff factor related to each other as follows; {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{i-1}{\left(\frac{1}{n}-1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.635-1}{\frac{1}{2}-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.365}{0.5}{/tex} = 0.73 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 73%
solution: Here, n = 2 because of phenol forms dimer on association. Given, mass of phenol, W 2 = 20 g , mass of benzene, W 1 = 1 kg = 1000 g {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.69 K, K f = 5.1 K kg mol -1 We have, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} W_{1}}{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{f} \times W_{1}}{/tex} M 2 = {tex}\frac{5.1 \times 20 \times 1000}{0.69 \times 1000 }{/tex} = 147.82 g mol -1 M 2 (Calculated), C 6 H 5 OH = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 16 = 94 g mol -1 Van't Hoff factor, i = {tex}\frac{M_{2}(\text { Calculated })}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{94}{147.82}{/tex} = 0.635 Phenol undergoes association as follows: 2C 6 H 5 OH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} (C 6 H 5 OH) 2 We, know that, dissociation constant and van't Hoff factor related to each other as follows; {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{i-1}{\left(\frac{1}{n}-1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.635-1}{\frac{1}{2}-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.365}{0.5}{/tex} = 0.73 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 73%
solution_html:
Here, n = 2 because of phenol forms dimer on association.
Given, mass of phenol, W2 = 20 g , mass of benzene, W1 = 1 kg = 1000 g
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 0.69 K, Kf = 5.1 K kg mol-1
We have, 
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} W_{1}}{/tex}
M2 = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{f} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
M2 = {tex}\frac{5.1 \times 20 \times 1000}{0.69 \times 1000 }{/tex} = 147.82 g mol-1
M2 (Calculated), C6H5OH = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 16  = 94 g mol-1
Van't Hoff factor, i = {tex}\frac{M_{2}(\text { Calculated })}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{94}{147.82}{/tex} = 0.635
Phenol undergoes association as follows: 2C6H5OH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} (C6H5OH)2
We, know that, dissociation constant and van't Hoff factor related to each other as follows; 
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{i-1}{\left(\frac{1}{n}-1\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.635-1}{\frac{1}{2}-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.365}{0.5}{/tex} = 0.73
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 73%
