question_html: An aqueous solution containing 12.48 g of barium chloride in 1.0 kg of water boils at 373.0832 K. Calculate the degree of dissociation of barium chloride. (Given, Kb for H2O = 0.52 K kg mol-1, molar mass of BaCI2 = 208.34 g mol-1).
question_text: An aqueous solution containing 12.48 g of barium chloride in 1.0 kg of water boils at 373.0832 K. Calculate the degree of dissociation of barium chloride. (Given, K b for H 2 O = 0.52 K kg mol -1 , molar mass of BaCI 2 = 208.34 g mol -1 ).
explanation: Given, mass of BaCl 2 , W 2 = 12.48g, mass of water, W 1 = 1.0 kg = 1000 g, T b (solution) = 373.0832 K K b for H 2 O = 0 52 Km -1 and Molar mass of BaCl 2 , M 2 = 208.34 g mol -1 {tex}\Delta {T_b} = {T_b} - T_b^0 = 373.0832K - 373K{/tex} = 0.0832 K M 2 (Observed) = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.52 \times 12.48 \times 1000}{0.0832 \times 1000 }{/tex} = 78 g mol -1 Van't Hoff factor, i = {tex}\frac{M_{2} \text { (Calculated) }}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{208.34 }{78}{/tex} = 2.67 For BaCI 2 , m = 3 as it gives 3 ions on dissociation. We know that dissociation constant and Van't Hoff factor related to each other as follows: {tex}\alpha = \frac{{i - 1}}{{m - 1}} = \frac{{2.67 - 1}}{{3 - 1}} = \frac{{1.67}}{2} = 0.835{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 83.5%
solution: Given, mass of BaCl 2 , W 2 = 12.48g, mass of water, W 1 = 1.0 kg = 1000 g, T b (solution) = 373.0832 K K b for H 2 O = 0 52 Km -1 and Molar mass of BaCl 2 , M 2 = 208.34 g mol -1 {tex}\Delta {T_b} = {T_b} - T_b^0 = 373.0832K - 373K{/tex} = 0.0832 K M 2 (Observed) = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.52 \times 12.48 \times 1000}{0.0832 \times 1000 }{/tex} = 78 g mol -1 Van't Hoff factor, i = {tex}\frac{M_{2} \text { (Calculated) }}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{208.34 }{78}{/tex} = 2.67 For BaCI 2 , m = 3 as it gives 3 ions on dissociation. We know that dissociation constant and Van't Hoff factor related to each other as follows: {tex}\alpha = \frac{{i - 1}}{{m - 1}} = \frac{{2.67 - 1}}{{3 - 1}} = \frac{{1.67}}{2} = 0.835{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 83.5%
solution_html:
Given,
mass of BaCl2, W2 = 12.48g, mass of water, W1 = 1.0 kg = 1000 g,
Tb (solution) = 373.0832 K
Kb for H2O = 0 52 Km-1 and Molar mass of BaCl2, M2 = 208.34 g mol-1
{tex}\Delta {T_b} = {T_b} - T_b^0 = 373.0832K - 373K{/tex} = 0.0832 K
M2 (Observed) = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.52 \times 12.48 \times 1000}{0.0832 \times 1000 }{/tex} = 78 g mol-1
Van't Hoff factor, i = {tex}\frac{M_{2} \text { (Calculated) }}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{208.34 }{78}{/tex} = 2.67
For BaCI2, m = 3 as it gives 3 ions on dissociation.
We know that dissociation constant and Van't Hoff factor related to each other as follows: 
{tex}\alpha = \frac{{i - 1}}{{m - 1}} = \frac{{2.67 - 1}}{{3 - 1}} = \frac{{1.67}}{2} = 0.835{/tex}
{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 83.5%

question_html: What is van't Hoff factor? What possible values can it have if the solute molecules undergo dissociation?
question_text: What is van't Hoff factor? What possible values can it have if the solute molecules undergo dissociation?
explanation: van't Hoff factor It is defined as the ratio of the experimental value of colligative property to the calculated value of the colligative property and is used to find out the extent of dissociation or association. Mathematically, It is represented as I = {tex}\frac{{{\text{Observed}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{{{\text{Calculated}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{/tex} In dissociation, the observed molar mass has lesser value than normal, so i is more than one (i > 1). e.g. The value of i for ethanoic acid in benzene is nearly 0.5 while for aqueous KCL, NaCl and MgS0 4 solution, it is nearly 2. The value of i for K 2 SO 4 is nearly 3.
solution: van't Hoff factor It is defined as the ratio of the experimental value of colligative property to the calculated value of the colligative property and is used to find out the extent of dissociation or association. Mathematically, It is represented as I = {tex}\frac{{{\text{Observed}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{{{\text{Calculated}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{/tex} In dissociation, the observed molar mass has lesser value than normal, so i is more than one (i > 1). e.g. The value of i for ethanoic acid in benzene is nearly 0.5 while for aqueous KCL, NaCl and MgS0 4 solution, it is nearly 2. The value of i for K 2 SO 4 is nearly 3.
solution_html:
van't Hoff factor It is defined as the ratio of the experimental value of colligative property to the calculated value of the colligative property and is used to find out the extent of dissociation or association. Mathematically, It is represented as
I = {tex}\frac{{{\text{Observed}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{{{\text{Calculated}}\,{\text{colligative}}\,{\text{property}}}}{/tex}
In dissociation, the observed molar mass has lesser value than normal, so i is more than one (i > 1). e.g. The value of i for ethanoic acid in benzene is nearly 0.5 while for aqueous KCL, NaCl and MgS04 solution, it is nearly 2. The value of i for K2SO4 is nearly 3.

question_html: Calculate the boiling point of one molar aqueous solution. Density of KBr solution is 1.06 g mL-1 (Kb for H2O = 0.52 K kg mol-1, atomic mass of K = 39, Br = 80).
question_text: Calculate the boiling point of one molar aqueous solution. Density of KBr solution is 1.06 g mL -1 (K b for H 2 O = 0.52 K kg mol -1 , atomic mass of K = 39, Br = 80).
explanation: Given, concentration of the solution = 1 molar Density of the solution = l.06 g mL -1 M 2 , molar mass of KBr = 39 + 80 = 119 g mol -1 K b for H 2 O = 0.52 K kg mol -1 KBr {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} K + + Br - i.e. ions produced = 2 i = 2 Molality, m = {tex}\frac{M \times 1000}{(1000 \times d)-\left(M \times M_{2}\right)}{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{1 \times 1000}{(1000 \times 106)-(1 \times 119)}{/tex} = 1.0626 mol kg -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = iK b m = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 052 K kg mol −1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.0626 mol kg −1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = 1.105 K T b = 373 K + 10105 K = 374.105 K
solution: Given, concentration of the solution = 1 molar Density of the solution = l.06 g mL -1 M 2 , molar mass of KBr = 39 + 80 = 119 g mol -1 K b for H 2 O = 0.52 K kg mol -1 KBr {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} K + + Br - i.e. ions produced = 2 i = 2 Molality, m = {tex}\frac{M \times 1000}{(1000 \times d)-\left(M \times M_{2}\right)}{/tex} m = {tex}\frac{1 \times 1000}{(1000 \times 106)-(1 \times 119)}{/tex} = 1.0626 mol kg -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = iK b m = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 052 K kg mol −1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.0626 mol kg −1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = 1.105 K T b = 373 K + 10105 K = 374.105 K
solution_html:
Given, concentration of the solution = 1 molar
Density of the solution = l.06 g mL-1
M2, molar mass of KBr = 39 + 80 = 119 g mol-1
Kb for H2O = 0.52 K kg mol-1
KBr {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} K+ + Br-
i.e. ions produced = 2
i = 2
Molality, m = {tex}\frac{M \times 1000}{(1000 \times d)-\left(M \times M_{2}\right)}{/tex}
m = {tex}\frac{1 \times 1000}{(1000 \times 106)-(1 \times 119)}{/tex}
= 1.0626 mol kg-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = iKbm
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 052 K kg mol−1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.0626 mol kg−1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = 1.105 K
Tb = 373 K + 10105 K = 374.105 K

question_html: State the main advantage of molality over molarity as the unit of concentration.
question_text: State the main advantage of molality over molarity as the unit of concentration.
explanation: Molality is temperature independent while molarity decreases with increase in concentration. This is because of expansion or contraction of the liquid with temperature.
solution: Molality is temperature independent while molarity decreases with increase in concentration. This is because of expansion or contraction of the liquid with temperature.
solution_html: Molality is temperature independent while molarity decreases with increase in concentration. This is because of expansion or contraction of the liquid with temperature.

1. Find the boiling point of a solution containing 0.520 g of glucose dissolved in 80.2 g of water. [Given : Kb for water = 0.52Km−1]

question_text: Find the boiling point of a solution containing 0.520 g of glucose dissolved in 80.2 g of water. [Given : K b for water = 0.52Km −1 ] What mass of ethylene glycol (molar mass 62.0gmol −1 ) must be added to 5.50kg of water to lower the freezing point of water from O o C to - {tex}10.0^0\;C{/tex} ? ( {tex}K_f{/tex} for water = {tex}1.86 \;K\; kg\; {mol}^{-1}{/tex} )
explanation: Given, w B = 0.520 g, w A = 80.2 g, {tex}K_b = 0.52 \;{Km}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}{M_2}({C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}) = (6 \times 12 )+( 12 \times 1) +( 6 \times 16){/tex} {tex}= \,180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_b} = \frac{{{K_b} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {w_A}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.52K{m^{ - 1}} \times 0.520g \times 1000g\,kg{\,^{ - 1}}}}{{180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 80.2g}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_b} = 0.0187K{/tex} Boiling point of solution {tex}({{\rm{T}}_b}) = T_b^o + \Delta {T_b}{/tex} {tex} = 373K + 0.0187K{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_b} = 373.019K{/tex} Given {tex}{w_A} = 5.5kg\, = \,5.5 \times 1000 = 5500g{/tex} {tex}{M_B} = \,62\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_f=0^oC-(-10^oC)=10^0C/\,10K{/tex} {tex}{K_f} = 1.86\,K\,kg\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} As we know, {tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {w_A}}}{/tex} {tex}{w_B} = \frac{{\Delta {T_f} \times {M_B} \times {w_A}}}{{{k_f} \times 1000}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{10K \times 62g\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 5500g}}{{1.86\,K\,kg\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 1000g\,k{g^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} {tex}{w_B} = \,1833.33g\, = \,1.833\,kg{/tex}
solution: Given, w B = 0.520 g, w A = 80.2 g, {tex}K_b = 0.52 \;{Km}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}{M_2}({C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}) = (6 \times 12 )+( 12 \times 1) +( 6 \times 16){/tex} {tex}= \,180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_b} = \frac{{{K_b} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {w_A}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.52K{m^{ - 1}} \times 0.520g \times 1000g\,kg{\,^{ - 1}}}}{{180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 80.2g}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_b} = 0.0187K{/tex} Boiling point of solution {tex}({{\rm{T}}_b}) = T_b^o + \Delta {T_b}{/tex} {tex} = 373K + 0.0187K{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_b} = 373.019K{/tex} Given {tex}{w_A} = 5.5kg\, = \,5.5 \times 1000 = 5500g{/tex} {tex}{M_B} = \,62\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta T_f=0^oC-(-10^oC)=10^0C/\,10K{/tex} {tex}{K_f} = 1.86\,K\,kg\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} As we know, {tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {w_A}}}{/tex} {tex}{w_B} = \frac{{\Delta {T_f} \times {M_B} \times {w_A}}}{{{k_f} \times 1000}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{10K \times 62g\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 5500g}}{{1.86\,K\,kg\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 1000g\,k{g^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} {tex}{w_B} = \,1833.33g\, = \,1.833\,kg{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Given, wB = 0.520 g, wA = 80.2 g,
    {tex}K_b = 0.52 \;{Km}^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}{M_2}({C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}) = (6 \times 12 )+( 12 \times 1) +( 6 \times 16){/tex}
    {tex}= \,180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {T_b} = \frac{{{K_b} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {w_A}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{0.52K{m^{ - 1}} \times 0.520g \times 1000g\,kg{\,^{ - 1}}}}{{180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 80.2g}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {T_b} = 0.0187K{/tex}
    Boiling point of solution {tex}({{\rm{T}}_b}) = T_b^o + \Delta {T_b}{/tex}
    {tex} = 373K + 0.0187K{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta {T_b} = 373.019K{/tex}
  2. Given{tex}{w_A} = 5.5kg\, = \,5.5 \times 1000 = 5500g{/tex}
    {tex}{M_B} = \,62\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta T_f=0^oC-(-10^oC)=10^0C/\,10K{/tex}
    {tex}{K_f} = 1.86\,K\,kg\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
    As we know,
    {tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {w_A}}}{/tex}
    {tex}{w_B} = \frac{{\Delta {T_f} \times {M_B} \times {w_A}}}{{{k_f} \times 1000}}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{10K \times 62g\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 5500g}}{{1.86\,K\,kg\,mo{l^{ - 1}} \times 1000g\,k{g^{ - 1}}}}{/tex}
    {tex}{w_B} = \,1833.33g\, = \,1.833\,kg{/tex}

question_html: Calculate the freezing point of a solution containing 0.520 g glucose (C6H12O6) dissolved in 80.20 g of water. (For water Kf = 1.86 K kg mol-1)
question_text: Calculate the freezing point of a solution containing 0.520 g glucose (C 6 H 12 O 6 ) dissolved in 80.20 g of water. (For water K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: M B =Molecular mass of glucose, C 6 H 12 O 6 {tex} = (6\times12 )+(12\times1)+6\times16)=180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} w B = mas of glucose = 0.520g W A = mass of water = 80.20g K f = 1.86KKg/mol {tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {w_A}}}= \frac{{1.86 \times 0.520 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 80.20}}{/tex} = 0.0669
solution: M B =Molecular mass of glucose, C 6 H 12 O 6 {tex} = (6\times12 )+(12\times1)+6\times16)=180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex} w B = mas of glucose = 0.520g W A = mass of water = 80.20g K f = 1.86KKg/mol {tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {w_A}}}= \frac{{1.86 \times 0.520 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 80.20}}{/tex} = 0.0669
solution_html:
MB =Molecular mass of glucose, C6H12O6
{tex} = (6\times12 )+(12\times1)+6\times16)=180\,g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
wB = mas of glucose = 0.520g
WA = mass of water = 80.20g
Kf = 1.86KKg/mol 
{tex}\Delta {T_f} = \frac{{{K_f} \times {w_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {w_A}}}= \frac{{1.86 \times 0.520 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 80.20}}{/tex}
= 0.0669

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What mass of ethylene glycol (molar mass 62.0 g mol-1) must be added to 5.50 kg of water to lower the freezing point of water from 0°C to - 10.0°C? (Kf for water = 1.86 K kg mol-1)
question_text: What mass of ethylene glycol (molar mass 62.0 g mol -1 ) must be added to 5.50 kg of water to lower the freezing point of water from 0°C to - 10.0°C? (K f for water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: Given, M 2 (ethylene glycol) = 62 g mol -1 , W 1 = 5.50 kg = 5500 g, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 10 K [0 ∘ C - (-10 ∘ C) = 10 ∘ C = 10K] and K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \cdot W_{2} \cdot 1000}{M_{2} W_{1}}{/tex} W 1 = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f} \cdot M_{2} W_{1}}{K_{f} 1000}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10 \mathrm{K} \times 62 \ \mathrm{g} \mathrm \ {mol}^{-1} \times 5500 \mathrm{g}}{1.86 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000}{/tex} W 2 = 1833.33 g = 1.833 kg
solution: Given, M 2 (ethylene glycol) = 62 g mol -1 , W 1 = 5.50 kg = 5500 g, {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 10 K [0 ∘ C - (-10 ∘ C) = 10 ∘ C = 10K] and K f = 1.86 K kg mol -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \cdot W_{2} \cdot 1000}{M_{2} W_{1}}{/tex} W 1 = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f} \cdot M_{2} W_{1}}{K_{f} 1000}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10 \mathrm{K} \times 62 \ \mathrm{g} \mathrm \ {mol}^{-1} \times 5500 \mathrm{g}}{1.86 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000}{/tex} W 2 = 1833.33 g = 1.833 kg
solution_html:
Given, M2 (ethylene glycol) = 62 g mol-1,
W1 = 5.50 kg = 5500 g, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 10 K [0∘C - (-10∘C) = 10∘C = 10K]
and Kf = 1.86 K kg mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \cdot W_{2} \cdot 1000}{M_{2} W_{1}}{/tex}
W1 = {tex}\frac{\Delta T_{f} \cdot M_{2} W_{1}}{K_{f} 1000}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{10 \mathrm{K} \times 62 \ \mathrm{g} \mathrm \ {mol}^{-1} \times 5500 \mathrm{g}}{1.86 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 1000}{/tex}
W2 = 1833.33 g = 1.833 kg

question_html: 0.1 mole of acetic acid was dissolved in 1 kg of benzene. Depression in freezing point of benzene was determined to be 0.256 K. What conclusion can you draw about the state of the solute in solution? (Given, Kf for benzene = 5.12 K kg mol-1)
question_text: 0.1 mole of acetic acid was dissolved in 1 kg of benzene. Depression in freezing point of benzene was determined to be 0.256 K. What conclusion can you draw about the state of the solute in solution? (Given, K f for benzene = 5.12 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: Given, W 1 = 1kg = 1000 g (benzene) {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.256 K, K f = 5.12 K kg mol −1 0.1 mol of acetic acid= 0 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 6.0 g (W 2 ) [ {tex}\because{/tex} Molar mass of CH 3 COOH = 60 g mol -1 ] M 2 = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{f} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{512 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 6.0 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{0.256 \mathrm{K} \times 1000 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 120 g mol -1 i = {tex}\frac{M_{2} \text { (Calculated) }}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{60}{120}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0.5 The value of i is 0.5. It is less than l Hence, the solute, i.e. CH 3 COOH gets dimense in benzene.
solution: Given, W 1 = 1kg = 1000 g (benzene) {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.256 K, K f = 5.12 K kg mol −1 0.1 mol of acetic acid= 0 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 6.0 g (W 2 ) [ {tex}\because{/tex} Molar mass of CH 3 COOH = 60 g mol -1 ] M 2 = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{f} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{512 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 6.0 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{0.256 \mathrm{K} \times 1000 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 120 g mol -1 i = {tex}\frac{M_{2} \text { (Calculated) }}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{60}{120}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0.5 The value of i is 0.5. It is less than l Hence, the solute, i.e. CH 3 COOH gets dimense in benzene.
solution_html:
Given, W1 = 1kg = 1000 g (benzene)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 0.256 K,
Kf = 5.12 K kg mol−1
0.1 mol of acetic acid= 0 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 6.0 g (W2) [{tex}\because{/tex} Molar mass of CH3COOH = 60 g mol-1]
M2 = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{f} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{512 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 6.0 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{0.256 \mathrm{K} \times 1000 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
= 120 g mol-1
i = {tex}\frac{M_{2} \text { (Calculated) }}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{60}{120}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0.5
The value of i is 0.5. It is less than l Hence, the solute, i.e. CH3COOH gets dimense in benzene.

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
A solution prepared by dissolving 1.25 g of oil of wintergreen (methyl salicylate) in 99.0 g of benzene has a boiling point of 80.31°C. Determine the molar mass of this compound.
(Boiling point of pure benzene = 80.10°C and Kb for benzene = 2.53°C kg mol-1)
question_text: A solution prepared by dissolving 1.25 g of oil of wintergreen (methyl salicylate) in 99.0 g of benzene has a boiling point of 80.31°C. Determine the molar mass of this compound. (Boiling point of pure benzene = 80.10°C and K b for benzene = 2.53°C kg mol -1 )
explanation: Given, W 2 = 125 g W 1 = 990g K b = 2.53 o C kg mo -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = T b - T {tex}_b^o{/tex} = 80.31 - 8010 = 0.21 o C Use the formula M 2 = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.53 \times 1.25 \times 1000}{0.21 \times 99.0}{/tex} M 2 = 152 g mol -1 approx.
solution: Given, W 2 = 125 g W 1 = 990g K b = 2.53 o C kg mo -1 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T b = T b - T {tex}_b^o{/tex} = 80.31 - 8010 = 0.21 o C Use the formula M 2 = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.53 \times 1.25 \times 1000}{0.21 \times 99.0}{/tex} M 2 = 152 g mol -1 approx.
solution_html:
Given, W2 = 125 g
W1 = 990g
Kb = 2.53oC kg mo-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = Tb - T{tex}_b^o{/tex} = 80.31 - 8010 = 0.21oC
Use the formula
M2 = {tex}\frac{K_{b} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{b} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2.53 \times 1.25 \times 1000}{0.21 \times 99.0}{/tex}
M2 = 152 g mol-1 approx.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Define the terms osmosis and osmotic pressure. What is the advantage of using osmotic pressure as compared to other colligative properties for the determination of molar masses of solutes in solutions?
question_text: Define the terms osmosis and osmotic pressure. What is the advantage of using osmotic pressure as compared to other colligative properties for the determination of molar masses of solutes in solutions?
explanation: Osmosis : The movement of solvent molecules from a less concentrated solution to a more concentrated solution through a semipermeable membrane is called osmosis. Osmotic Pressure: Osmotic pressure is the extra pressure that is applied to the solution just to prevent the flow of solvent into the solution through a semipermeable membrane. The osmotic pressure method has an advantage over other colligative properties because Pressure measurement is around room temperature and the molarity of the solution is used instead of molality. Its magnitude is large as compared to other colligative properties even for very dilute solutions.
solution: Osmosis : The movement of solvent molecules from a less concentrated solution to a more concentrated solution through a semipermeable membrane is called osmosis. Osmotic Pressure: Osmotic pressure is the extra pressure that is applied to the solution just to prevent the flow of solvent into the solution through a semipermeable membrane. The osmotic pressure method has an advantage over other colligative properties because Pressure measurement is around room temperature and the molarity of the solution is used instead of molality. Its magnitude is large as compared to other colligative properties even for very dilute solutions.
solution_html:
Osmosis: The movement of solvent molecules from a less concentrated solution to a more concentrated solution through a semipermeable membrane is called osmosis.
Osmotic Pressure: Osmotic pressure is the extra pressure that is applied to the solution just to prevent the flow of solvent into the solution through a semipermeable membrane.
The osmotic pressure method has an advantage over other colligative properties because

  1. Pressure measurement is around room temperature and the molarity of the solution is used instead of molality.
  2. Its magnitude is large as compared to other colligative properties even for very dilute solutions.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Outer hard shells of two eggs are removed. One of the eggs is placed in pure water and the other is placed in saturated solution of sodium chloride. What will be observed and why?
question_text: Outer hard shells of two eggs are removed. One of the eggs is placed in pure water and the other is placed in saturated solution of sodium chloride. What will be observed and why?
explanation: The egg placed in pure water will swell because the concentration of proteins is high inside the egg as compared to water. Therefore, water diffuses through the semi-permeable membrane of egg and egg swells. On the other hand, the egg placed in sodium chloride solution will shrink due to osmosis, water will move out of the egg membrane, thereby, shrinking the egg.
solution: The egg placed in pure water will swell because the concentration of proteins is high inside the egg as compared to water. Therefore, water diffuses through the semi-permeable membrane of egg and egg swells. On the other hand, the egg placed in sodium chloride solution will shrink due to osmosis, water will move out of the egg membrane, thereby, shrinking the egg.
solution_html:
The egg placed in pure water will swell because the concentration of proteins is high inside the egg as compared to water. Therefore, water diffuses through the semi-permeable membrane of egg and egg swells.
On the other hand, the egg placed in sodium chloride solution will shrink due to osmosis, water will move out of the egg membrane, thereby, shrinking the egg.

question_html: What is meant by molality of the solution?
question_text: What is meant by molality of the solution?
explanation: Molality is the number of moles of solute dissolved in 1 kg or 1000 g of the solvent. it is denoted as 'm'. thus, a solution contains one gram mole of solute dissolved in 1Kg of water and regarded as 1 molal solution.
solution: Molality is the number of moles of solute dissolved in 1 kg or 1000 g of the solvent. it is denoted as 'm'. thus, a solution contains one gram mole of solute dissolved in 1Kg of water and regarded as 1 molal solution.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: What concentration of nitrogen should be present in a glass of water at room temperature. Assume a temperature of 25°C, a total pressure of atmosphere and mole fraction of nitrogen in air of 0.78 [KH for nitrogen = 8.42 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-7 M/mm Hg]
question_text: What concentration of nitrogen should be present in a glass of water at room temperature. Assume a temperature of 25°C, a total pressure of atmosphere and mole fraction of nitrogen in air of 0.78 [K H for nitrogen = 8.42 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -7 M/mm Hg]
explanation: P N2 = 1 atmosphere P N2 = K H {tex} \times {/tex} N 2 By Henry's law, {tex}x{N_2} = \frac{{{P_{N2}}}}{{{K_H}}} = \frac{1}{{8.42 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{/tex} {tex}x{N_2} = \frac{{n({N_2})}}{{n({N_2}) + n({H_2}O)}}{/tex} {tex}x{N_2} = \frac{{n({N_2})}}{{n({H_2}O)}}{/tex} n(N 2 ) = xN 2 - n(H 2 O) {tex}n{N_2} = x{N_2} \cdot \frac{{1000}}{{18}} = \frac{1}{{8.42 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}} \times 55.5{/tex} = 6.59 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 7 moles Hence concentration of nitrogen = 6.59 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 7 mol L -1
solution: P N2 = 1 atmosphere P N2 = K H {tex} \times {/tex} N 2 By Henry's law, {tex}x{N_2} = \frac{{{P_{N2}}}}{{{K_H}}} = \frac{1}{{8.42 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{/tex} {tex}x{N_2} = \frac{{n({N_2})}}{{n({N_2}) + n({H_2}O)}}{/tex} {tex}x{N_2} = \frac{{n({N_2})}}{{n({H_2}O)}}{/tex} n(N 2 ) = xN 2 - n(H 2 O) {tex}n{N_2} = x{N_2} \cdot \frac{{1000}}{{18}} = \frac{1}{{8.42 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}} \times 55.5{/tex} = 6.59 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 7 moles Hence concentration of nitrogen = 6.59 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 7 mol L -1
solution_html:
PN2 = 1 atmosphere
PN2 = KH {tex} \times {/tex} N2
By Henry's law,
{tex}x{N_2} = \frac{{{P_{N2}}}}{{{K_H}}} = \frac{1}{{8.42 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{/tex}
{tex}x{N_2} = \frac{{n({N_2})}}{{n({N_2}) + n({H_2}O)}}{/tex}
{tex}x{N_2} = \frac{{n({N_2})}}{{n({H_2}O)}}{/tex}
n(N2) = xN2 - n(H2O)
{tex}n{N_2} = x{N_2} \cdot \frac{{1000}}{{18}} = \frac{1}{{8.42 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}} \times 55.5{/tex}
= 6.59 {tex} \times {/tex} 107 moles
Hence concentration of nitrogen = 6.59 {tex} \times {/tex} 107 mol L-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
100 mg of a protein is dissolved in just enough water to make 10.0 mL of solution. If this solution has an osmotic pressure of 13.3 mm Hg at 25°C what is the molar mass of the protein?
(R = 0.0821 L at mol-1 K-1 760 mm Hg = 1 atm )
question_text: 100 mg of a protein is dissolved in just enough water to make 10.0 mL of solution. If this solution has an osmotic pressure of 13.3 mm Hg at 25°C what is the molar mass of the protein? (R = 0.0821 L at mol -1 K -1 760 mm Hg = 1 atm )
solution_html:
Here W = 100 mg = 0.100 g
V = 10.0 mL = 0.01 L
{tex}\pi = 13.33mm\,Hg = \frac{{13.3}}{{760}}atm{/tex}
T = 25°C = 25 + 273 = 298 K
R = 0.0821 L atm mol-1 K-1
M = ?
Molar Mass M {tex} = \frac{{WRT}}{{\pi V}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.1 \times 0.0821 \times 298}}{{\frac{{13.3}}{{760}} \times 0.01}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.1 \times 0.0821 \times 298 \times 760}}{{13.3 \times 0.01}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1859 \times 4008}}{{0.133}}{/tex} = 13980.4 g mol-1

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Calculate the freezing point depression expected for 0.0711 m aqueous solution of Na2SO4. If this solution actually freezes at -0.320'C, what would be the value of Van't Hoff factor? (Kf for water = 1.86oC {tex}{mol}^{-1}{/tex})
question_text: Calculate the freezing point depression expected for 0.0711 m aqueous solution of Na 2 SO 4 . If this solution actually freezes at -0.320'C, what would be the value of Van't Hoff factor? (K f for water = 1.86 o C {tex}{mol}^{-1}{/tex} )
solution_html:
Given, {tex}m = 0.0711\,m,\,\Delta {T_f} = 0.320,\,{K_f} = {1.86^0}C\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {T_f} = i\,{K_f}\,m{/tex}
{tex}i\, = \,\frac{{\Delta {T_f}}}{{{K_f} \times m}} = \frac{{0.320}}{{1.86 \times 0.0711}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{0.320}}{{0.132}} = 2.42{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Calculate the freezing point of a solution containing 18 g glucose, C12H22O6, and 68.4 g sucrose, C12H22O11 in 200 g of water. The freezing point of pure water is 273 K and Kf for water is 1.86 K kg mol-1.
question_text: Calculate the freezing point of a solution containing 18 g glucose, C 12 H 22 O 6, and 68.4 g sucrose, C 12 H 22 O 11 in 200 g of water. The freezing point of pure water is 273 K and K f for water is 1.86 K kg mol -1 .
explanation: The molar mass of glucose, C 6 H 12 O 6 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 180 g mol -1 Molar mass of sucrose, C 12 H 22 O 11 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 =342 g mol -1 n glucose = {tex}\frac{18}{180}{/tex} = 0.1, n glucose = {tex}\frac{68.4}{342}{/tex} = 0.2 Total moles of solute, n 2 = 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.3 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because n_{2}=\frac{w_{2}}{M_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times n_{2} \times 1000}{W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.86 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 0.3 \ \mathrm{mol} \times 1000}{200 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 2.79 k Freezing point of aqueous solution = 273 - 2.79 = 270.21 K
solution: The molar mass of glucose, C 6 H 12 O 6 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 180 g mol -1 Molar mass of sucrose, C 12 H 22 O 11 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 =342 g mol -1 n glucose = {tex}\frac{18}{180}{/tex} = 0.1, n glucose = {tex}\frac{68.4}{342}{/tex} = 0.2 Total moles of solute, n 2 = 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.3 {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because n_{2}=\frac{w_{2}}{M_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times n_{2} \times 1000}{W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.86 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 0.3 \ \mathrm{mol} \times 1000}{200 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 2.79 k Freezing point of aqueous solution = 273 - 2.79 = 270.21 K
solution_html:
The molar mass of glucose, C6H12O6
= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 16
= 180 g mol-1
Molar mass of sucrose, C12H22O11
= 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 16
=342 g mol-1
nglucose = {tex}\frac{18}{180}{/tex} = 0.1, nglucose = {tex}\frac{68.4}{342}{/tex} = 0.2
Total moles of solute, n2 = 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.3
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} W_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because n_{2}=\frac{w_{2}}{M_{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times n_{2} \times 1000}{W_{1}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1.86 \ \mathrm{K} \ \mathrm{kg} \ \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 0.3 \ \mathrm{mol} \times 1000}{200 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
= 2.79 k
Freezing point of aqueous solution
= 273 - 2.79 = 270.21 K

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Distinguish between the terms molality and molarity. Under what condition are the molarity and molality of a solution nearly the same?
question_text: Distinguish between the terms molality and molarity. Under what condition are the molarity and molality of a solution nearly the same?
explanation: Molality (m) : It is the number of moles of the solute dissolved per kilogram (kg) of the solvent. It is denoted by m. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Molality (m) {tex} = \frac{{{\text{No}}{\text{. of Moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Mass of solvent in kg}}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \,m = \frac{{{W_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {W_1}}}{/tex} Where W 1 = mass of solvent W 2 = mass of solute M 2 = Molar mass of solute . Unit is mol/Kg Molarity (m): It is the number of moles of the solute dissolved per litre of the solution. It is denoted by M. Molarity {tex} = \frac{{{\text{No}}{\text{. of moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Volume of solution in litre}}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \;M = \frac{{{W_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {V_1}}}{/tex} where W 2 = mass of solvent M 2 = molar mass of solute V = Volume of solution. Unit is mol/L The molarity and molality of a solution will be nearly same if the mass of solvent is nearly equal to the volume of solution.
solution: Molality (m) : It is the number of moles of the solute dissolved per kilogram (kg) of the solvent. It is denoted by m. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Molality (m) {tex} = \frac{{{\text{No}}{\text{. of Moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Mass of solvent in kg}}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \,m = \frac{{{W_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {W_1}}}{/tex} Where W 1 = mass of solvent W 2 = mass of solute M 2 = Molar mass of solute . Unit is mol/Kg Molarity (m): It is the number of moles of the solute dissolved per litre of the solution. It is denoted by M. Molarity {tex} = \frac{{{\text{No}}{\text{. of moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Volume of solution in litre}}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \;M = \frac{{{W_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {V_1}}}{/tex} where W 2 = mass of solvent M 2 = molar mass of solute V = Volume of solution. Unit is mol/L The molarity and molality of a solution will be nearly same if the mass of solvent is nearly equal to the volume of solution.
solution_html:
Molality (m) : It is the number of moles of the solute dissolved per kilogram (kg) of the solvent. It is denoted by m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Molality (m) {tex} = \frac{{{\text{No}}{\text{. of Moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Mass of solvent in kg}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \,m = \frac{{{W_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {W_1}}}{/tex}
Where W1 = mass of solvent
W2 = mass of solute
M2 = Molar mass of solute . Unit is mol/Kg
Molarity (m): It is the number of moles of the solute dissolved per litre of the solution. It is denoted by M.
Molarity {tex} = \frac{{{\text{No}}{\text{. of moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Volume of solution in litre}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \;M = \frac{{{W_2} \times 1000}}{{{M_2} \times {V_1}}}{/tex}
where W2 = mass of solvent
M2 = molar mass of solute
V = Volume of solution. Unit is mol/L
The molarity and molality of a solution will be nearly same if the mass of solvent is nearly equal to the volume of solution.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The solubility of pure nitrogen gas at {tex}25^0\;C{/tex} and 1 atm is {tex}6.8 \times {10}^{-4}\; mol\; {L}^{-1}{/tex}. What is the concentration of nitrogen dissolved in water under atmospheric conditions? The partial pressure of nitrogen gas in the atmosphere is 0.78 atm.
question_text: The solubility of pure nitrogen gas at {tex}25^0\;C{/tex} and 1 atm is {tex}6.8 \times {10}^{-4}\; mol\; {L}^{-1}{/tex} . What is the concentration of nitrogen dissolved in water under atmospheric conditions? The partial pressure of nitrogen gas in the atmosphere is 0.78 atm.
explanation: According to Henry's law solubility(S) = {tex}{{\rm{K}}_H} \times p{/tex} so, at {tex}25^\circ C,6.8 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}} = {K_H} \times 1\,atm\,\,\,...(i){/tex} Similarly, at {tex}25^0\; C{/tex} , solubility of nitrogen gas {tex}S = \,{K_H} \times 0.78atm\,\,\,...(ii){/tex} Dividing(i) by (ii), we have {tex}\frac{{6.8 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}}}{s} = \frac{{{K_H} \times 1atm}}{{{K_H} \times 0.78atm}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \,\,\,s = \,6.8 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 0.78{/tex} {tex}s\, = \,5.304{\kern 1pt} \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution: According to Henry's law solubility(S) = {tex}{{\rm{K}}_H} \times p{/tex} so, at {tex}25^\circ C,6.8 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}} = {K_H} \times 1\,atm\,\,\,...(i){/tex} Similarly, at {tex}25^0\; C{/tex} , solubility of nitrogen gas {tex}S = \,{K_H} \times 0.78atm\,\,\,...(ii){/tex} Dividing(i) by (ii), we have {tex}\frac{{6.8 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}}}{s} = \frac{{{K_H} \times 1atm}}{{{K_H} \times 0.78atm}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \,\,\,s = \,6.8 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 0.78{/tex} {tex}s\, = \,5.304{\kern 1pt} \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to Henry's law
solubility(S) = {tex}{{\rm{K}}_H} \times p{/tex}
so, at {tex}25^\circ C,6.8 \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}} = {K_H} \times 1\,atm\,\,\,...(i){/tex}
Similarly, at {tex}25^0\; C{/tex}, solubility of nitrogen gas
{tex}S = \,{K_H} \times 0.78atm\,\,\,...(ii){/tex}
Dividing(i) by (ii), we have
{tex}\frac{{6.8 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}}}{s} = \frac{{{K_H} \times 1atm}}{{{K_H} \times 0.78atm}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \,\,\,s = \,6.8 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 0.78{/tex}
{tex}s\, = \,5.304{\kern 1pt} \times {10^{ - 4}}mol\,{L^{ - 1}}{/tex}

question_html: Henry's law constant for CO2 in water is 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 Pa at 298 K. Calculate the quantity of CO2 in 500 mL of soda water when packed under 2.5 atm CO2  pressure at 298 K.
question_text: Henry's law constant for CO 2 in water is 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 Pa at 298 K. Calculate the quantity of CO 2 in 500 mL of soda water when packed under 2.5 atm CO 2 pressure at 298 K.
explanation: It is given that: KH = {tex}1.67\times10^8Pa{/tex} {tex}{P_{c{o_2}}}{/tex} = 2.5 atm = {tex}2.5\times1.01325\times10^5Pa{/tex} {tex}=2.533125\times10^5Pa{/tex} According to Henry's law: {tex}P_{CO_2}=K_{H\;}\;x{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{Pc{o_2}}}{{{K_H}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2.533125 \times {{10}^5}}}{{1.67 \times {{10}^8}}}{/tex} = 0.00152 We can write, {tex}x = \frac{{{n_{co}}_{_2}}}{{{n_{c{o_2}}} + {n_{{H_2}O}}}} \approx \frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}O}}}}{/tex} [Since, {tex}{n_{c{o_2}}}{/tex} is negligible as compared to {tex}{n_{{H_2}O}}{/tex} ] In 500 mL of soda water, the volume of water = 500 mL [Neglecting the amount of soda present] We can write: 500 mL of water = 500 g of water = {tex}\frac{{500}}{{18}}{/tex} mol of water = 27.78 mol of water Now, {tex}\frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}O}}}} = x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{27.78}} = 0.00152{/tex} {tex}{n_{c{o_2}}} = 0.042\,mol{/tex} Hence, quantity of CO 2 in 500 mL of soda water = {tex}(0.042 \times 44)g{/tex} = 1.848 g
solution: It is given that: KH = {tex}1.67\times10^8Pa{/tex} {tex}{P_{c{o_2}}}{/tex} = 2.5 atm = {tex}2.5\times1.01325\times10^5Pa{/tex} {tex}=2.533125\times10^5Pa{/tex} According to Henry's law: {tex}P_{CO_2}=K_{H\;}\;x{/tex} {tex}x = \frac{{Pc{o_2}}}{{{K_H}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2.533125 \times {{10}^5}}}{{1.67 \times {{10}^8}}}{/tex} = 0.00152 We can write, {tex}x = \frac{{{n_{co}}_{_2}}}{{{n_{c{o_2}}} + {n_{{H_2}O}}}} \approx \frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}O}}}}{/tex} [Since, {tex}{n_{c{o_2}}}{/tex} is negligible as compared to {tex}{n_{{H_2}O}}{/tex} ] In 500 mL of soda water, the volume of water = 500 mL [Neglecting the amount of soda present] We can write: 500 mL of water = 500 g of water = {tex}\frac{{500}}{{18}}{/tex} mol of water = 27.78 mol of water Now, {tex}\frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}O}}}} = x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{27.78}} = 0.00152{/tex} {tex}{n_{c{o_2}}} = 0.042\,mol{/tex} Hence, quantity of CO 2 in 500 mL of soda water = {tex}(0.042 \times 44)g{/tex} = 1.848 g
solution_html:
It is given that:
KH = {tex}1.67\times10^8Pa{/tex}
{tex}{P_{c{o_2}}}{/tex} = 2.5 atm = {tex}2.5\times1.01325\times10^5Pa{/tex}
{tex}=2.533125\times10^5Pa{/tex}
According to Henry's law:
{tex}P_{CO_2}=K_{H\;}\;x{/tex}
{tex}x = \frac{{Pc{o_2}}}{{{K_H}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{2.533125 \times {{10}^5}}}{{1.67 \times {{10}^8}}}{/tex}
= 0.00152
We can write, {tex}x = \frac{{{n_{co}}_{_2}}}{{{n_{c{o_2}}} + {n_{{H_2}O}}}} \approx \frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}O}}}}{/tex}
[Since, {tex}{n_{c{o_2}}}{/tex}is negligible as compared to {tex}{n_{{H_2}O}}{/tex}]
In 500 mL of soda water, the volume of water = 500 mL
[Neglecting the amount of soda present]
We can write:
500 mL of water = 500 g of water
={tex}\frac{{500}}{{18}}{/tex} mol of water
= 27.78 mol of water
Now, {tex}\frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{{n_{{H_2}O}}}} = x{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{{n_{c{o_2}}}}}{{27.78}} = 0.00152{/tex}
{tex}{n_{c{o_2}}} = 0.042\,mol{/tex}
Hence, quantity of CO2 in 500 mL of soda water = {tex}(0.042 \times 44)g{/tex}
= 1.848 g

subject: Chemistry
question_html: The depression in freezing point of water observed for the same amount of acetic acid, trichloroacetic acid and trifluoroacetic acid increases in the order given above. Explain briefly.
question_text: The depression in freezing point of water observed for the same amount of acetic acid, trichloroacetic acid and trifluoroacetic acid increases in the order given above. Explain briefly.
explanation: Among H, Cl, and F, H is least electronegative while F is most electronegative. Then, F can withdraw electrons towards itself due to -I effect more than Cl and H. Thus, trifluoroacetic acid can easily lose {tex}H^+{/tex} ions i.e., trifluoroacetic acid ionizes to the largest extent. Now, the more ions produced, the greater is the depression of the freezing point. Hence, the depression in the freezing point increases in the order: Acetic acid < trichloroacetic acid < trifluoroacetic acid.
solution: Among H, Cl, and F, H is least electronegative while F is most electronegative. Then, F can withdraw electrons towards itself due to -I effect more than Cl and H. Thus, trifluoroacetic acid can easily lose {tex}H^+{/tex} ions i.e., trifluoroacetic acid ionizes to the largest extent. Now, the more ions produced, the greater is the depression of the freezing point. Hence, the depression in the freezing point increases in the order: Acetic acid < trichloroacetic acid < trifluoroacetic acid.
solution_html:
Among H, Cl, and F, H is least electronegative while F is most electronegative. Then, F can withdraw electrons towards itself due to -I effect more than Cl and H. Thus, trifluoroacetic acid can easily lose {tex}H^+{/tex} ions i.e., trifluoroacetic acid ionizes to the largest extent. Now, the more ions produced, the greater is the depression of the freezing point. Hence, the depression in the freezing point increases in the order:
Acetic acid < trichloroacetic acid < trifluoroacetic acid.

question_html: Calculate the temperature at which a solution containing 54 g of glucose( C6H12O6 )in 250 g of water will freeze. ( Kf of water = 1.86 K kg mol-1)
question_text: Calculate the temperature at which a solution containing 54 g of glucose( C 6 H 12 O 6 )in 250 g of water will freeze. ( K f of water = 1.86 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: Molecular mass of glucose {tex}{M_B=\;(6\;\times12)\;+(12\;\times\;1)\;+\;(6\;\times\;16)\;=\;180g/mol}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} = \frac{{{k_{\text{f}}} \times {W_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {W_B}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.86 \times 54 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 250}}{/tex} = 2.23 Freezing point of solution {tex}{T_f\;={T^0}_f\;-\triangle T_f\;}{/tex} = 273 - 2.23 =270.77K
solution: Molecular mass of glucose {tex}{M_B=\;(6\;\times12)\;+(12\;\times\;1)\;+\;(6\;\times\;16)\;=\;180g/mol}{/tex} {tex}\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} = \frac{{{k_{\text{f}}} \times {W_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {W_B}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1.86 \times 54 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 250}}{/tex} = 2.23 Freezing point of solution {tex}{T_f\;={T^0}_f\;-\triangle T_f\;}{/tex} = 273 - 2.23 =270.77K
solution_html:
Molecular mass of glucose
{tex}{M_B=\;(6\;\times12)\;+(12\;\times\;1)\;+\;(6\;\times\;16)\;=\;180g/mol}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta {T_{\text{f}}} = \frac{{{k_{\text{f}}} \times {W_B} \times 1000}}{{{M_B} \times {W_B}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1.86 \times 54 \times 1000}}{{180 \times 250}}{/tex} = 2.23
Freezing point of solution{tex}{T_f\;={T^0}_f\;-\triangle T_f\;}{/tex}
= 273 - 2.23 =270.77K

question_html: A solution containing 8 g of a substance in 100 g of diethyl ether boils at {tex}36.86^0\;C{/tex}, whereas pure ether boils at {tex}35.60^0\;C{/tex}. Determine the molecular mass of the solute. [For ether, {tex}K_b{/tex} = {tex} 2.02 K kg {mol}^{-1}{/tex}]
question_text: A solution containing 8 g of a substance in 100 g of diethyl ether boils at {tex}36.86^0\;C{/tex} , whereas pure ether boils at {tex}35.60^0\;C{/tex} . Determine the molecular mass of the solute. [For ether, {tex}K_b{/tex} = {tex} 2.02 K kg {mol}^{-1}{/tex} ]
explanation: Given, {tex}{w_B} = 8g,{w_A} = 100g,{K_b} = 2.02Kg\,mo{l^{ - 1}},\,{M_B} = ?{/tex} Elevation in boiling point ( {tex}\Delta {{\rm{T}}_b}{/tex} ) {tex} = {36.86^0}C - {35.60^0}C = {1.26^0}C{/tex} We know that {tex}M_B=\frac{1000\times w_B\times K_b}{\Delta T_b\times w_A}=\frac{1000\times8\times2.02}{1.02\;\times\;100}{/tex} {tex}{M_B} = 158.43g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}{w_B} = 8g,{w_A} = 100g,{K_b} = 2.02Kg\,mo{l^{ - 1}},\,{M_B} = ?{/tex} Elevation in boiling point ( {tex}\Delta {{\rm{T}}_b}{/tex} ) {tex} = {36.86^0}C - {35.60^0}C = {1.26^0}C{/tex} We know that {tex}M_B=\frac{1000\times w_B\times K_b}{\Delta T_b\times w_A}=\frac{1000\times8\times2.02}{1.02\;\times\;100}{/tex} {tex}{M_B} = 158.43g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,
{tex}{w_B} = 8g,{w_A} = 100g,{K_b} = 2.02Kg\,mo{l^{ - 1}},\,{M_B} = ?{/tex}
Elevation in boiling point ({tex}\Delta {{\rm{T}}_b}{/tex})
{tex} = {36.86^0}C - {35.60^0}C = {1.26^0}C{/tex}
We know that
{tex}M_B=\frac{1000\times w_B\times K_b}{\Delta T_b\times w_A}=\frac{1000\times8\times2.02}{1.02\;\times\;100}{/tex}
{tex}{M_B} = 158.43g\,mo{l^{ - 1}}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Calculate the mass of a non-volatile solute (molar mass 40 g mol-1) which should be dissolved in 114 g octane to reduce its vapour pressure to 80%.
question_text: Calculate the mass of a non-volatile solute (molar mass 40 g mol -1 ) which should be dissolved in 114 g octane to reduce its vapour pressure to 80%.
explanation: Let the vapour pressure of pure octane be {tex}p^0_1{/tex} Then, the vapour pressure of the octane after dissolving the non-volatile solute is {tex}\frac{80}{100}{/tex} {tex}p^0_1{/tex} = 0.8 Molar mass of solute, M 2 = 40 g mol -1 Mass of octane, w 1 = 114 g Molar mass of octane, {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18}\right), M_1{/tex} = {tex}8\times 12+18 \times 1{/tex} =114 {tex}\mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex} Applying the relation, {tex}\frac{p_1^0-p_1}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2 \times M_1}{M_2 \times w_1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_1^0-\left(0.8 p_1\right)^0}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2 \times 114}{40 \times 114}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.2 p_1^0}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2}{40}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.2 = {tex}\frac{w_2}{40}{/tex} w 2 = 8g Hence, the required mass of the solute is 8g.
solution: Let the vapour pressure of pure octane be {tex}p^0_1{/tex} Then, the vapour pressure of the octane after dissolving the non-volatile solute is {tex}\frac{80}{100}{/tex} {tex}p^0_1{/tex} = 0.8 Molar mass of solute, M 2 = 40 g mol -1 Mass of octane, w 1 = 114 g Molar mass of octane, {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18}\right), M_1{/tex} = {tex}8\times 12+18 \times 1{/tex} =114 {tex}\mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex} Applying the relation, {tex}\frac{p_1^0-p_1}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2 \times M_1}{M_2 \times w_1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_1^0-\left(0.8 p_1\right)^0}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2 \times 114}{40 \times 114}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.2 p_1^0}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2}{40}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.2 = {tex}\frac{w_2}{40}{/tex} w 2 = 8g Hence, the required mass of the solute is 8g.
solution_html:
Let the vapour pressure of pure octane be {tex}p^0_1{/tex}
Then, the vapour pressure of the octane after dissolving the non-volatile solute is {tex}\frac{80}{100}{/tex} {tex}p^0_1{/tex} = 0.8
Molar mass of solute, M2 = 40 g mol-1
Mass of octane, w1 = 114 g
Molar mass of octane, {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18}\right), M_1{/tex} = {tex}8\times 12+18 \times 1{/tex}
=114 {tex}\mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
Applying the relation,
{tex}\frac{p_1^0-p_1}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2 \times M_1}{M_2 \times w_1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_1^0-\left(0.8 p_1\right)^0}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2 \times 114}{40 \times 114}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.2 p_1^0}{p_1^0}=\frac{w_2}{40}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0.2 = {tex}\frac{w_2}{40}{/tex}
 w2 = 8g
Hence, the required mass of the solute is 8g.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: State Raoult's law for solutions of volatile liquids. Taking suitable examples, explain the meaning of positive and negative deviations from Raoult's law.
question_text: State Raoult's law for solutions of volatile liquids. Taking suitable examples, explain the meaning of positive and negative deviations from Raoult's law.
explanation: Raoult's law For a solution of volatile liquids. the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in solution. Thus, for any component, partial vapour pressure, p {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = p o {tex}\chi{/tex} where p o is the vapour pressure of pure component and Xis the mole fraction of that component. For positive and negative deviation from Raoult's law:- In the case of positive deviation: A-B interactions are weaker than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure increases which result in positive deviation. e g Ethanol + Acetone and CS 2 + Acetone show positive deviation. For positive deviation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H (mixing) = Positive. In the case of negative deviation: A-B interactions are stronger than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure decreases which result in negative deviation. e.g. Phenol + Anime and Chloroform + Acetone show negative deviation. For negative deviation, H (mixing) = Negative
solution: Raoult's law For a solution of volatile liquids. the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in solution. Thus, for any component, partial vapour pressure, p {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = p o {tex}\chi{/tex} where p o is the vapour pressure of pure component and Xis the mole fraction of that component. For positive and negative deviation from Raoult's law:- In the case of positive deviation: A-B interactions are weaker than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure increases which result in positive deviation. e g Ethanol + Acetone and CS 2 + Acetone show positive deviation. For positive deviation, {tex}\Delta{/tex} H (mixing) = Positive. In the case of negative deviation: A-B interactions are stronger than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure decreases which result in negative deviation. e.g. Phenol + Anime and Chloroform + Acetone show negative deviation. For negative deviation, H (mixing) = Negative
solution_html:
Raoult's law For a solution of volatile liquids. the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in solution. Thus, for any component, partial vapour pressure,
p {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\chi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = po{tex}\chi{/tex}
where po is the vapour pressure of pure component and Xis the mole fraction of that component.
For positive and negative deviation from Raoult's law:-
In the case of positive deviation: A-B interactions are weaker than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure increases which result in positive deviation.
e g Ethanol + Acetone and CS2 + Acetone show positive deviation.
For positive deviation, {tex}\Delta{/tex}H (mixing) = Positive.
In the case of negative deviation: A-B interactions are stronger than A-A and B-B interactions. Due to this, vapour pressure decreases which result in negative deviation. e.g. Phenol + Anime and Chloroform + Acetone show negative deviation.
For negative deviation, H (mixing) =  Negative

question_html:
The boiling point elevation of 0.30 g acetic acid in 100 g benzene is 0.0633 K. Calculate the molar mass of acetic acid from this data. What conclusion can you draw about the molecular state of the solute in the solution?
(Given, Kb for benzene = 2.53 K kg mol-1)
question_text: The boiling point elevation of 0.30 g acetic acid in 100 g benzene is 0.0633 K. Calculate the molar mass of acetic acid from this data. What conclusion can you draw about the molecular state of the solute in the solution? (Given, Kb for benzene = 2.53 K kg mol -1 )
explanation: Given, W 1 = 1kg = 1000 g (benzene) {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.256 K, K f = 5.12 K kg mol −1 0.1 mol of acetic acid= 0 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 6.0 g (W 2 ) [ {tex}\because{/tex} Molar mass of CH 3 COOH = 60 g mol -1 ] M 2 = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{f} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{512 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 6.0 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{0.256 \mathrm{K} \times 1000 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 120 g mol -1 i = {tex}\frac{M_{2} \text { (Calculated) }}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{60}{120}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0.5 The value of i is 0.5. It is less than 1. Hence, the solute, i e. CH 3 COOH gets dimense in benzene i = 05.Here 1 <1,therefore the solute, acetic acid is associated in benzene.
solution: Given, W 1 = 1kg = 1000 g (benzene) {tex}\Delta{/tex} T f = 0.256 K, K f = 5.12 K kg mol −1 0.1 mol of acetic acid= 0 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 6.0 g (W 2 ) [ {tex}\because{/tex} Molar mass of CH 3 COOH = 60 g mol -1 ] M 2 = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{f} \times W_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{512 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 6.0 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{0.256 \mathrm{K} \times 1000 \mathrm{g}}{/tex} = 120 g mol -1 i = {tex}\frac{M_{2} \text { (Calculated) }}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{60}{120}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0.5 The value of i is 0.5. It is less than 1. Hence, the solute, i e. CH 3 COOH gets dimense in benzene i = 05.Here 1 <1,therefore the solute, acetic acid is associated in benzene.
solution_html:
Given, W1 = 1kg = 1000 g (benzene)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 0.256 K,
Kf = 5.12 K kg mol−1
0.1 mol of acetic acid= 0 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 6.0 g (W2) [{tex}\because{/tex} Molar mass of CH3COOH = 60 g mol-1]
M2 = {tex}\frac{K_{f} \times W_{2} \times 1000}{\Delta T_{f} \times W_{1}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{512 \mathrm{K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \times 6.0 \mathrm{g} \times 1000}{0.256 \mathrm{K} \times 1000 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
= 120 g mol-1
i = {tex}\frac{M_{2} \text { (Calculated) }}{M_{2}(\text { Observed })}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{60}{120}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0.5
The value of i is 0.5. It is less than 1. Hence, the solute, i e. CH3COOH gets dimense in benzene
i = 05.Here 1 <1,therefore the solute, acetic acid is associated in benzene.

subject: Chemistry
question_html: Calculate the depression in the freezing point of water when 10 g of CH3CH2CHClCOOH is added to 250 g of water. Ka = 1.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-3 , Kf = 1.86 K Kg mol-1.
question_text: Calculate the depression in the freezing point of water when 10 g of CH 3 CH 2 CHClCOOH is added to 250 g of water. K a = 1.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -3 , K f = 1.86 K Kg mol -1 .
explanation: Molar mass of CH 3 CH 2 CHCl COOH {tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2\;=(4\times12)\;+(7\times1)+(2\times16)\;+(1\;\times35.5)\;=122.5g/mol}{/tex} Number of moles of CH 3 CH 2 CHCl COOH {tex} = \frac{{10}}{{122.5}}{/tex} = 8.16 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -2 mole {tex}\therefore{/tex} Molality of the solution (m) {tex}(m) = \frac{{8.16 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}mol}}{{250}} \times 1000\,g\,K{g^{ - 1}}{/tex} = 0.3264m If x is the degree of dissociation of CH 3 CH 2 CHCl COOH then {tex}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}}\limits_{n\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}} }\limits_{n(x - 1)} CHClCOOH \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}}\limits_0 }\limits_{nx} CHCl\mathop {\mathop {CO{O^ - } + }\limits_{{H^ + }} }\limits_{nx}{/tex} To calculate Van't Hoff factor Total moles of particles = n[1 - x + x + x] = n [1 + x] {tex}i = \frac{{n(1 + x)}}{n} = 1 + x{/tex} We know that {tex}x = \sqrt {\frac{{{K_a}}}{x}}{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {\frac{{1.4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.3264}}}{/tex} = 0.065 i = 1 + 0.065 = 1.065 {tex}\Delta {T_f} = i{K_f}m{/tex} = 1.065 × 1.86 × 0.3264 = 0.65°
solution: Molar mass of CH 3 CH 2 CHCl COOH {tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2\;=(4\times12)\;+(7\times1)+(2\times16)\;+(1\;\times35.5)\;=122.5g/mol}{/tex} Number of moles of CH 3 CH 2 CHCl COOH {tex} = \frac{{10}}{{122.5}}{/tex} = 8.16 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -2 mole {tex}\therefore{/tex} Molality of the solution (m) {tex}(m) = \frac{{8.16 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}mol}}{{250}} \times 1000\,g\,K{g^{ - 1}}{/tex} = 0.3264m If x is the degree of dissociation of CH 3 CH 2 CHCl COOH then {tex}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}}\limits_{n\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}} }\limits_{n(x - 1)} CHClCOOH \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}}\limits_0 }\limits_{nx} CHCl\mathop {\mathop {CO{O^ - } + }\limits_{{H^ + }} }\limits_{nx}{/tex} To calculate Van't Hoff factor Total moles of particles = n[1 - x + x + x] = n [1 + x] {tex}i = \frac{{n(1 + x)}}{n} = 1 + x{/tex} We know that {tex}x = \sqrt {\frac{{{K_a}}}{x}}{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {\frac{{1.4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.3264}}}{/tex} = 0.065 i = 1 + 0.065 = 1.065 {tex}\Delta {T_f} = i{K_f}m{/tex} = 1.065 × 1.86 × 0.3264 = 0.65°
solution_html:
Molar mass of CH3CH2CHCl COOH
 {tex}\style{font-family:'Times New Roman'}{M_2\;=(4\times12)\;+(7\times1)+(2\times16)\;+(1\;\times35.5)\;=122.5g/mol}{/tex}
Number of moles of
CH3CH2CHCl COOH {tex} = \frac{{10}}{{122.5}}{/tex}
= 8.16 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-2 mole
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Molality of the solution (m)
{tex}(m) = \frac{{8.16 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}mol}}{{250}} \times 1000\,g\,K{g^{ - 1}}{/tex}
= 0.3264m
If x is the degree of dissociation of CH3CH2CHCl COOH then
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}}\limits_{n\,mol\,{L^{ - 1}}} }\limits_{n(x - 1)} CHClCOOH \rightleftharpoons \mathop {\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}}\limits_0 }\limits_{nx} CHCl\mathop {\mathop {CO{O^ - } + }\limits_{{H^ + }} }\limits_{nx}{/tex}
To calculate Van't Hoff factor
Total moles of particles
= n[1 - x + x + x]
= n [1 + x]
{tex}i = \frac{{n(1 + x)}}{n} = 1 + x{/tex}
We know that
{tex}x = \sqrt {\frac{{{K_a}}}{x}}{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt {\frac{{1.4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.3264}}}{/tex} = 0.065
i = 1 + 0.065 = 1.065
{tex}\Delta {T_f} = i{K_f}m{/tex}
= 1.065 × 1.86 × 0.3264 = 0.65°

subject: Chemistry
question_html:
Define the following terms:

  1. Mole fraction
  2. Molarity
question_text: Define the following terms: Mole fraction Molarity
explanation: Mole fraction: It is the ratio of number of moles of one component to the total number of moles present in the solution. If n 1 and n 2 be the no. of moles of solute and solvent, then mole fraction of solute x 1 . {tex}{x_1} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_1} + {n_2}}}{/tex} Molarity: It is number of moles of solute dissolved per litre of the solution. It is denoted by M. {tex}{\text{Molarity}} = \frac{{{\text{Moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Volume of solution in litre}}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{n}{V} \times 1000{/tex} unit is mol/L
solution: Mole fraction: It is the ratio of number of moles of one component to the total number of moles present in the solution. If n 1 and n 2 be the no. of moles of solute and solvent, then mole fraction of solute x 1 . {tex}{x_1} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_1} + {n_2}}}{/tex} Molarity: It is number of moles of solute dissolved per litre of the solution. It is denoted by M. {tex}{\text{Molarity}} = \frac{{{\text{Moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Volume of solution in litre}}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{n}{V} \times 1000{/tex} unit is mol/L
solution_html:
1. Mole fraction: It is the ratio of number of moles of one component to the total number of moles present in the solution.
    If n1 and n2 be the no. of moles of solute and solvent, then mole fraction of solute x1.
    {tex}{x_1} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_1} + {n_2}}}{/tex}
  2. Molarity: It is number of moles of solute dissolved per litre of the solution. It is denoted by M.
    {tex}{\text{Molarity}} = \frac{{{\text{Moles of solute}}}}{{{\text{Volume of solution in litre}}}}{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac{n}{V} \times 1000{/tex} unit is mol/L
